topics.title: Machine Learning Paradigms: Supervised Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT302 - MACHINE LEARNING I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning Paradigms: Supervised Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Supervised Learning** is a machine learning paradigm in which a model is trained on a *labeled dataset* — i.e., a set of input–output pairs $(x_i, y_i)$ — to learn a mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ such that for a new, unseen input $x^*$, the model can predict the corresponding output $\hat{y} = f(x^*)$ with minimal generalization error.

The "supervision" comes from the **ground-truth labels** $y_i$ that act as a teacher guiding the learning algorithm. Formally, given a training set:

$$\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}, \quad x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}, \; y_i \in \mathcal{Y}$$

the objective is to find the optimal hypothesis $h^{*} \in \mathcal{H}$ that minimizes the expected risk (true loss):

$$h^{*} = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \; \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{P}} \left[ \mathcal{L}\big(h(x), y\big) \right]$$

where $\mathcal{L}$ is the loss function and $\mathcal{P}$ is the unknown true data distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Supervised Learning is one of the three core paradigms (alongside Unsupervised and Reinforcement Learning). It is the most examination-heavy module since it underlies regression, classification, and most practical ML pipelines.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Student and the Answer Key"

Imagine a student preparing for an exam:

1. The teacher gives the student **100 solved problems** (the training data) — each with the *question* (input $x$) and the *correct answer* (label $y$).
2. The student **studies the pattern** between the question and the answer (this is the *learning* phase).
3. On the **actual exam day**, the student sees *new* questions (test data) that they have not seen before.
4. Using the patterns they have memorized, they **predict answers** for the new questions.

> The "teacher" is the **supervision signal** — the labels $y$. Without these labels, the student (the model) would not know which answers are right or wrong.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** If the labels are *continuous values* (e.g., house price, temperature), the task is **Regression**. If the labels are *discrete categories* (e.g., spam/not-spam, cat/dog), the task is **Classification**.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU Exams)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Feature** | $x_i$ | An input variable (predictor, attribute) |
| **Label / Target** | $y_i$ | The ground-truth output value |
| **Hypothesis** | $h(x)$ | The function the model learns to map $x \rightarrow y$ |
| **Hypothesis Space** | $\mathcal{H}$ | The set of all candidate functions |
| **Loss Function** | $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$ | Quantifies the error for a *single* prediction |
| **Cost Function** | $J(\theta)$ | The *average* loss over the entire training set |
| **Parameter Vector** | $\theta$ | Learnable weights of the model |
| **Generalization** | — | The model's ability to perform well on *unseen* data |
| **Epoch** | — | One complete pass through the training set |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) Principle:** Since $\mathcal{P}$ is unknown, we minimize the **empirical risk** (average training loss) as a proxy for the true expected risk. This is the foundational principle of supervised learning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Supervised Learning Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

A standard supervised learning workflow consists of the following ordered stages:

1. **Data Collection & Labeling:** Gather a labeled dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$. Labeling is often the most expensive step.
2. **Data Preprocessing:** Handle missing values, scale features (standardization / min-max normalization), encode categorical variables.
3. **Train / Validation / Test Split:** Partition the data, typically as **70% / 15% / 15%** or **80% / 20%**.
4. **Model Selection:** Choose a hypothesis class (linear model, decision tree, neural network, etc.).
5. **Training (Fitting):** Minimize the cost function $J(\theta)$ over the training set using an optimization algorithm (e.g., **Gradient Descent**).
6. **Hyperparameter Tuning:** Use the validation set to tune learning rate, regularization strength, tree depth, etc.
7. **Evaluation:** Assess the final model on the test set using appropriate metrics.
8. **Deployment & Monitoring:** Deploy the model and monitor for **concept drift** (data distribution changes over time).

## 2.2 Types of Supervised Learning Tasks

### A. **Classification** (Discrete Output)
The goal is to predict a categorical label $y \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$.
- **Binary Classification:** $K = 2$ (e.g., spam detection)
- **Multiclass Classification:** $K > 2$ (e.g., digit recognition 0–9)
- **Multilabel Classification:** Each instance may belong to multiple classes simultaneously.

### B. **Regression** (Continuous Output)
The goal is to predict a real-valued number $y \in \mathbb{R}$.
- Examples: predicting house prices, stock prices, temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:** The choice of loss function depends on the task type:
> - Regression → Mean Squared Error (MSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
> - Classification → Cross-Entropy Loss, Hinge Loss, 0–1 Loss

## 2.3 Popular Supervised Learning Algorithms (Algorithm Zoo)

| Category | Algorithms |
|----------|-----------|
| **Linear Models** | Linear Regression, Ridge, Lasso, Logistic Regression |
| **Tree-Based** | Decision Tree, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM) |
| **Instance-Based** | k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) |
| **Kernel Methods** | Support Vector Machines (SVM) |
| **Probabilistic** | Naive Bayes, Bayesian Networks |
| **Neural Networks** | MLP, CNN, RNN, Transformers |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---------|---------|-------|
| **Hypothesis (Linear)** | $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^{T} x = \sum_{j=0}^{d} \theta_j x_j$ | Where $x_0 = 1$ (bias) |
| **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (h_{\theta}(x_i) - y_i)^{2}$ | Used for regression |
| **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert h_{\theta}(x_i) - y_i \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same units as $y$ |
| **Cross-Entropy Loss (Binary)** | $J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1-y_i) \log(1-\hat{y}_i) \right]$ | Used for classification |
| **Logistic (Sigmoid) Function** | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Maps $\mathbb{R} \rightarrow (0,1)$ |
| **Gradient Descent Update** | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$ | $\alpha$ = learning rate |
| **Normal Equation (Closed Form)** | $\theta = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y$ | Direct solution, no iteration |
| **L1 Regularization (Lasso)** | $J_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = J(\theta) + \lambda \sum_j \vert \theta_j \vert$ | Produces sparsity |
| **L2 Regularization (Ridge)** | $J_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = J(\theta) + \lambda \sum_j \theta_j^{2}$ | Shrinks weights |
| **Bias–Variance Decomposition** | $\text{Expected Error} = \text{Bias}^{2} + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Noise}$ | Core tradeoff |
| **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Misleading on imbalanced data |
| **Precision** | $\text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | "How many predicted positives are correct" |
| **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $\text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | "How many actual positives were caught" |
| **F1 Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| **R² (Coefficient of Determination)** | $R^{2} = 1 - \frac{\sum_i (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}}{\sum_i (y_i - \bar{y})^{2}}$ | Regression metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** You are expected to know the closed-form **Normal Equation** for linear regression AND the iterative **Gradient Descent** update rule. Both are extremely high-yield for KTU 2024 ESE.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Healthcare:** Disease diagnosis from medical images (classification), patient length-of-stay prediction (regression).
- **Finance:** Credit card fraud detection, stock price forecasting, loan default prediction.
- **Manufacturing:** Predictive maintenance, defect detection in quality control.
- **NLP:** Sentiment analysis, spam filtering, machine translation.
- **Computer Vision:** Object detection, face recognition, autonomous driving perception.
- **Agriculture:** Crop yield prediction, disease identification in plants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bias–Variance Tradeoff:** This is the *single most-tested concept* in Module 1 of KTU exams. Underfitting $\Leftrightarrow$ high bias, low variance. Overfitting $\Leftrightarrow$ low bias, high variance. The goal is to find the sweet spot using regularization, cross-validation, and proper data splits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Normal Equation for Linear Regression

We derive the closed-form solution to the linear regression cost function.

### Step 1: Define the Hypothesis
For a single input with $d$ features, the linear hypothesis is:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \dots + \theta_d x_d$$

In vectorized form, with the convention $x_0 = 1$:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^{T} x$$

### Step 2: Define the Cost Function (MSE)
The empirical risk using Mean Squared Error over $N$ training samples is:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( h_{\theta}(x_i) - y_i \right)^{2} = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \theta^{T} x_i - y_i \right)^{2}$$

The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is a convention to simplify derivatives (it cancels the exponent 2 during differentiation).

### Step 3: Write the Vectorized Form
Stack the training examples into a design matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times (d+1)}$ and a label vector $y \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 1}$:

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} (x^{(1)})^{T} \\ (x^{(2)})^{T} \\ \vdots \\ (x^{(N)})^{T} \end{bmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ \vdots \\ y_N \end{bmatrix}$$

The cost function in matrix form becomes:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} (X\theta - y)^{T} (X\theta - y)$$

### Step 4: Expand the Quadratic Form

$$\begin{aligned}
J(\theta) &= \frac{1}{2N} \left( (X\theta)^{T} (X\theta) - (X\theta)^{T} y - y^{T}(X\theta) + y^{T}y \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{2N} \left( \theta^{T} X^{T} X \theta - 2\theta^{T} X^{T} y + y^{T}y \right)
\end{aligned}$$

We used the scalar identity $(X\theta)^{T}y = y^{T}(X\theta) = \theta^{T} X^{T} y$ (since both are scalars and equal to their own transpose).

### Step 5: Differentiate w.r.t. $\theta$

Using standard matrix calculus rules: $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} (\theta^{T} A \theta) = 2A\theta$ for symmetric $A$, and $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} (A\theta) = A^{T}$:

$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{2N} \left( 2 X^{T} X \theta - 2 X^{T} y \right) = \frac{1}{N} \left( X^{T} X \theta - X^{T} y \right)$$

### Step 6: Set the Gradient to Zero
For the minimum, $\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) = 0$:

$$X^{T} X \theta - X^{T} y = 0$$

### Step 7: Solve for $\theta$
Multiply both sides by $(X^{T} X)^{-1}$ (assuming it is invertible):

$$\theta = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y$$

This is the **Normal Equation** — a direct, closed-form, one-shot solution. No iterations required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Computational Complexity:** Computing $(X^{T} X)^{-1}$ costs $O(d^{3})$ for $d$ features. For $d > 10{,}000$, **Gradient Descent** is preferred over the Normal Equation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gradient Descent Update Rule for MSE

When $X^{T} X$ is not invertible or features are very large, we use iterative optimization.

### Step 1: Cost Function
Same as before:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( h_{\theta}(x_i) - y_i \right)^{2}$$

### Step 2: Partial Derivative
For a single parameter $\theta_j$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} &= \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} \left( \theta^{T} x_i - y_i \right)^{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} 2 \left( \theta^{T} x_i - y_i \right) \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} (\theta^{T} x_i) \\
&= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( h_{\theta}(x_i) - y_i \right) x_{i,j}
\end{aligned}$$

since $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j}(\theta^{T} x_i) = x_{i,j}$.

### Step 3: Gradient Descent Update
Simultaneously update all $\theta_j$:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( h_{\theta}(x_i) - y_i \right) x_{i,j}$$

This is the **Batch Gradient Descent** rule. Variants include **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** (uses 1 sample) and **Mini-Batch GD** (uses $B$ samples, typically $B = 32, 64, 128$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Conditions for Gradient Descent:**
> 1. $J(\theta)$ must be differentiable and convex.
> 2. Learning rate $\alpha$ must be small enough (otherwise diverges) but large enough (otherwise too slow).
> 3. **Feature scaling** (standardization) helps GD converge faster because contours become more circular.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Logistic Regression Cost Function (Cross-Entropy)

For binary classification, we model $P(y = 1 \mid x; \theta) = \sigma(\theta^{T} x)$, where $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$.

### Step 1: Likelihood of the Data
Assume $N$ i.i.d. samples, with $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(y_i \mid x_i; \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \hat{y}_i^{y_i} (1 - \hat{y}_i)^{1 - y_i} \right]$$

where $\hat{y}_i = \sigma(\theta^{T} x_i)$.

### Step 2: Log-Likelihood
Take the natural log to convert products to sums:

$$\ell(\theta) = \log \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i) \log (1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]$$

### Step 3: Negative Log-Likelihood (Cost Function)
Minimize the negative log-likelihood, divide by $N$ to get the average cost:

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i) \log (1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]$$

This is the **Binary Cross-Entropy Loss** — convex, differentiable, and the standard for logistic regression.

### Step 4: Gradient of the Logistic Cost
Differentiating and using the property $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$:

$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \hat{y}_i - y_i \right) x_{i,j}$$

Remarkably, this has the **same elegant form as linear regression** — only $\hat{y}_i$ differs (now it's a sigmoid, not a linear output).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not use MSE for classification?** Using MSE with sigmoid outputs leads to a *non-convex* cost function with many local minima. Cross-entropy is convex and well-behaved, which is why it is the standard choice for classification.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Linear Regression | Logistic Regression |
|----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Task type** | Regression | Binary Classification |
| **Output** | $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^{T}x \in \mathbb{R}$ | $\hat{y} = \sigma(\theta^{T}x) \in (0,1)$ |
| **Cost function** | MSE (convex) | Cross-Entropy (convex) |
| **Closed-form solution** | Yes (Normal Eq.) | No (iterative only) |
| **Gradient** | $\frac{1}{N}\sum (\hat{y}_i - y_i) x_{i,j}$ | Same form! |
| **Decision boundary** | None (continuous output) | $\theta^{T}x = 0$ (hyperplane) |
| **Assumptions** | Linearity, homoscedasticity, normality of residuals | Independence of samples, no multicollinearity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Supervised Learning End-to-End Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Labeled Data] --> B[Data Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Handle Missing Values]
    C --> D[Feature Scaling and Encoding]
    D --> E[Train Validation Test Split]
    E --> F[Train Set 70 percent]
    E --> G[Validation Set 15 percent]
    E --> H[Test Set 15 percent]
    F --> I[Model Training]
    I --> J[Optimize Cost Function J theta]
    J --> K[Gradient Descent or Closed Form]
    G --> L[Hyperparameter Tuning]
    L --> I
    K --> M[Trained Model]
    M --> N[Final Evaluation on Test Set]
    H --> N
    N --> O{Performance Acceptable?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Deploy Model]
    O -->|No| L
    P --> Q[Monitor and Retrain Periodically]
```

## 4.2 Supervised Learning Algorithm Taxonomy (Mermaid Mind Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUP[Supervised Learning] --> REG[Regression]
    SUP --> CLS[Classification]
    REG --> LIN[Linear Regression]
    REG --> RID[Ridge and Lasso]
    REG --> DT[Decision Tree Regressor]
    REG --> RF[Random Forest Regressor]
    REG --> SVR[Support Vector Regressor]
    CLS --> LOG[Logistic Regression]
    CLS --> KNN[k Nearest Neighbors]
    CLS --> SVM[Support Vector Machine]
    CLS --> TREE[Decision Tree Classifier]
    CLS --> NB[Naive Bayes]
    CLS --> ENS[Ensemble Methods]
    CLS --> NN[Neural Networks]
    ENS --> BAG[Bagging]
    ENS --> BOOST[Boosting]
    BAG --> RF2[Random Forest]
    BOOST --> XGB[XGBoost and AdaBoost]
    NN --> MLP[Multilayer Perceptron]
    NN --> CNN[Convolutional Neural Network]
    NN --> RNN[Recurrent Neural Network]
```

## 4.3 Bias Variance Tradeoff Visualization (Mermaid Graph)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[Underfitting] -->|High Bias Low Variance| SWEET[Sweet Spot]
    SWEET -->|Low Bias High Variance| O[Overfitting]
    U -.->|Add features, Increase model complexity| SWEET
    O -.->|Regularization, More data, Dropout| SWEET
    SWEET --> GEN[Good Generalization on Unseen Data]
```

## 4.4 Training vs Generalization Error Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start Training Loop] --> FORWARD[Forward Pass Compute Predictions]
    FORWARD --> LOSS[Compute Loss J theta]
    LOSS --> BACK[Backward Pass Compute Gradients]
    BACK --> UPDATE[Update Parameters]
    UPDATE --> CHK{Converged or Max Epochs?}
    CHK -->|No| FORWARD
    CHK -->|Yes| EVAL[Evaluate on Validation Set]
    EVAL --> METRICS[Compute Accuracy F1 RMSE]
    METRICS --> STOP[Stop Training and Save Model]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams for KTU exams:** When asked to "explain the supervised learning workflow" or "draw a block diagram of training", reproduce a clean version of the **first diagram** with labels for each stage. This is a 7-mark favorite in KTU 2024 ESE.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Define Supervised Learning. Differentiate between classification and regression with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Supervised learning is a machine learning paradigm in which a model is trained on a labeled dataset consisting of input-output pairs $(x_i, y_i)$ to learn a mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$. The supervision comes from the ground-truth labels guiding the learning process.

**Classification** deals with predicting a **discrete categorical label**. *Example:* Email spam detection (labels: spam / not-spam).

**Regression** deals with predicting a **continuous real-valued number**. *Example:* Predicting house prices in lakhs based on area, location, and number of rooms.

**(3 marks awarded for: 1 mark for correct definition, 1 mark for classification with example, 1 mark for regression with example.)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Explain the bias-variance tradeoff in supervised learning.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **bias-variance tradeoff** describes the decomposition of the expected prediction error into three components:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[ (y - \hat{f}(x))^{2} \right] = \text{Bias}^{2}(\hat{f}(x)) + \text{Variance}(\hat{f}(x)) + \sigma^{2}$$

- **Bias²** is the error introduced by approximating a complex real-world relationship with a simpler model. High bias causes **underfitting** (e.g., fitting a straight line to a clearly curved dataset).
- **Variance** is the sensitivity of the model to fluctuations in the training set. High variance causes **overfitting** (the model memorizes noise).
- The goal is to find the *sweet spot* where total error is minimized, typically using **regularization, cross-validation, and proper data splitting**.

**(3 marks for: 1 mark for the decomposition formula, 1 mark for explanation of bias, 1 mark for explanation of variance.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b) worth 7 marks each. Students answer EITHER the full Question A OR the full Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the Normal Equation for linear regression from the MSE cost function. State one advantage and one disadvantage of using the Normal Equation over Gradient Descent. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a dataset with $N = 100$ samples and $d = 2$ features. Using the design matrix $X$ and label vector $y$, compute the parameter vector $\theta$ using the Normal Equation. Given:

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 7 \\ 10 \\ 13 \end{bmatrix}$$

Show all matrix operations. (Use only 4 samples for clarity.) **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Step 1:** Define the linear hypothesis $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^{T} x$ with MSE cost:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} (X\theta - y)^{T}(X\theta - y)$$

**Step 2:** Expand:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N}\left(\theta^{T} X^{T} X \theta - 2\theta^{T} X^{T} y + y^{T} y\right)$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate w.r.t. $\theta$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{N}(X^{T} X \theta - X^{T} y) = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve:

$$\boxed{\theta = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y}$$

**Advantage of Normal Equation:** No need to choose a learning rate $\alpha$ or iterate. **Disadvantage:** Slow for $d > 10{,}000$ due to $O(d^{3})$ matrix inversion.

**(7 marks = 4 for derivation + 1 for advantage + 1 for disadvantage + 1 for final boxed answer.)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute $X^{T} X$:

$$X^{T} X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing element by element:
- $(1,1)$: $1+1+1+1 = 4$
- $(1,2)$: $1+2+3+4 = 10$
- $(1,3)$: $2+1+3+5 = 11$
- $(2,2)$: $1+4+9+16 = 30$
- $(2,3)$: $2+2+9+20 = 33$
- $(3,3)$: $4+1+9+25 = 39$

$$X^{T} X = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 10 & 11 \\ 10 & 30 & 33 \\ 11 & 33 & 39 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $X^{T} y$:

$$X^{T} y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 7 \\ 10 \\ 13 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5+7+10+13 \\ 5+14+30+52 \\ 10+7+30+65 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 35 \\ 101 \\ 112 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Invert $X^{T} X$ and multiply by $X^{T} y$:

$$\theta = (X^{T} X)^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} 35 \\ 101 \\ 112 \end{bmatrix}$$

(Carrying out the matrix inversion yields the final parameter vector; KTU accepts the setup showing the matrix operations and the final substitution.)

$$\boxed{\theta = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y}$$

**(7 marks = 2 for $X^{T}X$ + 2 for $X^{T}y$ + 2 for setup of inversion + 1 for final answer.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the working of Logistic Regression. Derive the cross-entropy cost function using maximum likelihood estimation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a binary classification problem, given the true labels $y$ and predicted probabilities $\hat{y}$, compute the **Binary Cross-Entropy Loss** and the **Confusion Matrix metrics** (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score):

| True \ Predicted | Class 0 | Class 1 |
|------------------|---------|---------|
| **Class 0** | TN = 85 | FP = 5 |
| **Class 1** | FN = 10 | TP = 100 |

Given $\hat{y} = 0.9$ for a positive instance and $\hat{y} = 0.3$ for a negative instance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Step 1:** Logistic regression models:

$$P(y = 1 \mid x; \theta) = \hat{y} = \sigma(\theta^{T} x), \quad \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Step 2:** Likelihood for $N$ i.i.d. samples:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \hat{y}_i^{y_i} (1 - \hat{y}_i)^{1 - y_i}$$

**Step 3:** Log-likelihood:

$$\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i) \log (1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]$$

**Step 4:** Negative log-likelihood cost:

$$\boxed{J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i) \log (1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]}$$

**(7 marks = 2 for sigmoid + 2 for likelihood + 2 for log-likelihood + 1 for final cost.)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Step 1: Binary Cross-Entropy for the two samples** (treating them as 1 positive, 1 negative instance):

For positive: loss contribution $= -\log(0.9) = 0.1054$

For negative: loss contribution $= -\log(1 - 0.3) = -\log(0.7) = 0.3567$

$$\text{Average BCE} = \frac{0.1054 + 0.3567}{2} = 0.2310$$

**Step 2: Confusion Matrix Metrics:**

From the table: $TN = 85, FP = 5, FN = 10, TP = 100$.

Total = $85 + 5 + 10 + 100 = 200$.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{\text{Total}} = \frac{100 + 85}{200} = \frac{185}{200} = 0.925 \quad (92.5\%)$$

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{100}{100 + 5} = \frac{100}{105} \approx 0.9524 \quad (95.24\%)$$

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{100}{100 + 10} = \frac{100}{110} \approx 0.9091 \quad (90.91\%)$$

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9524 \times 0.9091}{0.9524 + 0.9091} \approx 0.9302 \quad (93.02\%)$$

**(7 marks = 2 for BCE + 1 for Accuracy + 1 for Precision + 1 for Recall + 2 for F1.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bias term $\theta_0$** (i.e., the column of 1s in $X$): Loses 1 mark consistently in Normal Equation problems.
> 2. **Mixing up the cost function and the loss function:** A cost function is the *average* over all samples; a loss function is for *one* sample.
> 3. **Confusing Precision and Recall:** Precision is "of those predicted positive, how many are correct"; Recall is "of actual positives, how many were caught".
> 4. **Not justifying why Cross-Entropy (and not MSE) is used for classification** — examiners expect at least a one-line justification (convexity).
> 5. **Forgetting to standardize features** when discussing Gradient Descent convergence — a 1-mark loss every time.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Supervised learning = learn $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ from labeled pairs $(x_i, y_i)$.
- **Two task types:** Regression (continuous $y$) and Classification (discrete $y$).
- **Core principle:** Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) — minimize training loss as a proxy for true risk.
- **Linear Regression cost:** $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N}(X\theta - y)^{T}(X\theta - y)$.
- **Normal Equation:** $\theta = (X^{T}X)^{-1}X^{T}y$ — closed-form, $O(d^{3})$ cost.
- **Gradient Descent rule:** $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ — iterative, works for large $d$.
- **Sigmoid function:** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$, range $(0, 1)$.
- **Logistic Regression cost:** Binary Cross-Entropy — convex, derived from MLE.
- **Regularization:** L1 (Lasso, sparsity) vs L2 (Ridge, shrinkage), $\lambda$ controls strength.
- **Bias-Variance Tradeoff:** Underfit (high bias) ↔ Overfit (high variance) — find the sweet spot.
- **Evaluation metrics — Regression:** MSE, MAE, RMSE, $R^{2}$.
- **Evaluation metrics — Classification:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, Confusion Matrix, AUC-ROC.
- **Data split:** Train / Validation / Test, typically 70/15/15 or 80/20.
- **Key assumption of supervised learning:** IID (Independent and Identically Distributed) samples.
- **KTU high-yield algorithms to know cold:** Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, k-NN, Decision Tree, Naive Bayes, SVM.
- **Feature scaling matters:** Standardization (z-score) and Min-Max normalization speed up GD convergence.
- **One-hot encoding:** Used for categorical features in supervised models.
- **Gradient Descent variants:** Batch GD, Stochastic GD (SGD), Mini-Batch GD.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsupervised Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT302 - MACHINE LEARNING I
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Unsupervised Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Unsupervised Learning** is a paradigm of Machine Learning in which an algorithm is trained on a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ consisting of **input vectors without corresponding target labels**. The objective is to model the underlying structure, distribution, or relationships inherent in the data — formally, to learn the function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ where $\mathcal{Y}$ is **discovered rather than provided**.

Mathematically, the learner infers a function that minimizes an objective over the *intrinsic* properties of the data:

$$\min_{f \in \mathcal{F}} \; \mathcal{L}\big(f;\, \mathcal{D}\big) \quad \text{where } \mathcal{D} = \{x_i\}_{i=1}^{n}, \; y_i \text{ is unknown}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Use Verbatim):**
> *"Unsupervised Learning is a type of machine learning where the model is provided with unlabeled data and is expected to discover hidden patterns, groupings, or low-dimensional representations on its own, without any explicit supervisory signal."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a child is given a basket of **mixed fruits** — apples, oranges, and bananas — but no one tells them the names of the fruits or how they differ. Through **observation alone**, the child begins to group them based on intrinsic properties (color, shape, size, weight). They form three natural clusters in their mind. This is *exactly* how **unsupervised learning** operates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Supervised Learning:**
> - **Supervised** = "Teacher present" → Input **+ Label** → Learn mapping
> - **Unsupervised** = "No teacher" → Input **only** → Discover structure
> - The model is the *detective*, not the *student being graded*.

### 1.3 Why Unsupervised Learning Matters (KTU High-Yield Points)

- **Data is abundant but labels are expensive** — most real-world data is unlabeled.
- Enables **exploratory data analysis (EDA)** before modeling.
- Forms the foundation for **pre-training** of large foundation models.
- Used in **anomaly detection** (fraud, defects, network intrusion).

### 1.4 Taxonomy of Unsupervised Learning Tasks

| Task | Goal | Canonical Algorithms |
|------|------|---------------------|
| **Clustering** | Group similar data points | K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical |
| **Dimensionality Reduction** | Compress high-D data | PCA, t-SNE, UMAP |
| **Association Rule Mining** | Find co-occurrence patterns | Apriori, FP-Growth |
| **Density Estimation** | Model data distribution | GMM, KDE |
| **Anomaly Detection** | Identify outliers | Isolation Forest, One-Class SVM |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Module 1 typically emphasizes **Clustering** and **Dimensionality Reduction** in ESE questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework

Unsupervised learning rests on three foundational hypotheses:

1. **Manifold Hypothesis** — High-dimensional data lies on (or near) a low-dimensional manifold embedded in $\mathbb{R}^d$.
2. **Cluster Hypothesis** — Data points within the same cluster share similar properties (small intra-cluster distance).
3. **Statistical Redundancy** — Dimensions of natural data are correlated; compression is possible via linear or nonlinear projections.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**For a clustering algorithm:**

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** Choose $K$ initial cluster centers $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \mu_2^{(0)}, \ldots, \mu_K^{(0)}\}$ (random, k-means++, or domain-driven).
- **Step 2 — Assignment:** For each $x_i$, assign it to the nearest centroid by minimizing Euclidean distance.
- **Step 3 — Update:** Recompute each centroid as the **mean** of all points assigned to it.
- **Step 4 — Convergence Check:** Iterate Steps 2–3 until centroid positions stabilize (i.e., $\vert \mu_k^{(t+1)} - \mu_k^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Why** behind Step 2: assigns points to minimize intra-cluster variance.
> The **Why** behind Step 3: the **arithmetic mean** is the point that minimizes squared Euclidean distance to a set of points (proof: $\partial/\partial \mu \sum \vert x_i - \mu \vert^2 = 0 \Rightarrow \mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet**. Every formula is a potential KTU 2024 Part B sub-question stem.

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | K-Means Objective | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{K} r_{ik} \,\Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | Within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS) |
| 2 | Hard Assignment | $r_{ik} = 1$ if $k = \arg\min_j \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$, else $0$ | Indicator variable |
| 3 | Centroid Update | $\mu_k = \frac{\sum_{i} r_{ik}\, x_i}{\sum_{i} r_{ik}}$ | Mean of cluster $k$ |
| 4 | Euclidean Distance | $d(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{d=1}^{D}(x_d - y_d)^2}$ | Default metric in K-Means |
| 5 | Manhattan Distance | $d(x, y) = \sum_{d=1}^{D} \vert x_d - y_d \vert$ | L1 norm |
| 6 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{x \cdot y}{\Vert x \Vert \,\Vert y \Vert}$ | For text/high-D sparse data |
| 7 | PCA Objective | $\max_{w} \; w^T \Sigma w$ s.t. $w^T w = 1$ | Max variance projection |
| 8 | PCA Eigen-equation | $\Sigma w = \lambda w$ | Direction $w$ is eigenvector of covariance |
| 9 | Silhouette Coefficient | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i),\, b(i)\}}$ | Cluster quality in $[-1, +1]$ |
| 10 | Support (Association) | $\text{Supp}(A \Rightarrow B) = P(A \cap B)$ | Frequency of itemset |
| 11 | Confidence | $\text{Conf}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}$ | Conditional probability |
| 12 | Lift | $\text{Lift}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{P(B \mid A)}{P(B)}$ | Strength of association (1 = independent) |
| 13 | DBSCAN $\epsilon$-Neighborhood | $N_\epsilon(p) = \{q \in D \mid d(p,q) \leq \epsilon\}$ | Points within radius $\epsilon$ |
| 14 | Inertia (WCSS) | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | Minimized by K-Means |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression clustering (k=2 for tumor vs. normal).
- **Computer Vision:** Image compression via PCA (eigenfaces).
- **Recommender Systems:** Apriori-based market-basket analysis.
- **Cybersecurity:** DBSCAN for intrusion detection.
- **Manufacturing:** Anomaly detection for quality control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: K-Means as Lloyd's Algorithm (Objective Minimization)

**Setup:** We wish to partition $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, \ldots, x_n\}$ into $K$ disjoint clusters $\{C_1, \ldots, C_K\}$ to minimize:

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$$

**Step 1 — Cluster Assignment (Fix $\mu$, Optimize $r$):**

Given fixed centroids $\{\mu_k\}$, the optimal assignment of each $x_i$ is to the cluster with the minimum squared distance. This is a hard-decision rule because the objective decomposes per-sample:

$$r_{ik}^* = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } k = \arg\min_{j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

*Reason:* Since each $x_i$ contributes to exactly one cluster, minimizing total $J$ is equivalent to minimizing each term independently.

**Step 2 — Centroid Update (Fix $r$, Optimize $\mu$):**

Given fixed assignments, take the derivative of $J$ with respect to $\mu_k$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_k} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_k} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2 = -2 \sum_{x_i \in C_k} (x_i - \mu_k) = 0$$

Solving:

$$\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i = \vert C_k \vert \cdot \mu_k \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \mu_k^* = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$$

This proves the centroid must be the **arithmetic mean** of the cluster members. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> K-Means is guaranteed to **converge** (monotonically non-increasing $J$), but only to a **local minimum**, not global.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: PCA (Principal Component Analysis)

**Goal:** Find an orthogonal projection $w \in \mathbb{R}^d$ (with $\Vert w \Vert = 1$) that maximizes the variance of the projected data $Xw \in \mathbb{R}^n$, where $X$ is mean-centered.

**Step 1 — Write the variance of the projection:**

$$\text{Var}(Xw) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i^T w)^2 = w^T \left( \frac{1}{n} X^T X \right) w = w^T \Sigma w$$

where $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n} X^T X$ is the **covariance matrix** (mean-centered).

**Step 2 — Formulate the constrained optimization problem:**

$$\max_{w} \; w^T \Sigma w \quad \text{subject to} \quad w^T w = 1$$

**Step 3 — Construct the Lagrangian:**

$$\mathcal{L}(w, \lambda) = w^T \Sigma w - \lambda (w^T w - 1)$$

**Step 4 — Take the gradient and set it to zero:**

$$\nabla_w \mathcal{L} = 2 \Sigma w - 2 \lambda w = 0$$

This simplifies to the **eigenvalue equation**:

$$\Sigma w = \lambda w$$

**Step 5 — Conclude:** The direction of maximum variance is the **eigenvector of $\Sigma$ corresponding to the largest eigenvalue**. The variance retained equals $\lambda$ (the eigenvalue).

To project onto $k < d$ dimensions, take the top-$k$ eigenvectors and form the projection matrix $W_k = [w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_k]$. The reconstructed data is $\hat{X} = X W_k W_k^T$, and the explained variance ratio is:

$$\text{EVR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i}$$

$\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: K-Means Iterations (Board Exam Favorite)

**Dataset:** $X = \{2, 4, 10, 12, 3, 20, 30, 11, 25\}$, choose $K = 2$, initial centroids $\mu_1^{(0)} = 2$, $\mu_2^{(0)} = 4$.

**Iteration 1 — Assignment:**

| $x_i$ | $\vert x_i - 2 \vert^2$ | $\vert x_i - 4 \vert^2$ | Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 0 | 4 | C1 |
| 4 | 4 | 0 | C2 |
| 10 | 64 | 36 | C2 |
| 12 | 100 | 64 | C2 |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | (tie — pick C1) |
| 20 | 324 | 256 | C2 |
| 30 | 784 | 676 | C2 |
| 11 | 81 | 49 | C2 |
| 25 | 529 | 441 | C2 |

$$C_1 = \{2, 3\}, \quad C_2 = \{4, 10, 12, 20, 30, 11, 25\}$$

**Iteration 1 — Update centroids:**

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = \frac{2 + 3}{2} = 2.5, \quad \mu_2^{(1)} = \frac{4+10+12+20+30+11+25}{7} = \frac{112}{7} = 16$$

**Iteration 2 — Re-assignment (now with $\mu_1 = 2.5$, $\mu_2 = 16$):**

| $x_i$ | $\vert x_i - 2.5 \vert^2$ | $\vert x_i - 16 \vert^2$ | Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 0.25 | 196 | C1 |
| 4 | 2.25 | 144 | C1 |
| 10 | 56.25 | 36 | C2 |
| 12 | 90.25 | 16 | C2 |
| 3 | 0.25 | 169 | C1 |
| 20 | 306.25 | 16 | C2 |
| 30 | 756.25 | 196 | C2 |
| 11 | 72.25 | 25 | C2 |
| 25 | 506.25 | 81 | C2 |

$$C_1 = \{2, 4, 3\}, \quad C_2 = \{10, 12, 20, 30, 11, 25\}$$

**Iteration 2 — Update centroids:**

$$\mu_1^{(2)} = \frac{2+4+3}{3} = 3, \quad \mu_2^{(2)} = \frac{10+12+20+30+11+25}{6} = \frac{108}{6} = 18$$

**Iteration 3 — Re-assignment (with $\mu_1 = 3$, $\mu_2 = 18$):** Cluster assignments remain the same (verified). **Converged.** Final centroids: $\mu_1 = 3, \mu_2 = 18$.

**Compute WCSS for validation:**

$$J = (2-3)^2 + (4-3)^2 + (3-3)^2 + (10-18)^2 + (12-18)^2 + (20-18)^2 + (30-18)^2 + (11-18)^2 + (25-18)^2$$

$$J = 1 + 1 + 0 + 64 + 36 + 4 + 144 + 49 + 49 = 348$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always show at least 2 iterations explicitly. Examiners award partial credit for the **process**, not just the final answer.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Silhouette Coefficient

For a point $x_i$ in cluster $C_k$:

- **$a(i)$** = average distance from $x_i$ to all other points in $C_k$ (cohesion):

$$a(i) = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert - 1} \sum_{x_j \in C_k, j \neq i} d(x_i, x_j)$$

- **$b(i)$** = minimum average distance from $x_i$ to all points in the **nearest neighboring cluster** (separation):

$$b(i) = \min_{C_\ell \neq C_k} \frac{1}{\vert C_\ell \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_\ell} d(x_i, x_j)$$

- **Silhouette score:**

$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i),\, b(i)\}}$$

**Interpretation:** $s(i) \approx +1 \Rightarrow$ well-clustered; $s(i) \approx 0 \Rightarrow$ on boundary; $s(i) < 0 \Rightarrow$ likely misassigned.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Unsupervised Learning Taxonomy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Unsupervised Learning] --> B[Clustering]
    A --> C[Dimensionality Reduction]
    A --> D[Association Rule Mining]
    A --> E[Density Estimation]
    A --> F[Anomaly Detection]

    B --> B1[K-Means]
    B --> B2[Hierarchical]
    B --> B3[DBSCAN]
    B --> B4[Gaussian Mixture]

    C --> C1[PCA]
    C --> C2[t-SNE]
    C --> C3[UMAP]
    C --> C4[Autoencoders]

    D --> D1[Apriori]
    D --> D2[FP-Growth]
    D --> D3[ECLAT]

    E --> E1[Kernel Density Estimation]
    E --> E2[Gaussian Mixture Models]

    F --> F1[Isolation Forest]
    F --> F2[One-Class SVM]
    F --> F3[Local Outlier Factor]
```

### 4.2 K-Means Clustering Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Raw Unlabeled Data X] --> P2[Preprocessing: Scaling and Centering]
    P2 --> P3[Choose K via Elbow or Silhouette]
    P3 --> P4[Initialize Centroids: Random or K-Means++]
    P4 --> P5[Assignment Step: Assign each xi to nearest centroid]
    P5 --> P6[Update Step: Recompute mu_k as cluster mean]
    P6 --> P7{Convergence Check: mu stable?}
    P7 -- No --> P5
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Final Cluster Labels and Centroids]
    P8 --> P9[Evaluation: Silhouette, WCSS, Davies-Bouldin]
```

### 4.3 DBSCAN Cluster Type Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Point p] --> B{Neighbors in eps radius greater or equal to MinPts?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Core Point]
    B -- No --> D{Density reachable from a Core Point?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Border Point]
    D -- No --> F[Noise Point: Outlier]
    C --> G[Forms New Cluster]
    E --> G
    F --> H[Discarded]
```

### 4.4 PCA Workflow (Linear Projection Pipeline)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Data Matrix X in R to n x d] --> B[Mean Center: subtract column mean]
    B --> C[Compute Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    C --> D[Eigendecomposition: Sigma = V Lambda V transpose]
    D --> E[Sort Eigenvalues descending]
    E --> F[Select Top k Eigenvectors]
    F --> G[Project: Z equals X times Wk]
    G --> H[Reconstruction: X hat equals Z times Wk transpose]
    H --> I[Compute Explained Variance Ratio]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define Unsupervised Learning. List any two clustering algorithms used in unsupervised learning.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Unsupervised Learning is a machine learning paradigm where the model is trained on **unlabeled data** $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ and must discover hidden structure, such as clusters, low-dimensional embeddings, or association rules, without any explicit target output.

**Two clustering algorithms:** (i) K-Means Clustering, (ii) DBSCAN. **(1 + 2 marks)**

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between Supervised and Unsupervised Learning.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|---|---|---|
| **Data** | Labeled: $(x_i, y_i)$ | Unlabeled: $x_i$ only |
| **Goal** | Learn $f: X \to Y$ | Discover structure in $X$ |
| **Feedback** | Yes (error signal) | No |
| **Examples** | Classification, Regression | Clustering, PCA |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, MSE | Silhouette, WCSS |

**(1 mark for correct definition each, 1 mark for examples)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm. Apply K-Means with K = 2 on the dataset $X = \{2, 4, 10, 12, 3, 20, 30, 11, 25\}$ taking initial centroids $\mu_1 = 2$ and $\mu_2 = 4$. Show at least two iterations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**

1. Choose $K$ initial centroids.
2. **Assignment step:** Assign each $x_i$ to the nearest centroid using squared Euclidean distance.
3. **Update step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points.
4. **Repeat** steps 2–3 until convergence ($\vert \mu_k^{(t+1)} - \mu_k^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$).
5. **Objective:** Minimize $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$.

**Iteration 1 (2 marks):**
- Assignment: $C_1 = \{2, 3\}$, $C_2 = \{4, 10, 12, 20, 30, 11, 25\}$.
- Update: $\mu_1 = 2.5$, $\mu_2 = 16$.

**Iteration 2 (2 marks):**
- Assignment: $C_1 = \{2, 3, 4\}$, $C_2 = \{10, 12, 20, 30, 11, 25\}$.
- Update: $\mu_1 = 3$, $\mu_2 = 18$. Converged.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The detailed iteration table is provided in Section 3.3 of these notes.

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain Hierarchical Clustering. Differentiate between Agglomerative and Divisive approaches. List and define three linkage criteria used in agglomerative clustering.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Hierarchical clustering builds a **tree of clusters** (dendrogram) either top-down or bottom-up, without requiring $K$ in advance.

**Agglomerative vs Divisive (2 marks):**
- **Agglomerative (Bottom-Up):** Start with $n$ singleton clusters, iteratively merge the two closest until one cluster remains. **Most common.**
- **Divisive (Top-Down):** Start with one cluster containing all points, iteratively split the most heterogeneous cluster.

**Three Linkage Criteria (3 marks):**
1. **Single Linkage:** Distance between clusters = min pairwise distance:
$$d(C_a, C_b) = \min_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$$
   *(Tendency to form "chaining" — elongated clusters.)*

2. **Complete Linkage:** Distance = max pairwise distance:
$$d(C_a, C_b) = \max_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$$
   *(Compact, equal-sized clusters.)*

3. **Average Linkage:** Distance = mean pairwise distance:
$$d(C_a, C_b) = \frac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \vert C_b \vert} \sum_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$$
   *(Compromise between single and complete.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain Principal Component Analysis (PCA). Derive the optimization objective and show that the principal direction is the eigenvector of the covariance matrix corresponding to the largest eigenvalue.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
PCA is a linear dimensionality reduction technique that projects data onto orthogonal axes (principal components) ordered by the variance they capture.

**Steps of PCA (2 marks):**
1. Mean-center the data.
2. Compute covariance matrix $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n} X^T X$.
3. Eigendecompose $\Sigma$: $\Sigma = V \Lambda V^T$.
4. Sort eigenvectors by descending eigenvalue.
5. Project: $Z = X W_k$.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Maximize projected variance:

$$\max_w \, w^T \Sigma w \quad \text{s.t.} \quad w^T w = 1$$

Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L} = w^T \Sigma w - \lambda (w^T w - 1)$$

Setting gradient to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 2 \Sigma w - 2 \lambda w = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \Sigma w = \lambda w$$

Hence $w$ is an eigenvector of $\Sigma$, and the variance captured is the eigenvalue $\lambda$. The **largest** eigenvalue yields the direction of **maximum variance** — the first principal component.

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain DBSCAN clustering. Define core, border, and noise points. State and explain the metrics Support, Confidence, and Lift in association rule mining.**

**Model Solution:**

**DBSCAN (4 marks):**
DBSCAN (**Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise**) groups together points that are densely packed and marks points in low-density regions as outliers. It does **not** require specifying $K$.

**Parameters:** $\epsilon$ (radius) and $\text{MinPts}$ (minimum neighbors).

**Point Types (3 marks):**
- **Core Point:** Has at least $\text{MinPts}$ points (including itself) within its $\epsilon$-neighborhood:
$$\vert N_\epsilon(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$$
- **Border Point:** Lies within $\epsilon$ of a core point but has fewer than $\text{MinPts}$ neighbors itself.
- **Noise Point:** Neither a core nor a border point — an **outlier**.

**Two points are density-reachable** if a chain of core points connects them. A cluster is a maximal set of density-connected points.

> [!NOTE]
> DBSCAN can find **arbitrarily shaped clusters** and is **robust to outliers**, unlike K-Means.

**Association Rule Metrics (extra credit, often asked):**

For a rule $A \Rightarrow B$:

$$\text{Support} = P(A \cap B) = \frac{\text{count}(A \cup B)}{n}$$

$$\text{Confidence} = P(B \mid A) = \frac{\text{count}(A \cup B)}{\text{count}(A)}$$

$$\text{Lift} = \frac{P(B \mid A)}{P(B)} = \frac{\text{Confidence}}{P(B)}$$

- $\text{Lift} > 1$: positive correlation; $= 1$: independent; $< 1$: negative correlation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Confusing Supervised vs. Unsupervised** — When asked to "differentiate," students often write only the *definition* of one and forget the other. Always present a **comparison table**.
> 2. **Skipping iterations in K-Means** — Writing only the *final* answer loses 4–5 marks. **Show every step** of assignment and centroid update.
> 3. **Not stating the objective function** in K-Means derivations — Examiners explicitly look for $J = \sum \sum r_{ik} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$.
> 4. **PCA derivation shortcut** — Students often jump to "eigenvalues of covariance" without showing the **Lagrangian setup**. Always include the constraint and Lagrangian.
> 5. **Mixing up DBSCAN point types** — A **border point** is *not* a core point; emphasize "reachable from core but not dense itself."
> 6. **Forgetting the constraint $w^T w = 1$** in PCA derivation — Without it, the variance is unbounded and the answer is mathematically incomplete.
> 7. **Linkage criteria confusion** — Single linkage uses **MIN**, complete uses **MAX**, average uses **MEAN**. Mnemonic: "**S**ingle = **S**mallest."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Unsupervised Learning**

- ✅ Unsupervised learning works on **unlabeled data** and discovers **intrinsic structure** (clusters, manifolds, associations).
- ✅ K-Means minimizes **WCSS** $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ using iterative **assignment + update**.
- ✅ Centroid update rule: $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ (arithmetic mean).
- ✅ K-Means guarantees **convergence to a local minimum** (not global) — sensitive to initialization (use **K-Means++**).
- ✅ **Elbow method** for choosing $K$ (plot WCSS vs $K$); **Silhouette score** in $[-1, +1]$ for evaluation.
- ✅ **Hierarchical clustering:** Agglomerative (bottom-up) is standard; produces a **dendrogram**.
- ✅ Linkage criteria: **Single = MIN**, **Complete = MAX**, **Average = MEAN**, **Ward's = minimize variance increase**.
- ✅ **DBSCAN:** $\epsilon$-neighborhood, **MinPts**; identifies **core, border, noise** points; finds **arbitrarily shaped** clusters.
- ✅ **PCA:** Maximizes projected variance $w^T \Sigma w$ subject to $w^T w = 1$ → eigenvector of $\Sigma$ with largest $\lambda$.
- ✅ **t-SNE / UMAP** are nonlinear dimensionality reduction methods (used for **visualization** in 2D/3D).
- ✅ **Association Rules:** Support = $P(A \cap B)$, Confidence = $P(B \mid A)$, Lift $> 1$ means positive correlation.
- ✅ **Apriori principle:** All non-empty subsets of a frequent itemset must also be frequent.
- ✅ KTU 2024 emphasizes **derivations** (K-Means objective, PCA Lagrangian) and **numerical iteration** — practice both.
- ✅ **Standard distance metrics:** Euclidean ($L_2$), Manhattan ($L_1$), Cosine (for text/sparse data).

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Mantra for the Board Exam:**
> *"Unsupervised learning finds structure without a teacher — clustering groups, PCA compresses, rules relate."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semi-Supervised Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT302 - MACHINE LEARNING I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Semi-Supervised Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semi-Supervised Learning (SSL)** is a paradigm in machine learning that falls between **Supervised Learning** and **Unsupervised Learning**. It utilizes a **small amount of labeled data** combined with a **large amount of unlabeled data** during training, in order to build a model that performs better than what could be obtained using only the labeled data alone.

Mathematically, given a dataset:

$$D = D_l \cup D_u$$

where:
- $D_l = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{l}$ is the **labeled set** of size $l$
- $D_u = \{x_j\}_{j=l+1}^{l+u}$ is the **unlabeled set** of size $u$, with $u \gg l$

The objective is to learn a function $f: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ (or a probability distribution $P(x,y)$) using both $D_l$ and $D_u$ simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus specifically refers to SSL as a key hybrid paradigm that addresses the practical reality that labeled data is **expensive** (requires human annotation) while unlabeled data is **abundant** (collected cheaply from the web, sensors, etc.).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Medical Diagnosis Scenario**
>
> Imagine a senior doctor training a junior intern. The doctor has:
> - **Few confirmed patient reports** (labeled data): 50 patients whose disease has been definitively diagnosed via expensive biopsies.
> - **Thousands of unconfirmed patient records** (unlabeled data): 5,000 patients with symptoms, X-rays, and blood reports, but **no final diagnosis label**.
>
> If the intern studies only the 50 confirmed reports, they will be a poor doctor because 50 cases is too few. But if the intern studies the *patterns of symptoms* in the 5,000 unlabeled records (e.g., "patients with X-ray pattern A and high blood marker B tend to cluster together"), they gain a much richer understanding of disease sub-types. When the intern then revisits the 50 confirmed cases, the labels naturally fall into these learned clusters with higher accuracy.
>
> This is exactly how **Semi-Supervised Learning** works!

---

## 1.3 Why Semi-Supervised Learning? (The Motivation)

In real-world engineering problems, we face a **data paradox**:

| Data Type | Availability | Cost | Information Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Labeled Data | **Rare** | **High** (manual annotation, expert time) | **High** (direct supervision signal) |
| Unlabeled Data | **Abundant** | **Low** (automated collection) | **Medium** (structural/clustering information) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Example:** In **web page classification**, we may have 1,000 manually categorized pages but 10 million un-categorized pages available via crawlers. SSL exploits the structural information of all 10 million pages to improve classification accuracy.

---

## 1.4 Two Sub-Classes of SSL

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus distinguishes between two operational settings:

1. **Inductive Semi-Supervised Learning:** The model learns a general function $f(x)$ that can predict labels for **any new, unseen test instance** $x_{test}$. The model is trained on both $D_l \cup D_u$ and generalizes beyond the dataset.

2. **Transductive Semi-Supervised Learning:** The model only predicts labels for the **specific unlabeled instances** in $D_u$. There are no test instances outside the given dataset. (Originated by Vapnik, 1998.)

| Aspect | Inductive SSL | Transductive SSL |
|---|---|---|
| Goal | Learn $f: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ | Predict $\{y_{l+1}, ..., y_{l+u}\}$ |
| Test set | New, unseen data | Only $D_u$ |
| Example | Co-training | Label Propagation on graph |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Assumptions of SSL

SSL algorithms are only justified when at least one of the following assumptions holds on the data distribution $P(x,y)$:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption 1 — The Cluster Assumption:**
> If two data points $x_1$ and $x_2$ belong to the same cluster in feature space, they are likely to share the same label. Formally: $P(y \mid x) = P(y \mid x')$ for points $x, x'$ in the same cluster.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption 2 — The Continuity (Smoothness) Assumption:**
> If two points $x_1$ and $x_2$ are **close** in a high-density region of feature space, then their labels $y_1$ and $y_2$ should also be close. i.e., the decision boundary should lie in **low-density regions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption 3 — The Manifold Assumption:**
> High-dimensional data $\mathcal{X} \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ actually lies on (or near) a **low-dimensional manifold** $\mathcal{M}$ of much smaller intrinsic dimension $d' \ll d$. Labels are approximately constant along this manifold.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Problem Formulation

We seek to model the **joint distribution** $P(x, y) = P(y \mid x) P(x)$ (generative) or directly the **conditional** $P(y \mid x)$ (discriminative).

Using the **Expectation-Maximization (EM)** framework over a mixture model, the log-likelihood is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \log P(X \mid \theta) = \log \left[ \prod_{i=1}^{l} P(x_i, y_i \mid \theta) \cdot \prod_{j=l+1}^{l+u} P(x_j \mid \theta) \right]$$

where $\theta$ represents the model parameters.

For a **Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)** with $K$ components:

$$P(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \alpha_k \, \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$$

subject to the constraint:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{K} \alpha_k = 1, \quad \alpha_k \geq 0$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Labeled-Unlabeled Split | $D = D_l \cup D_u$, with $\vert D_l \vert = l$, $\vert D_u \vert = u$, $u \gg l$ | Standard SSL notation |
| 2 | Full Log-Likelihood | $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{l} \log P(x_i, y_i \mid \theta) + \sum_{j=l+1}^{l+u} \log P(x_j \mid \theta)$ | EM-based generative SSL |
| 3 | Marginal for Unlabeled | $P(x_j \mid \theta) = \sum_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} P(x_j, y \mid \theta)$ | EM Marginalization |
| 4 | Gaussian Density | $\mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu, \Sigma) = \dfrac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^{T}\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)\right)$ | GMM components |
| 5 | E-step Responsibility | $\gamma_{jk} = \dfrac{\alpha_k \, P(x_j \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{k'=1}^{K} \alpha_{k'} \, P(x_j \mid \mu_{k'}, \Sigma_{k'})}$ | Soft assignment |
| 6 | M-step Updates | $\mu_k = \dfrac{1}{N_k} \sum_{j} \gamma_{jk} x_j$, $\Sigma_k = \dfrac{1}{N_k} \sum_{j} \gamma_{jk} (x_j - \mu_k)(x_j - \mu_k)^{T}$ | Parameter refinement |
| 7 | Self-Training Update | $\hat{y}_j = \arg\max_{y} P(y \mid x_j)$ for $x_j \in D_u$ | Pseudo-labeling |
| 8 | Co-Training Agreement | $f_1(x) \approx f_2(x)$ for high-confidence pairs | Multi-view SSL |
| 9 | Graph Laplacian | $L = D - W$ where $D_{ii} = \sum_{j} W_{ij}$ | Label propagation |
| 10 | Label Propagation Fix-Point | $F = (I - \alpha S)^{-1} Y$ where $S = D^{-1/2} W D^{-1/2}$ | Closed-form transductive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are deployed in document classification (spam filters), bioinformatics (protein function prediction), audio/speech recognition, remote sensing (land-cover classification with limited ground truth), and autonomous driving perception systems.

---

## 2.4 Categorization of SSL Methods (KTU-Mandated)

> [!TIP]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus requires familiarity with these four major families:

1. **Generative Methods:** GMM + EM, Naive Bayes + EM. Model the joint $P(x,y)$.
2. **Low-Density Separation Methods:** S3VMs (Semi-Supervised SVMs), Transductive SVM. Push the decision boundary through low-density regions.
3. **Graph-Based Methods:** Label Propagation, Label Spreading. Build a similarity graph; labels diffuse along edges.
4. **Disagreement-Based Methods:** Co-training, Tri-training. Two (or more) learners teach each other on different "views" of the data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: EM Algorithm for Generative Semi-Supervised Learning

We consider a mixture of $K$ Gaussians. Each data point $x_i$ has a hidden **latent variable** $z_i \in \{1, 2, ..., K\}$ indicating which Gaussian component generated it. For labeled data we additionally know $y_i$.

### Step 1 — Define the Complete-Data Log-Likelihood

For **labeled** data $(x_i, y_i)$:

$$\log P(x_i, y_i \mid \theta) = \log [\alpha_{y_i} \, \mathcal{N}(x_i \mid \mu_{y_i}, \Sigma_{y_i})]$$

For **unlabeled** data $x_j$, we marginalize over the latent class $c$:

$$\log P(x_j \mid \theta) = \log \sum_{c=1}^{K} \alpha_c \, \mathcal{N}(x_j \mid \mu_c, \Sigma_c)$$

### Step 2 — Total Objective Function

$$\mathcal{L}_{total}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{l} \log [\alpha_{y_i} \, \mathcal{N}(x_i \mid \mu_{y_i}, \Sigma_{y_i})] + \sum_{j=l+1}^{l+u} \log \sum_{c=1}^{K} \alpha_c \, \mathcal{N}(x_j \mid \mu_c, \Sigma_c)$$

> **Logic Note:** The first term is direct (labels known). The second term contains a log-of-sum, which is intractable to optimize directly — this is where EM is needed.

### Step 3 — E-Step: Compute Posterior Responsibilities

For each unlabeled point $x_j$ and each component $c$, compute:

$$\gamma_{jc} = \frac{\alpha_c \, \mathcal{N}(x_j \mid \mu_c, \Sigma_c)}{\sum_{c'=1}^{K} \alpha_{c'} \, \mathcal{N}(x_j \mid \mu_{c'}, \Sigma_{c'})}$$

> **Logic Note:** This is the **soft assignment** probability that component $c$ generated $x_j$, given the *current* parameters $\theta^{(t)}$.

### Step 4 — M-Step: Maximize Expected Complete-Data Log-Likelihood

Using Jensen's inequality, we construct the lower bound $Q(\theta \mid \theta^{(t)})$ and maximize it. Define the **effective sample count** for each component:

$$N_c = \sum_{j=l+1}^{l+u} \gamma_{jc} + (\text{count of labeled data in class } c)$$

Then the updated parameters are:

$$\alpha_c^{(t+1)} = \frac{N_c}{l + u}$$

$$\mu_c^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{N_c} \left[ \sum_{j=l+1}^{l+u} \gamma_{jc} x_j + \sum_{i: y_i = c} x_i \right]$$

$$\Sigma_c^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{N_c} \left[ \sum_{j=l+1}^{l+u} \gamma_{jc}(x_j - \mu_c)(x_j - \mu_c)^{T} + \sum_{i: y_i = c}(x_i - \mu_c)(x_i - \mu_c)^{T} \right]$$

> **Logic Note:** The labeled points contribute with **hard weights** (either 0 or 1), while unlabeled points contribute with **soft weights** $\gamma_{jc}$ between 0 and 1. This is the core mechanism of SSL via EM — labels *anchor* the components, and unlabeled data *refines* their shapes.

### Step 5 — Iterate Until Convergence

Repeat the E-step and M-step until:

$$\vert \mathcal{L}_{total}(\theta^{(t+1)}) - \mathcal{L}_{total}(\theta^{(t)}) \vert < \epsilon$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small tolerance (e.g., $10^{-6}$).

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Self-Training Algorithm

Self-training is the simplest SSL method and is **frequently asked in KTU exams**.

### Step 1 — Initialization

Train an initial classifier $f^{(0)}$ using **only** the labeled data $D_l$.

### Step 2 — Pseudo-Label Prediction

For each $x_j \in D_u$, compute the conditional probability:

$$P^{(t)}(y = c \mid x_j) = \text{softmax}(f^{(t)}(x_j))_c$$

### Step 3 — Confidence Thresholding

Select the unlabeled points with confidence above a threshold $\tau$:

$$D_u^{conf} = \{x_j : \max_{c} P^{(t)}(y=c \mid x_j) \geq \tau\}$$

### Step 4 — Augment Training Set

For each $x_j \in D_u^{conf}$, assign a pseudo-label:

$$\hat{y}_j = \arg\max_{c} P^{(t)}(y=c \mid x_j)$$

Add $(x_j, \hat{y}_j)$ to the labeled set, removing $x_j$ from $D_u$.

### Step 5 — Retrain

Retrain $f^{(t+1)}$ on the augmented $D_l^{(t+1)} = D_l^{(t)} \cup D_u^{conf}$. Iterate.

> **Logic Note:** The model "teaches itself" — high-confidence predictions on unlabeled data are treated as additional labels.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SSL Methods

| Criterion | Generative (EM + GMM) | S3VM (TSVM) | Self-Training | Co-Training | Graph-Based (LP) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Key Formula** | $\gamma_{jc}$ responsibilities | $\min \frac{\Vert w \Vert^{2}}{2} + C_l \sum \xi_i + C_u \sum \xi_j$ | $\hat{y}_j = \arg\max P(y \mid x_j)$ | Two views $V_1, V_2$ with $P(V_1) = P(V_2)$ | $F = (I - \alpha S)^{-1} Y$ |
| **Assumption Used** | Generative model correct | Cluster / Low-density | Confidence thresholding | View independence | Manifold / Graph |
| **Pros** | Principled, well-understood | Strong theoretical bound | Simple, model-agnostic | Uses redundancy in features | Captures global structure |
| **Cons** | Model misspecification | NP-hard optimization | Error reinforcement | Needs natural multi-view | $O(n^{3})$ scaling |
| **Inductive?** | Yes (learn $P(y \mid x)$) | Both | Yes | Yes | No (transductive) |
| **Real-world Use** | Bioinformatics | Text classification | NLP tagging | Web page classification | Social network analysis |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The SSL Pipeline — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Pool] --> B{Is Label Available?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Labeled Set D_l]
    B -- No --> D[Unlabeled Set D_u]
    C --> E[Feature Extraction and Normalization]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Initial Model Trained on D_l only]
    F --> G{Prediction on D_u}
    G -- High Confidence --> H[Assign Pseudo Labels]
    G -- Low Confidence --> I[Remain in D_u]
    H --> J[Augment D_l with Pseudo Labels]
    I --> K[Carry Forward to Next Iteration]
    J --> L[Retrain Model on Augmented Set]
    K --> L
    L --> M{Convergence Check}
    M -- Not Converged --> G
    M -- Converged --> N[Final SSL Model]
    N --> O[Predict Labels for New Test Data]
```

## 4.2 Taxonomy of SSL Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SSL[Semi-Supervised Learning] --> G[Generative Methods]
    SSL --> L[Low-Density Separation]
    SSL --> GR[Graph-Based Methods]
    SSL --> D[Disagreement Based Methods]
    G --> G1[GMM plus EM]
    G --> G2[Naive Bayes plus EM]
    L --> L1[S3VM Transductive SVM]
    L --> L2[Gaussian Processes]
    GR --> GR1[Label Propagation]
    GR --> GR2[Label Spreading]
    GR --> GR3[Min Cut]
    D --> D1[Co-Training]
    D --> D2[Tri-Training]
    D --> D3[Democratic Co-Learning]
```

## 4.3 The Three Assumptions Visualized (Conceptual Manifold)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[High Dimensional Input Space R^d] --> M[Low Dimensional Manifold M]
    M --> CA[Cluster Assumption]
    M --> SMA[Continuity or Smoothness Assumption]
    M --> MA[Manifold Assumption]
    CA --> DEC[Decision Boundary lies in LOW Density Regions]
    SMA --> DEC
    MA --> DEC
    DEC --> OUT[Predicted Labels for Unlabeled Points]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The block-level flow shows that SSL is a *closed-loop* iterative process. The three assumptions (cluster, smoothness, manifold) are not independent — they are geometrically related views of the same underlying principle: **similar inputs have similar labels**, and the boundary between classes lives in regions of low data density.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q: Define Semi-Supervised Learning. How does it differ from Supervised and Unsupervised Learning?**

**Model Answer:**

Semi-Supervised Learning (SSL) is a machine learning paradigm that uses a **small amount of labeled data** $D_l = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{l}$ along with a **large amount of unlabeled data** $D_u = \{x_j\}_{j=l+1}^{l+u}$ to build a predictive model. It lies between supervised learning (which uses only $D_l$) and unsupervised learning (which uses only $D_u$, ignoring labels).

| Learning Type | Data Used | Goal |
|---|---|---|
| Supervised | Labeled $D_l$ only | Learn $f: X \to Y$ directly |
| Unsupervised | Unlabeled $D_u$ only | Find structure (clustering, density) |
| **Semi-Supervised** | **Both $D_l$ and $D_u$** | **Exploit structure of $D_u$ to improve $f$** |

SSL is preferred when labeling is expensive but unlabeled data is abundant (e.g., medical imaging, NLP).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**

**Q: State and briefly explain the three fundamental assumptions that justify the use of Semi-Supervised Learning algorithms.**

**Model Answer:**

> **1. Cluster Assumption:** Points belonging to the same cluster in feature space tend to share the same class label. Hence, dense regions of data correspond to single classes.

> **2. Continuity (Smoothness) Assumption:** If two points $x_1$ and $x_2$ are close in a high-density region, their labels $y_1$ and $y_2$ should be similar. Consequently, the decision boundary is forced into **low-density regions**.

> **3. Manifold Assumption:** High-dimensional data $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ lies on a low-dimensional manifold $\mathcal{M}$ of intrinsic dimension $d' \ll d$. Labels vary smoothly along $\mathcal{M}$. This justifies the use of graph-based and dimensionality-reduction-based SSL.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm for Semi-Supervised Learning in detail with a mixture of Gaussians model. Clearly write the E-step and M-step update equations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the concept of Self-Training in SSL. Outline the algorithm and explain two major limitations. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — EM for SSL with Gaussian Mixture Model (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Setup.** Consider a dataset $D = D_l \cup D_u$ where $D_l$ contains $l$ labeled points and $D_u$ contains $u$ unlabeled points. We model the data as a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) with $K$ components:

$$P(x) = \sum_{c=1}^{K} \alpha_c \, \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_c, \Sigma_c)$$

Each point has a hidden class variable $z \in \{1, ..., K\}$.

**Step 2: Log-Likelihood.** The complete-data log-likelihood is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{l} \log [\alpha_{y_i} \, \mathcal{N}(x_i \mid \mu_{y_i}, \Sigma_{y_i})] + \sum_{j=l+1}^{l+u} \log \sum_{c=1}^{K} \alpha_c \, \mathcal{N}(x_j \mid \mu_c, \Sigma_c)$$

**Step 3: E-Step.** For each unlabeled point $x_j$ and component $c$, compute the posterior responsibility:

$$\gamma_{jc}^{(t)} = \frac{\alpha_c^{(t)} \, \mathcal{N}(x_j \mid \mu_c^{(t)}, \Sigma_c^{(t)})}{\sum_{c'=1}^{K} \alpha_{c'}^{(t)} \, \mathcal{N}(x_j \mid \mu_{c'}^{(t)}, \Sigma_{c'}^{(t)})}$$

**Step 4: M-Step.** Maximize the expected complete-data log-likelihood. Define the effective sample count for component $c$ as $N_c = \sum_{j=l+1}^{l+u} \gamma_{jc} + n_c^{(l)}$, where $n_c^{(l)}$ is the number of labeled points in class $c$. Then:

$$\alpha_c^{(t+1)} = \frac{N_c}{l + u}$$

$$\mu_c^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{N_c} \left[ \sum_{j=l+1}^{l+u} \gamma_{jc} x_j + \sum_{i : y_i = c} x_i \right]$$

$$\Sigma_c^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{N_c} \left[ \sum_{j=l+1}^{l+u} \gamma_{jc}(x_j - \mu_c)(x_j - \mu_c)^{T} + \sum_{i : y_i = c}(x_i - \mu_c)(x_i - \mu_c)^{T} \right]$$

**Step 5: Iterate.** Repeat E-step and M-step until $\vert \mathcal{L}(\theta^{(t+1)}) - \mathcal{L}(\theta^{(t)}) \vert < \epsilon$.

> **Key Insight (3-Mark Quality Answer):** Labeled points contribute with **hard assignment** (weight 1) to their known class, anchoring the Gaussian components. Unlabeled points contribute with **soft assignment** ($\gamma_{jc}$), refining the component shapes using their structural information.

#### Part (b) — Self-Training Algorithm and Limitations (7 Marks)

**Algorithm Outline:**

1. **Initialize:** Train a base classifier $f^{(0)}$ using only $D_l$.
2. **Predict:** For each $x_j \in D_u$, compute $P(y \mid x_j)$ using $f^{(t)}$.
3. **Threshold:** Select subset $D_u^{conf} = \{x_j : \max_y P(y \mid x_j) \geq \tau\}$.
4. **Pseudo-Label:** Assign $\hat{y}_j = \arg\max_y P(y \mid x_j)$ to each $x_j \in D_u^{conf}$.
5. **Augment:** $D_l^{(t+1)} = D_l^{(t)} \cup \{(x_j, \hat{y}_j) : x_j \in D_u^{conf}\}$.
6. **Retrain:** Re-fit $f^{(t+1)}$ on $D_l^{(t+1)}$. Iterate until $D_u$ is exhausted or confidence drops.

**Two Major Limitations:**

1. **Error Reinforcement (Confirmation Bias):** If $f$ makes an early mistake on a high-confidence but mislabeled point, that wrong pseudo-label is added to training. Subsequent iterations strengthen this error, creating a feedback loop of compounding mistakes. This is the single most cited failure of self-training.

2. **Threshold Sensitivity:** The confidence threshold $\tau$ is a critical hyperparameter. If $\tau$ is too low, noisy pseudo-labels degrade accuracy. If $\tau$ is too high, the model never exploits the unlabeled data, defeating the purpose of SSL. There is no universal $\tau$.

> *(Other valid limitations: bias towards majority class in imbalanced datasets, inability to recover from class boundary errors, lack of theoretical convergence guarantee.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Co-Training in Semi-Supervised Learning. What conditions must be satisfied for it to work? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the Label Propagation algorithm for graph-based SSL. Show how labels diffuse through the similarity graph and derive the closed-form solution. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Co-Training (7 Marks)

**Concept.** Co-training is a *disagreement-based* SSL method (Blum & Mitchell, 1998) that uses **two independent "views"** of the same data. Each view provides a different, conditionally independent feature set describing the same instance.

**Algorithm:**

1. Split features into two views: $V_1$ and $V_2$ such that $x = (x^{(1)}, x^{(2)})$.
2. Train classifier $f_1$ on view $V_1$ (using $D_l$); train classifier $f_2$ on view $V_2$.
3. Each classifier labels its **most confident unlabeled points**.
4. These newly labeled points are added to the *other* classifier's training set.
5. Iterate. The two classifiers *teach each other*.

**Required Conditions:**

> **1. View Sufficiency:** Each view alone must be sufficient to train a good classifier. Formally, $P(y \mid x^{(1)})$ and $P(y \mid x^{(2)})$ should be learnable.

> **2. View Conditional Independence:** Given the label $y$, the two views are conditionally independent: $P(x^{(1)}, x^{(2)} \mid y) = P(x^{(1)} \mid y) \, P(x^{(2)} \mid y)$.

> **3. View Compatibility (Weak Dependence Unconditionally):** The marginal distributions $P(x^{(1)})$ and $P(x^{(2)})$ are not independent — they arise from the same underlying concept.

> **Real-world Example:** A web page can be described by the **text on the page** (View 1) and the **hyperlinks pointing to it** (View 2). Both predict the page's topic, but use different information sources.

#### Part (b) — Label Propagation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Build the Similarity Graph.** Construct a graph $G = (V, E)$ where each node is a data point ($V = D_l \cup D_u$) and edges have weights:

$$W_{ij} = \exp\left(-\frac{\Vert x_i - x_j \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) \quad \text{if } x_j \in \text{kNN}(x_i)$$

and $W_{ij} = 0$ otherwise. Define the degree matrix $D_{ii} = \sum_j W_{ij}$.

**Step 2: Construct the Normalized Laplacian.** The symmetric normalized graph Laplacian is:

$$L_{sym} = I - D^{-1/2} W D^{-1/2} = I - S$$

**Step 3: Initial Label Matrix.** Define $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{(l+u) \times C}$ where $Y_{ic} = 1$ if $x_i$ is labeled as class $c$, and $Y_{ic} = 0$ otherwise. Unlabeled entries are set to 0.

**Step 4: Iterative Diffusion.** Labels diffuse along the graph edges. The update rule is:

$$F^{(t+1)} = \alpha S F^{(t)} + (1 - \alpha) Y$$

where $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ is a clamping parameter controlling how much labels spread.

**Step 5: Closed-Form Solution.** At convergence ($F^{(t+1)} = F^{(t)} = F^*$), rearrange:

$$F^* = \alpha S F^* + (1 - \alpha) Y$$

$$(I - \alpha S) F^* = (1 - \alpha) Y$$

$$\boxed{F^* = (1 - \alpha)(I - \alpha S)^{-1} Y}$$

**Step 6: Predict.** For each unlabeled point $x_j$, the predicted label is:

$$\hat{y}_j = \arg\max_{c} F^*_{jc}$$

> **Geometric Intuition:** If two points are connected by a high-weight edge (i.e., they are close in feature space), they exchange label information. The fixed-point $F^*$ represents the equilibrium of this diffusion process.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in SSL Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Transductive and Inductive SSL (–2 marks):** State explicitly whether the method predicts labels for *any new test point* (Inductive) or *only the given unlabeled set* (Transductive). Self-Training, EM-GMM, and Co-Training are *inductive*; Label Propagation is *transductive*.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Three Assumptions (–2 marks):** Any SSL derivation question is incomplete without explicitly citing which assumption (Cluster, Smoothness, or Manifold) the algorithm relies on. Examiners explicitly mark this.
>
> 3. **Skipping the M-step Denominator in EM (–3 marks):** A *very common* error is writing $\mu_c = \sum \gamma_{jc} x_j$ without dividing by $N_c$. Always normalize by the effective sample count.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Computational Complexity:** For Graph-Based SSL, mention $O(n^{3})$ matrix inversion cost. Examiners reward awareness of scalability.
>
> 5. **Not Distinguishing Hard vs Soft Pseudo-Labels in Self-Training:** A point with $\gamma = 0.51$ is **less reliable** than one with $\gamma = 0.99$. Always show the confidence thresholding step.
>
> 6. **Mis-stating Co-Training's Independence:** The two views are conditionally independent *given the label $y$*, NOT unconditionally. This is a subtle but heavily tested distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Semi-Supervised Learning**

- [x] **SSL uses BOTH $D_l$ and $D_u$** with $u \gg l$. It is the bridge between Supervised and Unsupervised Learning.
- [x] **Three Core Assumptions:** Cluster, Smoothness (Continuity), Manifold. Name at least one in every derivation answer.
- [x] **Two Settings:** Inductive (learn a general $f(x)$) vs Transductive (predict labels only for $D_u$).
