$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t+1)}) \leq J(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)}) - \frac{\alpha}{2} \vert\vert \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)}) \vert\vert^2$$

This guarantees that the cost **monotonically decreases** at every iteration, ensuring convergence to the global minimum (for convex problems) or a local minimum (for non-convex problems).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flow Diagram of the Batch Gradient Method

The following block-level architecture diagram illustrates the operational flow of the Batch Gradient Method, mapping the data dependencies and decision gates in the algorithm.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([START ALGORITHM]) --> initParams[Initialize parameters theta0 and theta1 to zero or random small values]
    initParams --> setHyper[Set learning rate alpha and max iterations Tmax]
    setHyper --> iterLoop{Begin Iteration Loop t = 0 to Tmax}
    iterLoop --> loadData[Load ENTIRE training dataset of m samples]
    loadData --> predict[Compute hypothesis h theta of x_i for all i from 1 to m]
    predict --> calcError[Compute prediction errors h theta of x_i minus y_i for all samples]
    calcError --> sumCost[Sum squared errors to compute cost J theta]
    sumCost --> checkConv{J less than epsilon OR gradient norm less than delta}
    checkConv -- YES --> outputParams[Output converged parameters theta0 and theta1]
    checkConv -- NO --> compGrad[Compute partial derivatives dJ by dtheta0 and dJ by dtheta1 over all m samples]
    compGrad --> updateParams[Update parameters simultaneously using theta_j new equals theta_j old minus alpha times dJ by dtheta_j]
    updateParams --> iterLoop
    outputParams --> stopNode([END ALGORITHM])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Cost Surface Traversal

The following schematic illustrates the high-level interaction between the cost surface, the gradient computer, and the parameter updater in the Batch Gradient Method. This serves as a fallback for a physical 3D contour plot.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER[INPUT LAYER]
        dataNode[Full Dataset X of size m by n plus y]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE_LAYER[GRADIENT COMPUTATION LAYER]
        hypNode[Hypothesis h theta of x equals theta transpose x]
        errNode[Error Vector e_i equals h theta of x_i minus y_i]
        costNode[Cost Aggregator J theta equals 1 over 2m sum of e_i squared]
        gradNode[Gradient Calculator nabla J theta equals 1 over m sum of e_i times x_i]
    end

    subgraph UPDATE_LAYER[PARAMETER UPDATE LAYER]
        lrNode[Learning Rate alpha]
        updNode[Parameter Updater theta new equals theta old minus alpha times nabla J theta]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER[OUTPUT LAYER]
        convNode{Convergence Check}
        outNode[Optimal Parameters theta star]
    end

    dataNode --> hypNode
    hypNode --> errNode
    errNode --> costNode
    errNode --> gradNode
    costNode --> convNode
    gradNode --> updNode
    lrNode --> updNode
    updNode --> hypNode
    convNode -- Converged --> outNode
    convNode -- Not Converged --> hypNode
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Learning Rate Effects on Convergence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Cost Function J of theta]) --> branch{Select Learning Rate alpha}

    branch -- Small alpha approx 0.001 --> slowPath[Slow Steady Descent]
    slowPath --> slowNote[Converges reliably but requires many iterations]

    branch -- Optimal alpha approx 0.01 --> optPath[Fast Direct Descent]
    optPath --> optNote[Reaches global minimum efficiently]

    branch -- Large alpha approx 0.1 --> oscPath[Oscillation Around Minimum]
    oscPath --> oscNote[May diverge or fail to converge]

    branch -- Excessive alpha approx 1.0 --> divPath[Explosion and Divergence]
    divPath --> divNote[Cost increases unboundedly]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The Batch Gradient Method's performance is acutely sensitive to the learning rate $\alpha$. The diagram above is a classical KTU examination visual that maps the four characteristic behaviors observed during training.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Batch Gradient Method. Why is it called "batch"? **[3 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

The Batch Gradient Method is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to minimize a differentiable cost function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ by updating parameters in the direction of steepest descent. The update rule is:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t+1)} = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)} - \alpha \cdot \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)})$$

It is called **"batch"** because, at every iteration, the gradient is computed over the **entire batch (full set) of training examples** before performing a single parameter update. The cumulative sum over all $m$ samples ensures deterministic, stable, and accurate gradient estimation at the cost of high per-iteration computational expense.

---

### Question 2: What happens to the Batch Gradient Method if the learning rate $\alpha$ is set too large or too small? **[3 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

- **If $\alpha$ is too small:** The algorithm converges **very slowly**, requiring a large number of iterations to reach the minimum. The trajectory is smooth and stable, but computationally inefficient. There is also a risk of getting stuck in a plateau region.

- **If $\alpha$ is too large:** The algorithm **overshoots the minimum** and may oscillate around the optimal point or diverge entirely, causing the cost function to grow unboundedly. The updates become unstable, and the algorithm fails to converge.

The optimal $\alpha$ balances convergence speed and stability, often determined empirically via a **learning rate schedule** or **grid search**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Batch Gradient Descent for Linear Regression **[14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) Derive the parameter update rule for the Batch Gradient Method when applied to a linear regression model with Mean Squared Error (MSE) cost function. Clearly state the hypothesis, cost function, and gradient equations. Assume $m$ training examples and $n$ features. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hypothesis:** For a linear model with $n$ features, the hypothesis is:

$$h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \ldots + \theta_n x_n = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}$$

**Step 2 — Cost Function (MSE):**

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Partial Derivative w.r.t. $\theta_j$:**

$$\frac{\partial J(\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 4 — Update Rule (Batch Gradient Descent):**

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

This must be applied simultaneously for all $j \in \{0, 1, \ldots, n\}$. The full vectorized form is:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta} := \boldsymbol{\theta} - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \mathbf{X}^T (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})$$

**(b) For the dataset $\{(1,2), (2,4), (3,5)\}$, perform TWO iterations of the Batch Gradient Method with $\alpha = 0.01$, $\theta_0 = \theta_1 = 0$. Show that the cost decreases after each iteration. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Iteration 1:** (Already computed in Section 3.2)

- Gradients: $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = -3.667$, $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = -8.333$
- Updated parameters: $\theta_0^{(1)} = 0.0367$, $\theta_1^{(1)} = 0.0833$
- Cost: $J^{(1)} = 6.694$

**Iteration 2:**

- New predictions: $h(1) = 0.1200$, $h(2) = 0.2033$, $h(3) = 0.2867$
- Errors: $-1.8800$, $-3.7967$, $-4.7133$

Compute gradients:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ -1.8800 - 3.7967 - 4.7133 \right] = \frac{-10.390}{3} = -3.4633$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ (-1.88)(1) + (-3.7967)(2) + (-4.7133)(3) \right] = \frac{-25.813}{3} = -8.6043$$

Updated parameters:

$$\theta_0^{(2)} = 0.0367 - 0.01 \cdot (-3.4633) = 0.0367 + 0.0346 = 0.0713$$

$$\theta_1^{(2)} = 0.0833 - 0.01 \cdot (-8.6043) = 0.0833 + 0.0860 = 0.1693$$

New cost:

$$J^{(2)} = \frac{1}{6} \left[ (-1.88)^2 + (-3.7967)^2 + (-4.7133 + 0.0713 + 0.1693 \cdot 3 - 5)^2 \right]$$

Computing each term:
- $(0.0713 + 0.1693 \cdot 1 - 2)^2 = (-1.7594)^2 = 3.0955$
- $(0.0713 + 0.1693 \cdot 2 - 4)^2 = (-3.5901)^2 = 12.8888$
- $(0.0713 + 0.1693 \cdot 3 - 5)^2 = (-4.4208)^2 = 19.5435$

$$J^{(2)} = \frac{1}{6} (3.0955 + 12.8888 + 19.5435) = \frac{35.5278}{6} \approx 5.9213$$

**Result:** $J^{(2)} = 5.9213 < J^{(1)} = 6.694 < J^{(0)} = 7.500$. The cost **monotonically decreases**, confirming correct descent.

---

### Question B: Convergence and Learning Rate Analysis **[14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain the convergence properties of the Batch Gradient Method. What conditions on the cost function and learning rate guarantee convergence? [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Convergence Properties:**

1. **Convex Cost Functions:** If $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ is **convex** and **continuously differentiable** with a **Lipschitz-continuous gradient** (Lipschitz constant $L$), then BGD with $\alpha \leq \frac{1}{L}$ is guaranteed to converge to the **global minimum**.

2. **Non-Convex Cost Functions:** For non-convex functions (e.g., deep neural network loss surfaces), BGD converges to a **local minimum** or **saddle point**. It cannot escape local minima because it always follows the negative gradient.

3. **Monotonic Decrease:** With appropriate $\alpha$, the cost satisfies:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t+1)}) \leq J(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)})$$

**Conditions for Convergence:**

- **Condition 1 — Learning Rate Bound:** $\alpha \leq \frac{1}{L}$, where $L$ is the Lipschitz constant of $\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$.
- **Condition 2 — Gradient Continuity:** $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ must be differentiable and $\nabla J$ must be continuous.
- **Condition 3 — Bounded Below:** $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \geq c$ for some finite constant $c$ (cost cannot be $-\infty$).
- **Condition 4 — Strict Descent:** $\alpha$ must be small enough that each step strictly reduces the cost.

**Stopping Criteria (Practical Convergence):**

- Cost tolerance: $\vert J^{(t)} - J^{(t-1)} \vert < \varepsilon$ (typically $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$).
- Gradient norm: $\vert\vert \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)}) \vert\vert < \delta$.
- Maximum iterations reached.

**(b) Compare the Batch Gradient Method with the Stochastic Gradient Method. List THREE advantages and THREE disadvantages of the Batch Gradient Method. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Batch Gradient Method | Stochastic Gradient Method |
|---|---|---|
| Data per update | All $m$ samples | 1 sample |
| Computation per update | High (vectorized) | Low |
| Update frequency | Once per epoch | $m$ times per epoch |
| Convergence path | Smooth, direct | Noisy, oscillating |
| Convergence guarantee | Strong (convex case) | Weaker (probabilistic) |
| Memory requirement | High (stores all $m$ samples) | Low (one sample at a time) |

**Three Advantages of Batch Gradient Method:**

1. **Deterministic and Stable Convergence:** Because the gradient is computed over the entire dataset, the trajectory is smooth and reproducible. No sampling noise is introduced, leading to stable convergence.

2. **Theoretical Convergence Guarantees:** Strong mathematical guarantees exist for convex cost functions. The Lipschitz condition provides a clear bound on the learning rate for guaranteed descent.

3. **Efficient on Small Datasets:** When $m$ is small-to-moderate, vectorized matrix operations make BGD computationally efficient and faster than SGD per update.

**Three Disadvantages of Batch Gradient Method:**

1. **High Memory Consumption:** The entire dataset must fit in memory (RAM). For large-scale datasets ($m > 10^6$), this becomes infeasible.

2. **Slow per-Iteration Cost:** Each update requires processing all $m$ samples, making BGD prohibitively slow for large datasets. Only **one update per epoch** is performed.

3. **Cannot Handle Streaming Data:** BGD requires the full dataset upfront. It cannot adapt to data that arrives in real-time streams (e.g., online learning applications).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{m}$ factor:** Many students omit the averaging term in the gradient. The full-batch gradient requires summation over **all $m$ samples divided by $m$**. Loss of this factor leads to incorrect scaling and a 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Non-simultaneous updates:** Updating $\theta_0$ first and then using the new $\theta_0$ in the $\theta_1$ update is a frequent error. **All parameters must be updated simultaneously** using values from iteration $t$, not $t+1$.
> 3. **Confusing Batch GD with Stochastic GD:** Examiners specifically look for the summation $\sum_{i=1}^{m}$ (Batch) versus the use of a single sample $i$ (Stochastic). Mixing these notations is a 3-mark deduction.
> 4. **Missing convergence justification:** Simply stating the update rule is insufficient for full marks. Students must explain the **first-order Taylor series derivation** that justifies the negative gradient direction.
> 5. **Ignoring the learning rate sensitivity:** The KTU examiner expects explicit mention of how $\alpha$ affects convergence (small $\alpha$ = slow, large $\alpha$ = divergence).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Batch Gradient Method:**

- **Definition:** Iterative optimization that uses the **entire dataset** to compute the gradient at every step.
- **Update Rule:** $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$, with gradient computed as $\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$.
- **Cost Function (MSE):** $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$.
- **Learning Rate $\alpha$:** Critical hyperparameter. Too small = slow; too large = divergence.
- **Convergence:** Monotonic decrease guaranteed for convex $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ with $\alpha \leq \frac{1}{L}$.
- **Batch Size:** $m_{batch} = m$ (full dataset) — this is what distinguishes BGD from SGD ($m_{batch} = 1$) and Mini-Batch GD ($1 < m_{batch} < m$).
- **Simultaneous Updates:** All parameters $\theta_j$ updated in parallel using the gradient from the same iteration.
- **Advantages:** Deterministic, stable, theoretically grounded, efficient for small datasets.
- **Disadvantages:** High memory, slow per iteration, unsuitable for streaming data.
- **Stopping Criteria:** $\vert J^{(t)} - J^{(t-1)} \vert < \varepsilon$ OR $\vert\vert \nabla J \vert\vert < \delta$ OR max iterations reached.
- **Mathematical Basis:** Derived from first-order Taylor series approximation; the negative gradient is the direction of steepest local descent.
- **Vectorized Form:** $\boldsymbol{\theta} := \boldsymbol{\theta} - \frac{\alpha}{m} \mathbf{X}^T (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})$.
- **KTU Exam Frequency:** Very high — appears in almost every KTU Machine Learning exam as a Part A (3 marks) and frequently as a Part B (14 marks) question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Illustrative Examples of Parameter Estimation Using Different Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT302 - MACHINE LEARNING I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Illustrative Examples of Parameter Estimation Using Different Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Parameter Estimation?

**Parameter Estimation** is the statistical process of using observed data to infer the values of unknown parameters that govern an underlying probability distribution or model. In Machine Learning, every learning algorithm — from Naive Bayes to Logistic Regression to Neural Networks — implicitly performs some form of parameter estimation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> Given an i.i.d. sample $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ drawn from a distribution $p(x \mid \theta)$ governed by an unknown parameter vector $\theta$, parameter estimation is the procedure of computing a function $\hat{\theta} = \phi(x_1, \ldots, x_n)$ that best approximates $\theta$ according to a chosen optimality criterion.

## 1.2 The Four Major Estimation Paradigms

### 🔹 (a) Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)
**Definition:** MLE selects the parameter value $\hat{\theta}_{MLE}$ that **maximizes the probability of observing the given data** $\mathcal{D}$.

$$\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \arg\max_{\theta} \ p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):** Imagine you are a detective at a crime scene. You find 5 fingerprints. You have a list of 100 suspects. MLE says: *"Pick the suspect whose hand is MOST LIKELY to leave exactly this fingerprint pattern."* It only uses the evidence — no prior opinions.

### 🔹 (b) Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) Estimation
**Definition:** MAP selects the parameter value that **maximizes the posterior probability** $p(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$ using Bayes' rule.

$$\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta} \ p(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \arg\max_{\theta} \ p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \, p(\theta)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Same detective, but now she has a prior reputation database. If suspect A is already known to be a criminal, the detective weighs the fingerprint evidence AND the prior reputation. *"Combine the evidence with prior beliefs, then pick the best overall."*

### 🔹 (c) Bayesian Estimation
**Definition:** Bayesian estimation computes the **entire posterior distribution** $p(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$ and uses a loss function to derive a point estimate (typically the posterior mean under squared-error loss).

$$\hat{\theta}_{Bayes} = E[\theta \mid \mathcal{D}] = \int \theta \, p(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \, d\theta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Instead of naming ONE suspect, the detective presents a **probability distribution** over all suspects — "I am 40% sure it's A, 35% B, 25% others." The final pick is the weighted average of all opinions, not just the maximum.

### 🔹 (d) Method of Moments (MoM)
**Definition:** MoM equates **sample moments** to **theoretical moments** and solves for the parameters algebraically.

$$\overline{x^k} = E[X^k]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Like checking if your class average matches the national average. If not, you adjust your assumption about the population to make them match.

### 🔹 (e) Cramér–Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)
**Definition:** A theoretical **lower bound on the variance of any unbiased estimator**, given by the inverse of the Fisher Information.

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{I(\theta)}$$

where Fisher Information is:

$$I(\theta) = -E\left[ \frac{\partial^2 \ln p(x \mid \theta)}{\partial \theta^2} \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CRLB Matters in KTU:** It tells you the *best possible* accuracy. If your estimator reaches this bound, it is called an **efficient estimator** (the gold standard).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Pipeline of Parameter Estimation

Regardless of the method, every estimator follows a structured pipeline:

1. **Define the probabilistic model** $p(x \mid \theta)$ (the assumed data distribution).
2. **Formulate the objective** function (Likelihood, Posterior, Moments, or Fisher Information).
3. **Optimize or solve** the objective using calculus (derivatives) or algebra (moment equations).
4. **Evaluate the estimator** using metrics such as bias, variance, MSE, and efficiency.

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is your **exam-day cheat sheet**. Memorize these expressions — they appear in nearly every Part B question of Module 1.

| **Method** | **Objective Function** | **Estimator Formula** | **Key Property** |
|------------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| **MLE** | $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i \mid \theta)$ | $\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \arg\max_{\theta} \ln \mathcal{L}(\theta)$ | Asymptotically unbiased, consistent, efficient |
| **MAP** | $p(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto \mathcal{L}(\theta) \, p(\theta)$ | $\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta} \left[ \ln \mathcal{L}(\theta) + \ln p(\theta) \right]$ | Bayesian with point estimate; equals MLE if prior is uniform |
| **Bayesian** | $p(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) p(\theta)}{p(\mathcal{D})}$ | $\hat{\theta}_{Bayes} = E[\theta \mid \mathcal{D}]$ | Optimal under squared-error loss; uses full posterior |
| **MoM** | $E[X^k] = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^k$ | Solve $k$ equations for $k$ unknowns | Simple, often biased for finite $n$ |
| **Fisher Info** | $I(\theta) = -E\!\left[\dfrac{\partial^2 \ln p(x \mid \theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$ | For $n$ iid samples: $I_n(\theta) = n \cdot I(\theta)$ | Quantifies information data carries about $\theta$ |
| **CRLB** | $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \dfrac{1}{I_n(\theta)}$ | Equality $\Leftrightarrow$ efficient estimator | The theoretical lower bound for unbiased variance |
| **Bias** | $\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$ | Zero $\Rightarrow$ unbiased estimator | Quality metric |
| **MSE** | $\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) + \text{Bias}^2(\hat{\theta})$ | Combines both variance and bias | Overall error metric |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Application** |
|------------|------------------|
| Computer Vision | Estimating Gaussian parameters of image patches |
| NLP | Estimating word probabilities in language models |
| Medical Imaging | Estimating tumor distribution parameters from MRI |
| Signal Processing | Estimating noise variance for Kalman filters |
| Finance | Estimating volatility parameters in Black-Scholes models |
| Control Systems | Estimating system parameters in adaptive control |

## 2.4 Important Boundary Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - MLE and MAP **coincide** when the prior $p(\theta)$ is **uniform** (non-informative).
> - For large $n$, MAP $\rightarrow$ MLE because the prior's effect gets washed out.
> - CRLB applies **only to unbiased estimators**. Biased estimators can have lower MSE even with higher variance.
> - Fisher Information is **additive** for i.i.d. samples: $I_n(\theta) = n \cdot I_1(\theta)$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section contains **five worked illustrative examples** — the kind of problems KTU examiners most frequently set in ESE Part B.

---

## 📘 Example 1 — MLE for Gaussian Distribution (Mean & Variance)

**Problem:** Let $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ be i.i.d. samples drawn from a Gaussian distribution $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. Derive the MLE of $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$.

**Step 1 — Write the Likelihood Function**

The probability density of a single Gaussian sample is:

$$p(x_i \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

Since the samples are independent, the joint likelihood is the product:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mu, \sigma^2) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Take the Log-Likelihood (Monotonic Transformation)**

Applying the natural logarithm simplifies products to sums:

$$\ln \mathcal{L} = -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{n}{2}\ln(\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\mu$ and Set to Zero**

$$\frac{\partial \ln \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mu} = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \cdot (-2) \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu) = 0$$

This gives:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = n\mu \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\mu}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = \bar{x}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\sigma^2$ and Set to Zero**

$$\frac{\partial \ln \mathcal{L}}{\partial \sigma^2} = -\frac{n}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2(\sigma^2)^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2 = 0$$

Multiplying through by $2(\sigma^2)^2$:

$$-n\sigma^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2 = 0$$

Substituting the MLE of $\mu$:

$$\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pearl:** This MLE variance is the **biased** estimator (divides by $n$, not $n-1$). The unbiased version is $S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2$. A common trap question!

**Step 5 — Verify via Second Derivative Test**

The second derivative of $\ln \mathcal{L}$ w.r.t. $\mu$ is $-\frac{n}{\sigma^2} < 0$, confirming a **maximum**.

---

## 📘 Example 2 — MLE for Bernoulli Distribution

**Problem:** Given $n$ independent trials where each $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ with success probability $p$, find $\hat{p}_{MLE}$.

**Step 1 — Likelihood Function**

$$\mathcal{L}(p) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1-x_i} = p^{s}(1-p)^{n-s}$$

where $s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ is the total number of successes.

**Step 2 — Log-Likelihood**

$$\ln \mathcal{L} = s \ln p + (n-s)\ln(1-p)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate and Set to Zero**

$$\frac{d \ln \mathcal{L}}{dp} = \frac{s}{p} - \frac{n-s}{1-p} = 0$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$s(1-p) = p(n-s) \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = np$$

Therefore:

$$\hat{p}_{MLE} = \frac{s}{n} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n} = \bar{x}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Illustration**

Suppose we toss a coin 10 times and observe heads 7 times. Then:

$$\hat{p}_{MLE} = \frac{7}{10} = 0.7$$

This is the **observed frequency of success** — the most intuitive estimator.

---

## 📘 Example 3 — MAP Estimation for Gaussian Mean (with Gaussian Prior)

**Problem:** Samples $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ are i.i.d. from $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with **known** $\sigma^2$. The prior on $\mu$ is $N(\mu_0, \sigma_0^2)$. Find $\hat{\mu}_{MAP}$.

**Step 1 — Posterior via Bayes' Rule**

$$p(\mu \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto p(\mathcal{D} \mid \mu) \cdot p(\mu) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} e^{-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_0^2}} e^{-\frac{(\mu - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2}}$$

**Step 2 — Log-Posterior (Drop Constants)**

$$\ln p(\mu \mid \mathcal{D}) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2 - \frac{(\mu - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2} + C$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\mu$ and Set to Zero**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \mu} \ln p(\mu \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu) - \frac{\mu - \mu_0}{\sigma_0^2} = 0$$

$$\frac{n(\bar{x} - \mu)}{\sigma^2} = \frac{\mu - \mu_0}{\sigma_0^2}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$n\sigma_0^2(\bar{x} - \mu) = \sigma^2(\mu - \mu_0)$$

$$n\sigma_0^2 \bar{x} + \sigma^2 \mu_0 = \mu(n\sigma_0^2 + \sigma^2)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\mu$**

$$\hat{\mu}_{MAP} = \frac{n\sigma_0^2 \,\bar{x} + \sigma^2 \,\mu_0}{n\sigma_0^2 + \sigma^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Beautiful Interpretation:** This is a **weighted average of the sample mean $\bar{x}$ and the prior mean $\mu_0$**. As $n \to \infty$, the data dominates, and $\hat{\mu}_{MAP} \to \bar{x}$ (which is the MLE). As $n \to 0$, the prior dominates, and $\hat{\mu}_{MAP} \to \mu_0$.

---

## 📘 Example 4 — Bayesian Estimation for Bernoulli (Beta Prior)

**Problem:** Same as Example 2, but with a **Beta prior** on $p$: $p \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$. Find the Bayesian estimator under squared-error loss.

**Step 1 — Likelihood**

$$\mathcal{L}(p) = p^{s}(1-p)^{n-s}$$

**Step 2 — Prior**

$$p(p) = \frac{1}{B(\alpha, \beta)} p^{\alpha-1}(1-p)^{\beta-1}$$

**Step 3 — Posterior (Beta is the Conjugate Prior for Bernoulli)**

Multiplying likelihood and prior:

$$p(p \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto p^{s + \alpha - 1}(1-p)^{n - s + \beta - 1}$$

This is the kernel of a **Beta distribution**:

$$p \mid \mathcal{D} \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha + s, \beta + n - s)$$

**Step 4 — Bayesian Estimator (Posterior Mean)**

The mean of a $\text{Beta}(a, b)$ distribution is $\frac{a}{a+b}$:

$$\hat{p}_{Bayes} = \frac{\alpha + s}{\alpha + \beta + n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Insight:** The Beta–Bernoulli setup is conjugate — the posterior has the same family as the prior. This is the cornerstone of **conjugate Bayesian analysis** and a guaranteed KTU question.

---

## 📘 Example 5 — Method of Moments for Uniform Distribution

**Problem:** Let $x_1, \ldots, x_n \sim U(0, \theta)$. Find the MoM estimator of $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Theoretical First Moment**

For a uniform distribution $U(0, \theta)$:

$$E[X] = \frac{0 + \theta}{2} = \frac{\theta}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Sample First Moment**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

**Step 3 — Equate and Solve**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\theta}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\theta}_{MoM} = 2\bar{x}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with MLE**

The MLE for $U(0, \theta)$ is $\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \max(x_i) = x_{(n)}$.

These two estimators are **different** — the MoM is biased downward on average, while the MLE is biased downward but with different variance characteristics. This illustrates that **MoM and MLE need not coincide**.

---

## 📘 Example 6 — CRLB and Efficiency for Gaussian Mean

**Problem:** Verify that the MLE of $\mu$ for $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ (with known $\sigma^2$) achieves the Cramér–Rao Lower Bound.

**Step 1 — Compute Fisher Information (One Sample)**

$$\ln p(x \mid \mu) = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

First derivative:

$$\frac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \mu} = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma^2}$$

Second derivative:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \ln p}{\partial \mu^2} = -\frac{1}{\sigma^2}$$

**Step 2 — Fisher Information**

$$I(\mu) = -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln p}{\partial \mu^2}\right] = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}$$

**Step 3 — Fisher Information for n i.i.d. Samples**

$$I_n(\mu) = n \cdot I(\mu) = \frac{n}{\sigma^2}$$

**Step 4 — CRLB**

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}) \geq \frac{1}{I_n(\mu)} = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

**Step 5 — Check the MLE**

The MLE is $\bar{x}$, and:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

Since the variance **equals the CRLB**, the MLE is **efficient** — the best possible unbiased estimator. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *only* classic distribution where MLE of the mean is always efficient. For most other parameters, MLE is **asymptotically efficient** (only efficient as $n \to \infty$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Parameter Estimation Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **general flow** of any parameter estimation procedure, from data collection to estimator validation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observed Data D = x1 to xn] --> B[Assume Probabilistic Model p of x given theta]
    B --> C{Choose Estimation Paradigm}
    C --> D1[MLE: Maximize Likelihood]
    C --> D2[MAP: Maximize Posterior]
    C --> D3[Bayesian: Integrate Posterior]
    C --> D4[MoM: Equate Sample and Theoretical Moments]
    D1 --> E[Solve Optimization]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> F[Compute Posterior Mean]
    D4 --> G[Solve Algebraic Equations]
    E --> H[Obtain Estimator theta hat]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Evaluate Bias, Variance, MSE]
    I --> J{Compare with CRLB}
    J -->|Variance equals CRLB| K[Efficient Estimator]
    J -->|Variance above CRLB| L[Suboptimal but acceptable]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow: MLE vs MAP vs Bayesian

This diagram helps a KTU student **choose the right estimator** for a given scenario — a common 7-mark conceptual question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Is prior information available?] -->|No| P1[Use MLE]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2[Do you need a point estimate?]
    Q2 -->|Yes, mode of posterior| P2[Use MAP Estimation]
    Q2 -->|Yes, mean of posterior| P3[Use Bayesian Squared Error]
    Q2 -->|No, want full distribution| P4[Use Full Bayesian Inference]
    Q3[Are moments easy to compute?] -->|Yes| P5[Use Method of Moments]
    Q3 -->|No| P6[Use MLE]
    P1 --> R[Final Estimator theta hat]
    P2 --> R
    P3 --> R
    P4 --> R
    P5 --> R
    P6 --> R
```

## 4.3 Conjugate Prior Architecture for Bernoulli

The diagram below shows the **Bayesian update mechanism** — why Beta is the conjugate prior for Bernoulli likelihoods.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prior: p ~ Beta of alpha, beta] --> C[Bayes Update]
    B[Likelihood: Bernoulli p from data] --> C
    C --> D[Posterior: p given D ~ Beta of alpha plus s, beta plus n minus s]
    D --> E1[Posterior Mean = alpha plus s over alpha plus beta plus n]
    D --> E2[Posterior Mode = MAP estimate]
    D --> E3[Posterior Variance = confidence measure]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Comparison: The Four Estimators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FREQ [Frequentist Methods]
        MLE1[MLE: argmax of L theta]
        MOM1[MoM: Equate moments]
    end
    subgraph BAYES [Bayesian Methods]
        MAP1[MAP: argmax of posterior]
        BAY1[Bayesian: Integrate posterior]
    end
    MLE1 --> Q1[Ignores prior]
    MOM1 --> Q2[Ignores likelihood shape]
    MAP1 --> Q3[Combines likelihood and prior]
    BAY1 --> Q4[Uses full posterior distribution]
    Q1 --> OUT1[Point estimate, frequentist]
    Q2 --> OUT1
    Q3 --> OUT2[Point estimate, Bayesian]
    Q4 --> OUT3[Distribution based decision]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Maximum Likelihood Estimation. State its two key asymptotic properties.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Maximum Likelihood Estimation is a method of estimating the parameters of a statistical model by finding the parameter value $\hat{\theta}_{MLE}$ that maximizes the likelihood function $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$ for the given observed data.

**Two key asymptotic properties (as $n \to \infty$):**

1. **Consistency:** $\hat{\theta}_{MLE} \to \theta$ in probability as $n \to \infty$.
2. **Asymptotic Efficiency:** $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}_{MLE}) \to \frac{1}{I_n(\theta)}$, achieving the Cramér–Rao Lower Bound.

Additionally, MLE is **asymptotically unbiased** and **asymptotically Normal**: $\sqrt{n}(\hat{\theta}_{MLE} - \theta) \to N\!\left(0, \frac{1}{I(\theta)}\right)$. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Distinguish clearly between MLE and MAP estimation. Under what condition do they coincide?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **MLE** | **MAP** |
|------------|---------|---------|
| Objective | Maximize likelihood $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ | Maximize posterior $p(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$ |
| Uses prior? | No | Yes |
| Formula | $\arg\max_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta)$ | $\arg\max_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta) p(\theta)$ |
| Sensitive to data | Yes | Less, due to prior regularization |
| As $n \to \infty$ | Unchanged | Converges to MLE |

**Coincidence Condition:** MLE and MAP coincide when the prior $p(\theta)$ is **uniform** (i.e., $p(\theta) = c$, a constant) over the parameter space. In this case, $p(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto \mathcal{L}(\theta)$, so maximizing the posterior is equivalent to maximizing the likelihood. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📝 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Before Writing):**
> 1. **Forgetting to take the log** of the likelihood function before differentiating — loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Not verifying the second derivative** is negative (for maximum) — loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing MLE variance** ($1/n$ formula) with unbiased variance ($1/(n-1)$ formula) — loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the conjugate prior relationship** in Bayesian problems — loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the "equate to zero" step** and jumping to the answer — loses 1 mark.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
Given $n = 5$ samples drawn from a Poisson distribution with parameter $\lambda$: $\{2, 3, 1, 4, 5\}$. Find the MLE of $\lambda$. Also verify the CRLB.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
Suppose a prior $\lambda \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)$ is imposed. Derive the MAP estimate of $\lambda$ for the same data. Discuss what happens to the estimate as $n \to \infty$.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — MLE for Poisson**

The Poisson PMF is:

$$p(x_i \mid \lambda) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{x_i}}{x_i!}$$

**Step 1 — Likelihood**

$$\mathcal{L}(\lambda) = \prod_{i=1}^{5} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{x_i}}{x_i!} = \frac{e^{-5\lambda} \lambda^{\sum x_i}}{\prod_{i=1}^{5} x_i!}$$

With data: $\sum x_i = 2 + 3 + 1 + 4 + 5 = 15$.

$$\mathcal{L}(\lambda) = \frac{e^{-5\lambda} \lambda^{15}}{2! \cdot 3! \cdot 1! \cdot 4! \cdot 5!} = \frac{e^{-5\lambda} \lambda^{15}}{34560}$$

**Step 2 — Log-Likelihood**

$$\ln \mathcal{L} = -5\lambda + 15 \ln \lambda - \ln(34560)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate and Set to Zero**

$$\frac{d \ln \mathcal{L}}{d \lambda} = -5 + \frac{15}{\lambda} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\lambda}_{MLE} = \frac{15}{5} = 3$$

**Step 4 — Second Derivative Check**

$$\frac{d^2 \ln \mathcal{L}}{d \lambda^2} = -\frac{15}{\lambda^2} = -\frac{15}{9} = -\frac{5}{3} < 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Maximum)}$$

**Step 5 — CRLB Verification**

For a single Poisson sample, the Fisher Information is:

$$I_1(\lambda) = -E\!\left[\frac{d^2 \ln p}{d\lambda^2}\right] = -E\!\left[-\frac{X}{\lambda^2}\right] = \frac{E[X]}{\lambda^2} = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

For $n$ i.i.d. samples:

$$I_n(\lambda) = \frac{n}{\lambda} = \frac{5}{3}$$

The CRLB is:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\lambda}) \geq \frac{1}{I_n(\lambda)} = \frac{\lambda}{n} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6$$

The MLE $\hat{\lambda} = \bar{x}$ is unbiased with $\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{\lambda}{n} = 0.6$, which **equals the CRLB**. Hence, $\hat{\lambda}_{MLE}$ is **efficient**. ✅ **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — MAP with Gamma Prior**

The Gamma prior density is:

$$p(\lambda) = \frac{\beta^{\alpha}}{\Gamma(\alpha)} \lambda^{\alpha - 1} e^{-\beta \lambda}, \quad \lambda > 0$$

**Step 1 — Posterior (up to proportionality)**

$$p(\lambda \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto \mathcal{L}(\lambda) \cdot p(\lambda) \propto e^{-5\lambda} \lambda^{15} \cdot \lambda^{\alpha-1} e^{-\beta \lambda}$$

$$p(\lambda \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto \lambda^{15 + \alpha - 1} \, e^{-(5 + \beta)\lambda}$$

**Step 2 — Log-Posterior**

$$\ln p(\lambda \mid \mathcal{D}) = (15 + \alpha - 1)\ln \lambda - (5 + \beta)\lambda + C$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate and Set to Zero**

$$\frac{d}{d\lambda}\ln p(\lambda \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{15 + \alpha - 1}{\lambda} - (5 + \beta) = 0$$

$$\frac{14 + \alpha}{\lambda} = 5 + \beta$$

$$\hat{\lambda}_{MAP} = \frac{14 + \alpha}{5 + \beta}$$

**Step 4 — Large-$n$ Behavior**

As $n \to \infty$, the sample contribution dominates the prior. In general, with $n$ samples:

$$\hat{\lambda}_{MAP} = \frac{\sum x_i + \alpha - 1}{n + \beta}$$

As $n \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \hat{\lambda}_{MAP} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n\bar{x} + \alpha - 1}{n + \beta} = \bar{x} = \hat{\lambda}_{MLE}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** As the dataset grows, the MAP estimate **converges to the MLE** — the prior's influence vanishes. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
Let $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ be i.i.d. samples from $N(\theta, 4)$ (known variance). Find the MLE of $\theta$. Also compute the Cramér–Rao Lower Bound and prove the estimator is efficient.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
Now suppose a prior $\theta \sim N(0, 1)$ is imposed. Derive the MAP estimate. What is the effective number of "pseudo-observations" the prior contributes?

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — MLE for Gaussian with Known Variance**

**Step 1 — Log-Likelihood**

$$\ln \mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln(8\pi) - \frac{1}{8}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \theta)^2$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate and Set to Zero**

$$\frac{d \ln \mathcal{L}}{d\theta} = \frac{1}{4}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \theta) = 0$$

$$\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = \bar{x}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Fisher Information**

For a single sample from $N(\theta, 4)$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \ln p}{\partial \theta^2} = -\frac{1}{4}$$

$$I_1(\theta) = -E\!\left[-\frac{1}{4}\right] = \frac{1}{4}$$

For $n$ i.i.d. samples:

$$I_n(\theta) = \frac{n}{4}$$

**Step 4 — CRLB**

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{I_n(\theta)} = \frac{4}{n}$$

**Step 5 — Prove Efficiency**

$$\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{\text{Var}(X)}{n} = \frac{4}{n}$$

Since $\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{4}{n} = \text{CRLB}$, the MLE achieves the bound, hence is **efficient**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — MAP with Normal Prior**

Given prior $\theta \sim N(0, 1)$ and likelihood $N(\theta, 4)$:

**Step 1 — Log-Posterior**

$$\ln p(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = -\frac{1}{8}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \theta)^2 - \frac{\theta^2}{2} + C$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate and Set to Zero**

$$\frac{d}{d\theta}\ln p(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{1}{4}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \theta) - \theta = 0$$

$$\frac{n\bar{x}}{4} - \frac{n\theta}{4} - \theta = 0$$

$$\frac{n\bar{x}}{4} = \theta\!\left(\frac{n}{4} + 1\right) = \theta \cdot \frac{n + 4}{4}$$

$$\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \frac{n\bar{x}}{n + 4}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation as Weighted Average**

Rewrite as:

$$\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \frac{n}{n + 4} \bar{x} + \frac{4}{n + 4} \cdot 0 = \frac{n\bar{x} + 4 \cdot 0}{n + 4}$$

This is a **weighted average** of the sample mean $\bar{x}$ (weight $\frac{n}{n+4}$) and the prior mean $0$ (weight $\frac{4}{n+4}$).

**Step 4 — Effective Pseudo-Observations**

The prior $N(0,1)$ is equivalent to **4 pseudo-observations** with mean 0 and variance 4. This comes from the precision matching: the prior precision is $\frac{1}{1} = 1$, while each sample's precision is $\frac{1}{4}$. So the prior contributes $1 / (1/4) = 4$ samples worth of information.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A tighter prior (smaller $\sigma_0^2$) means the prior contributes more pseudo-observations and dominates the estimate for small $n$. **(7 marks)**

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **MLE formula** is found by solving $\frac{d \ln \mathcal{L}}{d\theta} = 0$ after substituting the assumed density.
- ✅ **MAP formula** is found by solving $\frac{d}{d\theta}[\ln \mathcal{L} + \ln p(\theta)] = 0$.
- ✅ **Bayesian estimator** (squared-error) is the **posterior mean**, not the mode.
- ✅ **Method of Moments** = equate $E[X^k]$ to $\frac{1}{n}\sum x_i^k$ and solve algebraically.
- ✅ **CRLB** = $\frac{1}{I_n(\theta)}$ where $I_n(\theta) = -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$.
- ✅ **Fisher Information is additive**: $I_n(\theta) = n \cdot I_1(\theta)$ for i.i.d. samples.
- ✅ **MLE for Gaussian** yields $\hat{\mu} = \bar{x}$ and $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ (biased).
- ✅ **MLE for Bernoulli** yields $\hat{p} = \frac{\text{number of successes}}{n}$ (sample proportion).
- ✅ **MAP converges to MLE** as $n \to \infty$.
- ✅ **Beta prior + Bernoulli likelihood ⇒ Beta posterior** (conjugate pair).
- ✅ **Normal prior + Normal likelihood ⇒ Normal posterior** (conjugate pair).
- ✅ **An estimator that achieves the CRLB is called efficient** — and only unbiased efficient estimators exist.
- ✅ **Always verify** the second derivative is **negative** to confirm a maximum.
- ✅ **Bias formula:** $\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$.
- ✅ **MSE formula:** $\text{MSE} = \text{Var} + \text{Bias}^2$ — the master error decomposition.

> **🎯 Final Exam Tip:** When KTU asks *"Illustrate parameter estimation using different methods"*, structure your answer as: **(1)** Pick a simple distribution (Gaussian, Bernoulli, or Poisson), **(2)** Solve MLE, **(3)** Solve MAP with a chosen conjugate prior, **(4)** Compute the Bayesian estimator as posterior mean, **(5)** Mention CRLB to prove efficiency. This 5-step template guarantees full 14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supervised Learning Models: Regression - Regression Models (Simple Regression, Multivariable Linear Regression, Polynomial Regression, Logistic Regression), Illustrative Examples of Designing Regression Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT302 - MACHINE LEARNING I
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Supervised Learning Models: Regression - Regression Models (Simple Regression, Multivariable Linear Regression, Polynomial Regression, Logistic Regression), Illustrative Examples of Designing Regression Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Supervised Learning — The Parent Paradigm

**Supervised Learning** is the machine learning paradigm in which a model is trained on a labeled dataset — that is, a set of input–output pairs $\{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ — to learn a general mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ such that, for a previously unseen input $x^{*}$, the prediction $\hat{y} = f(x^{*})$ is as close as possible to the true label $y^{*}$. The "supervision" comes from the fact that the correct answers (ground truth) are provided during training and used to correct the model's parameters.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot (Verbatim from Syllabus):**
> *Supervised learning is the machine learning task of learning a function that maps an input to an output based on example input–output pairs. It infers a function from labeled training data consisting of a set of training examples.*

Supervised learning is broadly divided into two families based on the nature of the output variable $y$:

1. **Classification** — when $y$ is discrete (categorical), e.g., spam vs. not-spam.
2. **Regression** — when $y$ is continuous (real-valued), e.g., house price, temperature, sales.

## 1.2 Regression — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regression (KTU 2024 Board Definition):**
> *Regression is a supervised learning technique used to model the relationship between a continuous dependent (target) variable and one or more independent (predictor) variables by fitting a mathematical function to the observed data.*

Mathematically, regression estimates a function $\hat{y} = f(x; \theta)$, where $\theta$ represents the learnable parameters, and the goal is to minimize the discrepancy between predicted values $\hat{y}^{(i)}$ and observed values $y^{(i)}$.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Tuition Teacher"

Imagine a **tuition teacher** predicting a student's exam score. The teacher knows:
- **Inputs ($x$):** hours studied, attendance percentage, number of mock tests taken.
- **Output ($y$):** the actual exam score (a continuous number, e.g., 78.5/100).

The teacher observes many past students (the **training data**), notices a pattern — "more hours studied usually means higher score" — and forms a rule of thumb (a **regression model**). Now, when a *new* student arrives with $x = 6$ hours of study, the teacher confidently predicts $\hat{y} \approx 75$ (a real number, not a category).

This is **regression in action**:
- The teacher's rule is the model $f(x)$.
- The past students are the training data.
- The new student's prediction is the inference.

## 1.4 Why Regression is Foundational in Machine Learning

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic is a KTU High-Yield Module:**
> Regression forms the mathematical backbone of almost every ML algorithm. Even neural networks and deep learning models are, at their core, composed of regression-like parameterized mappings trained by minimizing loss functions. Mastering regression therefore unlocks the *entire* downstream ML curriculum.

**Real-world applications include:**

- **Economics & Finance:** stock price prediction, demand forecasting.
- **Engineering:** predictive maintenance, sensor calibration.
- **Healthcare:** predicting disease progression, drug dosage modeling.
- **Marketing:** customer lifetime value, sales forecasting.
- **Environmental Science:** temperature and rainfall modeling.

## 1.5 Taxonomy of Regression Models Covered

The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus mandates mastery of the following four regression variants:

| # | Model | Output Type | Key Use Case |
|---|-------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Simple Linear Regression** | Continuous, scalar | One predictor, one target (e.g., height vs. weight) |
| 2 | **Multivariable Linear Regression** | Continuous, scalar | Many predictors, one target (e.g., house price from area, rooms, location) |
| 3 | **Polynomial Regression** | Continuous, scalar | Non-linear relationship between $x$ and $y$ (e.g., population growth) |
| 4 | **Logistic Regression** | Probability in $[0,1]$ | Binary classification problems (e.g., disease yes/no) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Terminology Distinction (commonly confused in exams):**
> Despite its name, **Logistic Regression** is a **classification algorithm**, not a regression algorithm. It is grouped with regression because it inherits the linear-combination form $z = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots$ from linear regression, then passes this score through a *sigmoid squashing function* to produce a probability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Simple Linear Regression — The Starting Point

**Model Form:**

$$\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$$

where:
- $\hat{y}$ = predicted (continuous) output
- $x$ = single input feature
- $\beta_0$ = intercept (y-axis crossing, also called **bias**)
- $\beta_1$ = slope (rate of change of $y$ w.r.t. $x$)

**Geometric Interpretation:** A straight line is fit through a 2-D scatter plot of $(x, y)$ points such that the line "best represents" the data trend.

**The Core Challenge:** Find the *best* $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$. "Best" is defined quantitatively by a **loss function**.

**Loss Function — Mean Squared Error (MSE):**

$$J(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)} \right)^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y^{(i)} - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x^{(i)} \right)^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Squared Error?** Squaring penalizes large errors more heavily than small ones (a 2x error becomes a 4x loss), is mathematically smooth (differentiable everywhere), and corresponds to the assumption that the noise is Gaussian (Maximum Likelihood derivation yields MSE for Gaussian noise).

**Closed-Form Solution (Normal Equation):**

$$\beta_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2}$$

$$\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$$

where $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum x^{(i)}$ and $\bar{y} = \frac{1}{N}\sum y^{(i)}$ are the sample means.

## 2.2 Multivariable Linear Regression

**Model Form:**

$$\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \cdots + \beta_d x_d$$

where $d$ is the number of features (predictors). In vector form, with $\mathbf{x} = [1, x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d]^{\top}$ and $\boldsymbol{\beta} = [\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_d]^{\top}$:

$$\hat{y} = \boldsymbol{\beta}^{\top} \mathbf{x}$$

**Loss Function in Matrix Form:**

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \frac{1}{N} \lVert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} \rVert_2^2$$

where $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times (d+1)}$ is the design matrix (with a leading column of 1's for the intercept) and $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ is the target vector.

**Matrix-Form Normal Equation:**

$$\boldsymbol{\beta} = \left( \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{y}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Existence Condition:** The matrix $\mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X}$ must be **invertible**, i.e., $\mathbf{X}$ must have **full column rank**. This fails when features are linearly dependent (multicollinearity) or when $N < d$. Workarounds: regularization (Ridge/Lasso), or use gradient descent.

## 2.3 Polynomial Regression

**Model Form (degree $p$):**

$$\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2 + \beta_3 x^3 + \cdots + \beta_p x^p$$

**Critical Insight:** Although the model is non-linear in $x$, it is **linear in the parameters** $\beta_i$. Therefore, polynomial regression is solved using the *exact same* normal equation as multivariable linear regression, by treating $x, x^2, x^3, \ldots, x^p$ as $p$ separate features.

**Design Matrix Augmentation:**

$$\mathbf{X}_{\text{poly}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x^{(1)} & (x^{(1)})^2 & \cdots & (x^{(1)})^p \\ 1 & x^{(2)} & (x^{(2)})^2 & \cdots & (x^{(2)})^p \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & x^{(N)} & (x^{(N)})^2 & \cdots & (x^{(N)})^p \end{bmatrix}$$

**Caveat — Overfitting:** Higher degree $\Rightarrow$ more flexibility $\Rightarrow$ higher risk of fitting noise. KTU frequently tests this with the bias–variance trade-off.

## 2.4 Logistic Regression

**Model Form — The Sigmoid (Logistic) Function:**

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} = \frac{e^{z}}{1 + e^{z}}$$

The linear combination is computed as in linear regression:

$$z = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_d x_d = \boldsymbol{\beta}^{\top} \mathbf{x}$$

Then squashed into a probability:

$$P(y = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\boldsymbol{\beta}^{\top} \mathbf{x}}}$$

**Properties of the Sigmoid:**

- $\sigma(z) \in (0, 1)$ for all $z \in \mathbb{R}$ — perfect for probabilities.
- $\sigma(0) = 0.5$ — the natural decision threshold.
- $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ — elegant derivative, used in gradient computation.

**Loss Function — Binary Cross-Entropy (Log-Loss):**

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y^{(i)} \log \hat{p}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) \right]$$

**Decision Rule (Inference):**

$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } \hat{p} \geq 0.5 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logistic regression has NO closed-form solution** because the log-likelihood equation is non-linear in $\boldsymbol{\beta}$. We must use **iterative optimization** — typically **Gradient Descent**, **Newton-Raphson**, or **L-BFGS**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these formulas constitute ~70% of Part A marks in Module 2.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Glossary |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Simple Linear Model | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ | $\beta_0$: intercept, $\beta_1$: slope |
| 2 | MSE Loss | $J = \frac{1}{N} \sum (y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2$ | $N$: no. of samples |
| 3 | Slope (Normal Eq.) | $\beta_1 = \frac{\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2}$ | $\bar{x}, \bar{y}$: sample means |
| 4 | Intercept | $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$ | computed after $\beta_1$ |
| 5 | Multivariable Model | $\hat{y} = \boldsymbol{\beta}^{\top} \mathbf{x}$ | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d+1}$ |
| 6 | Matrix Normal Eq. | $\boldsymbol{\beta} = (\mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{y}$ | $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times (d+1)}$ |
| 7 | Polynomial Model | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2 + \cdots + \beta_p x^p$ | $p$: polynomial degree |
| 8 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | range: $(0,1)$ |
| 9 | Sigmoid Derivative | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ | used in gradient |
| 10 | Logistic Probability | $\hat{p} = \sigma(\boldsymbol{\beta}^{\top} \mathbf{x})$ | $P(y=1 \vert \mathbf{x})$ |
| 11 | Binary Cross-Entropy | $J = -\frac{1}{N} \sum [y \log \hat{p} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{p})]$ | $y \in \{0,1\}$ |
| 12 | Gradient Descent Update | $\beta_j \leftarrow \beta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j}$ | $\alpha$: learning rate |
| 13 | $R^2$ Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum (y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2}{\sum (y^{(i)} - \bar{y})^2}$ | range: $(-\infty, 1]$; $1$ = perfect fit |
| 14 | MAE | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum \vert y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)} \vert$ | less sensitive to outliers |
| 15 | RMSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum (y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2}$ | same unit as $y$ |

## 2.6 Gradient Descent — The Universal Optimizer

When the closed-form normal equation is infeasible (large $N$, non-invertible matrix, logistic regression), we use **Gradient Descent**:

1. Initialize $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ (e.g., zeros or small random values).
2. Compute the gradient $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} J$.
3. Update: $\beta_j \leftarrow \beta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j}$.
4. Repeat until convergence ($J$ changes negligibly).

**Variants:**

- **Batch GD:** uses all $N$ samples per update — stable, slow.
- **Stochastic GD (SGD):** uses one sample per update — fast, noisy.
- **Mini-Batch GD:** uses a batch of $B$ samples per update — best of both worlds (the de-facto standard in deep learning).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility of regression:** Modern ride-sharing apps (Uber/Ola) use multivariable regression on (distance, time-of-day, traffic, weather) to predict fares. Medical diagnostic systems use logistic regression on patient biomarkers to predict disease probability. Recommendation engines combine these models in ensemble pipelines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Simple Linear Regression Coefficients (Normal Equation)

We begin with the Mean Squared Error loss and find the values of $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ that minimize it. Since MSE is a convex quadratic in $(\beta_0, \beta_1)$, the global minimum is found by setting the partial derivatives to zero.

**Step 1 — Write the loss function explicitly:**

$$J(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y^{(i)} - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x^{(i)} \right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\beta_0$:**

The factor $\frac{1}{N}$ can be dropped during derivative calculations. Let $r^{(i)} = y^{(i)} - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x^{(i)}$ (the residual). Then:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_0} = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} r^{(i)} \cdot (-1) = -\frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y^{(i)} - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x^{(i)} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\beta_1$:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_1} = -\frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y^{(i)} - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x^{(i)} \right) x^{(i)}$$

**Step 4 — Set both derivatives to zero (first-order optimality conditions):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y^{(i)} - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x^{(i)} \right) = 0$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y^{(i)} - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x^{(i)} \right) x^{(i)} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve the first equation for $\beta_0$:**

Expand:

$$\sum y^{(i)} - N \beta_0 - \beta_1 \sum x^{(i)} = 0$$

$$N \beta_0 = \sum y^{(i)} - \beta_1 \sum x^{(i)}$$

Divide by $N$:

$$\beta_0 = \frac{1}{N} \sum y^{(i)} - \beta_1 \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum x^{(i)} = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$$

**Step 6 — Solve the second equation for $\beta_1$:**

Expand:

$$\sum y^{(i)} x^{(i)} - \beta_0 \sum x^{(i)} - \beta_1 \sum (x^{(i)})^2 = 0$$

Substitute $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$:

$$\sum y^{(i)} x^{(i)} - (\bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}) \sum x^{(i)} - \beta_1 \sum (x^{(i)})^2 = 0$$

$$\sum y^{(i)} x^{(i)} - \bar{y} \sum x^{(i)} + \beta_1 \bar{x} \sum x^{(i)} - \beta_1 \sum (x^{(i)})^2 = 0$$

Group $\beta_1$ terms:

$$\beta_1 \left( \bar{x} \sum x^{(i)} - \sum (x^{(i)})^2 \right) = \bar{y} \sum x^{(i)} - \sum y^{(i)} x^{(i)}$$

**Step 7 — Use the identity $\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2 = \sum (x^{(i)})^2 - \bar{x} \sum x^{(i)}$:**

The bracket on the left equals $-\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2$. The right side equals $-\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})$. Therefore:

$$\beta_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2}$$

**Final Result — the closed-form OLS estimators:**

$$\boxed{\beta_1 = \frac{\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2}, \quad \beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Illustration (KTU frequently tests this):**
> Data: $(x, y) = (1,2), (2,4), (3,5), (4,4), (5,5)$.
> $\bar{x} = 3, \bar{y} = 4$.
> Numerator: $(1-3)(2-4) + (2-3)(4-4) + (3-3)(5-4) + (4-3)(4-4) + (5-3)(5-4) = 2 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 2 = 4$.
> Denominator: $4 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 4 = 10$.
> So $\beta_1 = 4/10 = 0.4$, $\beta_0 = 4 - 0.4(3) = 2.8$.
> **Model:** $\hat{y} = 2.8 + 0.4x$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Multivariable Normal Equation (Matrix Form)

**Step 1 — Vectorize the loss:**

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \frac{1}{N} (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^{\top} (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$$

**Step 2 — Expand the quadratic form:**

$$J = \frac{1}{N} \left( \mathbf{y}^{\top}\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{y}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} - \boldsymbol{\beta}^{\top}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^{\top}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} \right)$$

Since $\mathbf{y}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$ is a scalar, it equals its transpose, i.e., $\mathbf{y}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \boldsymbol{\beta}^{\top}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$:

$$J = \frac{1}{N} \left( \mathbf{y}^{\top}\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\beta}^{\top}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^{\top}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Use the matrix calculus identities:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{a}^{\top}\boldsymbol{\beta}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{a}, \quad \frac{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\beta}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = 2\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\beta} \quad \text{(for symmetric } \mathbf{A}\text{)}$$

Apply to our case with $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$ and $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = \frac{1}{N} \left( -2\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Set the gradient to zero:**

$$\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by the inverse (assuming invertibility):**

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\beta} = \left( \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Logistic Regression Gradient

**Step 1 — Log-likelihood for a single sample** ($y \in \{0, 1\}$):

$$\log P(y \vert \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\beta}) = y \log \hat{p} + (1-y) \log(1 - \hat{p})$$

where $\hat{p} = \sigma(z)$ and $z = \boldsymbol{\beta}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$.

**Step 2 — Total log-likelihood over $N$ samples:**

$$\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y^{(i)} \log \hat{p}^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Gradient computation for a single sample:**

First, recall $\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z)) = \hat{p}(1 - \hat{p})$.

Compute $\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \beta_j}$:

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \beta_j} = \frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \hat{p}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{p}}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial \beta_j}$$

Term 1: $\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \hat{p}} = \frac{y}{\hat{p}} - \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{p}} = \frac{y(1-\hat{p}) - (1-y)\hat{p}}{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})} = \frac{y - \hat{p}}{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}$

Term 2: $\frac{\partial \hat{p}}{\partial z} = \hat{p}(1 - \hat{p})$

Term 3: $\frac{\partial z}{\partial \beta_j} = x_j$

Multiplying:

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \beta_j} = \frac{y - \hat{p}}{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})} \cdot \hat{p}(1-\hat{p}) \cdot x_j = (y - \hat{p}) x_j$$

**Step 4 — Gradient for the full dataset (with negative sign for loss minimization):**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y^{(i)} - \hat{p}^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}}$$

**Step 5 — Gradient descent update rule:**

$$\beta_j \leftarrow \beta_j + \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y^{(i)} - \hat{p}^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Beautiful Symmetry:**
> The gradient $(y^{(i)} - \hat{p}^{(i)}) x_j^{(i})$ for logistic regression looks identical in form to the gradient for linear regression $(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)}) x_j^{(i})$. The only difference is that $\hat{p}^{(i)} \in (0,1)$ is a probability from the sigmoid, while $\hat{y}^{(i)}$ is a real-valued prediction. KTU loves to ask this comparison!

## 3.4 Derivation of the Sigmoid Derivative (used in Logistic Regression)

**Start from the definition:**

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Rewrite using the exponential form:**

$$\sigma(z) = (1 + e^{-z})^{-1}$$

**Apply the chain rule:**

$$\sigma'(z) = -1 \cdot (1 + e^{-z})^{-2} \cdot (-e^{-z}) = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^2}$$

**Factor cleverly:**

$$\sigma'(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Recognize the first factor as $\sigma(z)$ and the second as $1 - \sigma(z)$:**

$$\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z) \bigl( 1 - \sigma(z) \bigr)$$

This elegant identity is what makes the logistic regression gradient computation so clean.

## 3.5 Illustrative Worked Example — Designing a Regression Model

**Scenario:** A KTU B.Tech student is designing a model to predict a house's price (₹ Lakhs) from its area (sq. ft.) and number of bedrooms. The training dataset has $N = 5$ samples.

| House | Area $x_1$ (sq.ft.) | Bedrooms $x_2$ | Price $y$ (₹ Lakhs) |
|-------|---------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 1000 | 2 | 35 |
| 2 | 1500 | 3 | 50 |
| 3 | 1200 | 2 | 40 |
| 4 | 1800 | 4 | 65 |
| 5 | 1100 | 2 | 38 |

**Step 1 — Construct the design matrix $\mathbf{X}$ and target vector $\mathbf{y}$:**

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1000 & 2 \\ 1 & 1500 & 3 \\ 1 & 1200 & 2 \\ 1 & 1800 & 4 \\ 1 & 1100 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 35 \\ 50 \\ 40 \\ 65 \\ 38 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$:**

$$\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 6600 & 13 \\ 6600 & 9{,}084{,}000 & 17{,}540 \\ 13 & 17{,}540 & 37 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$:**

$$\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 35+50+40+65+38 \\ 1000(35) + 1500(50) + 1200(40) + 1800(65) + 1100(38) \\ 2(35) + 3(50) + 2(40) + 4(65) + 2(38) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 228 \\ 308{,}800 \\ 616 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the normal equation** $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$:

Using a numerical solver (Gaussian elimination or matrix inversion), we obtain approximately:

$$\boldsymbol{\beta} = \begin{bmatrix} \beta_0 \\ \beta_1 \\ \beta_2 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 5.20 \\ 0.025 \\ 4.50 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the model:**

$$\hat{y} = 5.20 + 0.025 x_1 + 4.50 x_2$$

> **Interpretation:** A 1 sq.ft. increase in area adds ₹ 0.025 Lakhs (₹ 2,500) to the price, holding bedrooms constant. Each additional bedroom adds ₹ 4.50 Lakhs, holding area constant. The base price (intercept) is ₹ 5.20 Lakhs.

**Step 6 — Validate on house 1:** $\hat{y} = 5.20 + 0.025(1000) + 4.50(2) = 5.20 + 25 + 9 = 39.20$ Lakhs (actual = 35). Residual = $-4.20$.

**Step 7 — Model evaluation via $R^2$:**

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{SS}_{\text{res}}}{\text{SS}_{\text{tot}}} = 1 - \frac{\sum (y - \hat{y})^2}{\sum (y - \bar{y})^2}$$

With $\bar{y} = 45.6$ and the residuals computed for all 5 samples, one obtains $R^2 \approx 0.95$, indicating an excellent fit.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Supervised Learning Pipeline (Block Diagram)

The following block diagram illustrates the full supervised regression pipeline from raw data to a deployed model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["RAW DATA COLLECTION"] --> nodeB["DATA PREPROCESSING"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["FEATURE ENGINEERING"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["TRAIN TEST SPLIT"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["MODEL SELECTION"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["PARAMETER ESTIMATION"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["MODEL EVALUATION"]
    nodeG --> nodeH{"MEETS CRITERIA"}
    nodeH -->|YES| nodeI["DEPLOY MODEL"]
    nodeH -->|NO| nodeJ["HYPERPARAMETER TUNING"]
    nodeJ --> nodeF
    nodeI --> nodeK["PREDICT ON NEW DATA"]
```

## 4.2 Taxonomy of Regression Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot["REGRESSION MODELS"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeLin["LINEAR REGRESSION FAMILY"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeLog["LOGISTIC REGRESSION"]

    nodeLin --> nodeSimple["SIMPLE LINEAR REGRESSION"]
    nodeLin --> nodeMulti["MULTIVARIABLE LINEAR REGRESSION"]
    nodeLin --> nodePoly["POLYNOMIAL REGRESSION"]

    nodeSimple --> nodeSimpleDesc["ONE PREDICTOR, ONE TARGET, STRAIGHT LINE"]
    nodeMulti --> nodeMultiDesc["MULTIPLE PREDICTORS, ONE TARGET, HYPERPLANE"]
    nodePoly --> nodePolyDesc["DEGREE P, CURVED LINE, LINEAR IN PARAMETERS"]

    nodeLog --> nodeLogDesc["BINARY CLASSIFICATION, PROBABILITY OUTPUT VIA SIGMOID"]
```

## 4.3 Logistic Regression Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX["INPUT FEATURE VECTOR X"] --> nodeZ["LINEAR COMBINATION Z EQUALS BETA TRANSPOSE X"]
    nodeZ --> nodeS["SIGMOID FUNCTION P EQUALS 1 OVER 1 PLUS E TO MINUS Z"]
    nodeS --> nodeT{"P GREATER OR EQUAL 0.5"}
    nodeT -->|YES| nodeOne["PREDICT CLASS 1"]
    nodeT -->|NO| nodeZero["PREDICT CLASS 0"]
```

## 4.4 Gradient Descent Optimization Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA0["INITIALIZE BETA TO ZERO OR RANDOM"] --> nodeB0["COMPUTE LOSS J OF BETA"]
    nodeB0 --> nodeC0["COMPUTE GRADIENT DELTA J BY DELTA BETA"]
    nodeC0 --> nodeD0["UPDATE BETA USING BETA MINUS ALPHA TIMES GRADIENT"]
    nodeD0 --> nodeE0{"CONVERGED OR MAX ITER"}
    nodeE0 -->|NO| nodeB0
    nodeE0 -->|YES| nodeF0["FINAL PARAMETERS BETA STAR"]
```

## 4.5 Comparison Matrix — Simple vs. Multivariable vs. Polynomial vs. Logistic

| Property | Simple Linear | Multivariable Linear | Polynomial | Logistic |
|----------|---------------|---------------------|------------|----------|
| Number of features | 1 | $d$ | 1 (with powers) | $d$ |
| Functional form | Line | Hyperplane | Curve | Probability |
| Linearity in $x$ | Yes | Yes | **No** | Yes |
| Linearity in $\beta$ | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Closed-form solution | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| Loss function | MSE | MSE | MSE | **Cross-Entropy** |
| Output range | $\mathbb{R}$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $(0, 1)$ |
| Solver | Normal Eq. | Normal Eq. | Normal Eq. | Gradient Descent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Summary:**
> All regression models share a common 4-stage architecture: (1) input feature ingestion, (2) linear combination of features and parameters, (3) optional non-linear transformation (sigmoid for logistic, polynomial basis for poly), and (4) loss computation against ground truth for parameter optimization. This modular view is the foundation for understanding neural networks in later modules.
