$$J_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = \text{MSE}(\theta) + \lambda \cdot \Omega(\theta)$$

where $\lambda \geq 0$ is the regularization parameter and $\Omega(\theta)$ is the penalty term.

**Ridge Regularization (L2):**

In Ridge regression, the penalty is the **L2 norm** (squared) of the weight vector:

$$\Omega(\theta) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j^2 = \Vert \theta \Vert_2^2$$

The complete Ridge cost function is:

$$J_{\text{ridge}}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\right)^2 + \frac{\lambda}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\theta_j^2$$

**Properties of Ridge:**
- **Shrinks weights** toward zero but never makes them exactly zero.
- **Keeps all features** in the model (no feature selection).
- Has a **closed-form solution**: $\hat{\theta}_{\text{ridge}} = (X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty$ (note: the $\lambda I$ term guarantees invertibility even when $X^TX$ is singular).
- **Handles multicollinearity** well by distributing weights among correlated features.
- Useful when **all features are believed to contribute** to the output.

**Lasso Regularization (L1):**

In Lasso regression, the penalty is the **L1 norm** (absolute value) of the weight vector:

$$\Omega(\theta) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert \theta_j \vert = \Vert \theta \Vert_1$$

The complete Lasso cost function is:

$$J_{\text{lasso}}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert \theta_j \vert$$

**Properties of Lasso:**
- **Forces some weights to exactly zero**, producing a **sparse model**.
- Performs **automatic feature selection** — irrelevant features are eliminated.
- The name "LASSO" stands for **Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator**.
- **No closed-form solution** — solved using iterative methods like **coordinate descent** because the L1 norm is not differentiable at $\theta_j = 0$.
- Particularly useful in **high-dimensional settings** (e.g., genomics, text mining) where $n_{\text{features}} \gg n_{\text{samples}}$.

**Comparison:** Ridge shrinks all weights proportionally (L2 is gentle near zero), while Lasso aggressively zeroes out small weights (L1 has a sharp corner at zero).

### Part (b) — Elastic Net Regularization (7 Marks)

**Definition:**
Elastic Net is a hybrid regularization technique that **linearly combines** both L1 and L2 penalties. It was proposed by Zou and Hastie to overcome the individual limitations of Ridge and Lasso.

**Cost Function:**

$$J_{\text{EN}}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\right)^2 + \lambda_1 \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j^2 + \lambda_2 \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert \theta_j \vert$$

This is often rewritten using a **mixing ratio** $r \in [0, 1]$ and a single regularization strength $\lambda$:

$$J_{\text{EN}}(\theta) = \text{MSE}(\theta) + \lambda \left[ r \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j^2 + (1-r)\sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert \theta_j \vert \right]$$

- When $r = 1$ → Elastic Net reduces to **Ridge regression**.
- When $r = 0$ → Elastic Net reduces to **Lasso regression**.
- When $r = 0.5$ → balanced combination of both.

**Limitations of Individual Methods that Elastic Net Overcomes:**

1. **Lasso's limitation with correlated features:** When several features are highly correlated, Lasso tends to **arbitrarily select only one** of them and discard the rest, which can be unstable across different training sets. Elastic Net, with its L2 component, **groups correlated features together**, keeping or removing them as a group — leading to more stable feature selection.

2. **Ridge's limitation with sparse solutions:** Ridge never produces exactly zero weights, so it cannot perform feature selection in high-dimensional settings where only a small subset of features is truly relevant. Elastic Net's L1 component **forces sparsity**, retaining the feature-selection power of Lasso.

3. **Limit when $n_{\text{features}} \gg n_{\text{samples}}$:** Lasso is limited to selecting at most $n_{\text{samples}}$ features (a hard mathematical bound). Elastic Net **removes this bound** by allowing the L2 component to handle the additional features, making it more flexible in ultra-high-dimensional problems.

**Practical Use Case:**
In real-world applications like **gene expression analysis** (where thousands of genes are measured for a few hundred patients) or **text classification** (where vocabulary size is enormous), Elastic Net provides a robust balance: it identifies a sparse set of predictive features (via L1) while grouping correlated predictors together (via L2) to give stable, interpretable models.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting the intercept:** The regularization penalty should apply **only to feature weights $\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n$** and **not to the bias term $\theta_0$**. Penalizing $\theta_0$ is mathematically incorrect and costs marks.
> 2. **Confusing the closed-form:** Ridge has a closed form; **Lasso does not** (it is non-differentiable at zero). Do not write $(X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty$ for Lasso.
> 3. **Mixing L1 and L2 symbols:** Use $\lambda$ consistently for the regularization strength. Do not interchange $\lambda$ and $\alpha$ in the same answer.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation steps:** A 7-mark bias-variance question requires **at least 4-5 explicit algebraic steps**. Skipping the "add and subtract" step is the #1 reason students lose 2-3 marks.
> 5. **Calling L1 "Lasso squared":** L1 and L2 are **different norms**, not variants of each other. State this clearly.
> 6. **Forgetting cross-validation:** When asked "how do you choose $\lambda$?", the answer is **k-fold cross-validation**, not guesswork.
> 7. **Not defining irreduible noise $\sigma^2$:** In the bias-variance decomposition, $\sigma^2$ is the variance of the **noise** in the true data-generating process. Mention this explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Overfitting** = Low training error + High test error. Caused by too-complex model. **Underfitting** = High error on both. Caused by too-simple model.
- **Bias** measures systematic error (wrong assumptions). **Variance** measures sensitivity to training data fluctuations.
- The **bias-variance decomposition** formula: $\mathbb{E}[(y-\hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ — **memorize this verbatim**.
- As model complexity **increases** → Bias **decreases**, Variance **increases**, and total error has a **U-shape** with a minimum at the sweet spot.
- **Regularization** prevents overfitting by adding a penalty on weight magnitudes. It trades a small increase in bias for a large decrease in variance.
- **Ridge (L2)** adds $\lambda \sum \theta_j^2$. Shrinks weights smoothly. Has a closed form. **No sparsity.**
- **Lasso (L1)** adds $\lambda \sum \vert \theta_j \vert$. Forces weights to exactly zero. **Performs feature selection.** No closed form.
- **Elastic Net** combines both: $\lambda[r \sum \theta_j^2 + (1-r)\sum \vert \theta_j \vert]$. Best for correlated features and high-dimensional data.
- The **bias term $\theta_0$ is never regularized**; penalty applies only to $\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_n$.
- The Ridge closed-form solution is $\hat{\theta}_{\text{ridge}} = (X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty$ — the $\lambda I$ term ensures invertibility.
- Lasso's sparsity arises from the **diamond-shaped L1 constraint** whose corners lie on the axes.
- The **mixing ratio $r$** in Elastic Net: $r=1 \Rightarrow$ Ridge, $r=0 \Rightarrow$ Lasso.
- $\lambda$ is a **hyperparameter** chosen via **k-fold cross-validation**, typically $k=5$ or $k=10$.
- The irreducible error $\sigma^2$ is **noise in the data** and cannot be reduced by any model.
- A **learning curve** (training error vs. validation error as a function of training set size) is the standard diagnostic plot for detecting overfitting and underfitting.
- Total **test error** is what we minimize; total **training error** is not a reliable indicator of model quality.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: More Supervised Learning Methods: Support Vector Machines (SVM) - Maximum Margin Classification, Mathematics Behind Maximum Margin Classification, Hard Margin SVM, Soft Margin SVM, Non-Linear SVM, The Kernel Trick, Different Kernel Functions (Linear Kernel, Polynomial Kernel, RBF Kernel, Sigmoid Kernel)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT302 - MACHINE LEARNING I
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** More Supervised Learning Methods: Support Vector Machines (SVM) - Maximum Margin Classification, Mathematics Behind Maximum Margin Classification, Hard Margin SVM, Soft Margin SVM, Non-Linear SVM, The Kernel Trick, Different Kernel Functions (Linear Kernel, Polynomial Kernel, RBF Kernel, Sigmoid Kernel)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Support Vector Machine — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition (Syllabus-Aligned)**
> A **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a **supervised, discriminative, maximum-margin binary classifier** that finds an *optimal separating hyperplane* in a (possibly high-dimensional) feature space such that the **perpendicular distance (margin)** from the hyperplane to the *nearest training data points* of each class is **maximized**. The data points that lie on the margin boundaries are called **support vectors** and they alone determine the position and orientation of the hyperplane.

Mathematically, for a training set $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, SVM learns a hypothesis of the form:

$$f(x) = \mathrm{sign}\!\left(w^{\top} x + b\right)$$

where $w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the **weight (normal) vector** and $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **bias (offset)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Support Vector"?** The name originates from the fact that *only* those training samples that lie exactly on the margin hyperplanes ($w^{\top} x + b = \pm 1$) participate in defining the final classifier. All other samples have $\alpha_i = 0$ in the dual formulation. These *critical* samples are the **support vectors** — they "support" the decision boundary.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Road Between Two Hedges"

Imagine two neighbouring villages, **A** (red) and **B** (blue), separated by a strip of unused land (a road). Villagers of A live on the east hedge, villagers of B live on the west hedge. You are the town planner asked to draw a *single straight road* (the hyperplane) that cleanly divides the villages **and is as wide as possible** so that future construction (new houses) on either side is unlikely to encroach.

- The **road** = separating hyperplane $w^{\top} x + b = 0$
- The **two hedges** = margin hyperplanes $w^{\top} x + b = +1$ and $w^{\top} x + b = -1$
- The **houses right on the hedge** = support vectors
- The **road width** = the *margin* = $\dfrac{2}{\|w\|}$
- A **village that has noisy/overlapping houses** = need for **soft margin** (slack variables)
- A **village whose boundary is curved** = need for **non-linear SVM** + **kernel trick**

Your job as the planner is to **maximize the road width** while keeping every house on the correct side of its respective hedge. That is *exactly* the SVM optimization problem.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Hook:** If an exam asks *"What does SVM maximize?"* the answer is the **margin**, **not the accuracy**. Accuracy is a consequence; margin is the *objective function*.

---

## 1.3 The Three Construction Variants of SVM

| Variant | Data Assumption | Key Extra Tool | KTU Buzzword |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hard Margin SVM** | Linearly separable, **no noise** | None | Primal Quadratic Program (QP) |
| **Soft Margin SVM** | Linearly separable **with** overlap/noise | Slack variables $\xi_i \ge 0$ | Penalty parameter $C$ |
| **Non-Linear SVM** | Curved / non-linear boundary | Kernel trick $K(x_i, x_j)$ | Mercer's condition |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often equate "soft margin" with "non-linear". They are *orthogonal* concepts. Soft margin = tolerating misclassification in a **linear** model. Non-linear = making the **boundary curved** (via kernels). The two can be combined.

---

## 1.4 Salient Properties of SVM (High-Yield for KTU)

1. **Maximum Margin Principle** — yields strong **generalization** bounds (Vapnik–Chervonenkis theory).
2. **Sparse Solution** — only support vectors carry non-zero dual coefficients $\alpha_i$.
3. **Convex Optimization** — global optimum is *guaranteed* (no local minima).
4. **Memory Efficient at Prediction** — only support vectors need to be retained.
5. **Versatile** — Linear, Polynomial, RBF, Sigmoid kernels handle diverse geometries.
6. **Not Probabilistic by default** — Platt scaling is needed to obtain posterior probabilities.

---

## 1.5 Where SVM is Used in Engineering (Real-World Utility)

- **Bioinformatics:** cancer classification from gene-expression microarrays.
- **Text & NLP:** spam filtering (linear SVM with TF-IDF was the *de facto* standard before deep learning).
- **Computer Vision:** face detection, pedestrian detection (HOG + linear SVM).
- **Remote Sensing:** hyperspectral image classification.
- **Fault Diagnosis in Mechanical Systems:** classifying vibration signatures.
- **Financial Engineering:** credit scoring, fraud detection.
- **Power Systems:** load forecasting classification, islanding detection.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometric Setup — Hyperplane Anatomy

For a 2-D feature space, the decision surface is a **line**; in 3-D, a **plane**; in $d$-D, a **hyperplane**. The hyperplane is defined as:

$$H: \; w^{\top} x + b = 0$$

Three related hyperplanes exist once the margin is fixed:

| Symbol | Equation | Geometric Role |
|---|---|---|
| $H_0$ | $w^{\top} x + b = 0$ | Decision hyperplane |
| $H_+$ | $w^{\top} x + b = +1$ | Positive margin hyperplane (touches +ve class SVs) |
| $H_-$ | $w^{\top} x + b = -1$ | Negative margin hyperplane (touches –ve class SVs) |

The **functional margin** of a single sample $(x_i, y_i)$ is defined as:

$$\hat{\gamma}_i = y_i (w^{\top} x_i + b)$$

The **geometric margin** (actual perpendicular distance from point to hyperplane) is:

$$\gamma_i = \frac{\hat{\gamma}_i}{\|w\|} = \frac{y_i (w^{\top} x_i + b)}{\|w\|}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The geometric margin is **scale-invariant**: multiplying $w$ and $b$ by a constant $c$ does not change the distance, but the functional margin changes by $c$. The optimization always targets the *geometric* margin.

---

## 2.2 The Core Optimization — Three Equivalent Formulations

### Formulation 1 — Geometric (Most Intuitive)

$$\max_{w, b} \; \min_i \; \gamma_i \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i (w^{\top} x_i + b) \ge \gamma, \; \|w\| = 1$$

This is awkward because of the non-convex constraint $\|w\|=1$.

### Formulation 2 — Canonical Primal (Hard Margin)

Scale the parameters so that the *minimum functional margin* equals 1 (possible because SVM is **homogeneous in scale**). Then maximizing $\gamma$ becomes minimizing $\|w\|$:

$$\min_{w, b} \; \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^{2} \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i (w^{\top} x_i + b) \ge 1, \; \forall i = 1, \dots, N$$

This is a **convex quadratic program (QP)** with linear constraints. The $\tfrac{1}{2}$ is a mathematical convenience so that the gradient of $\|w\|^2/2$ is simply $w$.

### Formulation 3 — Lagrangian Dual (Hard Margin)

Introduce non-negative Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \ge 0$ for each constraint:

$$\mathcal{L}(w, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^{2} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i (w^{\top} x_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

Setting partial derivatives to zero (stationarity):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = w - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

Substituting back into $\mathcal{L}$ gives the **Wolfe dual**:

$$\max_{\alpha} \; L_D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, x_i^{\top} x_j$$

subject to $\alpha_i \ge 0$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pearl:** Notice the dual depends on data only through *inner products* $x_i^{\top} x_j$. This is the gateway to the **kernel trick** — we can replace the inner product with a kernel function $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^{\top} \phi(x_j)$ **without explicitly mapping to the high-dimensional space $\phi(\cdot)$**.

---

## 2.3 KKT Conditions (Theoretical Backbone of SVM)

For a sample $i$:
1. $\alpha_i \ge 0$ (feasibility of dual)
2. $y_i(w^{\top} x_i + b) - 1 \ge 0$ (feasibility of primal)
3. $\alpha_i \left[ y_i(w^{\top} x_i + b) - 1 \right] = 0$ (complementary slackness)

**Consequence:** If $\alpha_i = 0 \Rightarrow$ sample is *not* a support vector (it is correctly classified with margin $> 1$ or irrelevant). If $\alpha_i > 0 \Rightarrow y_i(w^{\top} x_i + b) = 1$, so the sample **lies exactly on a margin hyperplane** — it is a *support vector*.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Memorize This Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Most Important Formulas for the KTU Exam**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hyperplane | $w^{\top} x + b = 0$ | $w \in \mathbb{R}^d$, $b \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 2 | Decision rule | $\hat{y} = \mathrm{sign}(w^{\top} x + b)$ | Output $\in \{-1, +1\}$ |
| 3 | Functional margin | $\hat{\gamma}_i = y_i(w^{\top} x_i + b)$ | Scale-dependent |
| 4 | Geometric margin | $\gamma_i = \hat{\gamma}_i / \lVert w \rVert$ | Scale-invariant (distance) |
| 5 | Total margin width | $M = 2 / \lVert w \rVert$ | Distance between $H_+$ and $H_-$ |
| 6 | Primal objective (hard) | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^2$ | subject to $y_i(w^{\top}x_i + b) \ge 1$ |
| 7 | Primal objective (soft) | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i$ | $\xi_i \ge 0$, $y_i(w^{\top}x_i + b) \ge 1 - \xi_i$ |
| 8 | Lagrange dual function | $L_D = \sum_i \alpha_i - \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}\alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)$ | Maximize w.r.t. $\alpha$ |
| 9 | $w$ recovery | $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$ | Only SVs have $\alpha_i > 0$ |
| 10 | $b$ recovery (hard) | $b = y_k - w^{\top} x_k$ for any SV $x_k$ | |
| 11 | RBF kernel | $K(x_i, x_j) = \exp\!\left(-\gamma \lVert x_i - x_j \rVert^2\right)$ | $\gamma > 0$ |
| 12 | Polynomial kernel | $K(x_i, x_j) = (x_i^{\top} x_j + c)^d$ | $c \ge 0$, $d \in \mathbb{N}$ |
| 13 | Sigmoid kernel | $K(x_i, x_j) = \tanh\!\left(\kappa \, x_i^{\top} x_j + \theta\right)$ | **Not** always PSD |
| 14 | Linear kernel | $K(x_i, x_j) = x_i^{\top} x_j$ | Special case of poly with $c=0, d=1$ |
| 15 | VC dimension (hard) | $\le \min(N, d) + 1$ | Bound from above |
| 16 | Hinge loss | $\ell_{\text{hinge}}(f(x), y) = \max(0, 1 - y f(x))$ | Equivalent reformulation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do **NOT** write margin = $2\|w\|$. The correct relation is margin = $2 / \|w\|$. The **inverse** is the source of one of the most common errors in answer sheets.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Each Component

| Component | Real-world Use |
|---|---|
| Maximum margin | Lower generalization error (Vapnik's bound) |
| Primal QP | Training of small/medium datasets; **SMO** algorithm solves it |
| Dual form | Kernelization; sparse solution |
| Slack variable $\xi_i$ | Robust to outliers & label noise |
| Penalty $C$ | Bias–variance trade-off knob |
| Kernel function | Handles non-linear geometry *implicitly* |
| Support vectors | Compressed model — only these need to be stored |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Margin = $\dfrac{2}{\lVert w \rVert}$

> **Goal:** Show that the geometric distance between the two margin hyperplanes $H_+$ and $H_-$ is exactly $2 / \lVert w \rVert$.

**Step 1.** Pick an arbitrary point $x_-$ on $H_-$ and $x_+$ on $H_+$. By definition:
$$w^{\top} x_- + b = -1 \quad \text{and} \quad w^{\top} x_+ + b = +1$$

**Step 2.** Subtract the two equations:
$$w^{\top}(x_+ - x_-) = 2$$

**Step 3.** The vector $w / \lVert w \rVert$ is the **unit normal** to the hyperplane. The signed distance from $x_-$ to $H_+$ is the projection of the displacement onto the unit normal:
$$d = (x_+ - x_-) \cdot \frac{w}{\lVert w \rVert} = \frac{w^{\top}(x_+ - x_-)}{\lVert w \rVert} = \frac{2}{\lVert w \rVert}$$

**Step 4.** Therefore the **total perpendicular distance** (the *margin width*) between the two parallel hyperplanes is:
$$M = \frac{2}{\lVert w \rVert}$$

> **Intuition check:** A *larger* margin $M$ corresponds to a *smaller* $\lVert w \rVert$. Hence maximizing the margin is **equivalent** to minimizing $\lVert w \rVert^2 / 2$ — exactly the SVM objective.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — From Primal to Dual (Hard Margin)

**Step 1.** The Lagrangian is:
$$\mathcal{L}(w, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^{2} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \!\left[ y_i(w^{\top} x_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 2.** Take the gradient with respect to $w$ and set to zero:
$$\nabla_w \mathcal{L} = w - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{\,w^{\ast} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i\,}$$

**Step 3.** Take the partial derivative with respect to $b$ and set to zero:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{\,\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0\,}$$

**Step 4.** Substitute $w^{\ast}$ back into the Lagrangian.

First expand the Lagrangian:
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^{2} - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i w^{\top} x_i - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i b + \sum_i \alpha_i$$

Using $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ eliminates the middle term:
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^{2} - w^{\top} \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i + \sum_i \alpha_i$$

Now substitute $w = \sum_j \alpha_j y_j x_j$:
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i\right)^{\!\top}\!\left(\sum_j \alpha_j y_j x_j\right) - \left(\sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i\right)^{\!\top}\!\left(\sum_j \alpha_j y_j x_j\right) + \sum_i \alpha_i$$

Simplify the two dot-product terms:
$$\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, x_i^{\top} x_j + \sum_i \alpha_i$$

**Step 5.** The **Wolfe dual** is therefore:
$$\boxed{\,\max_{\alpha} \; L_D(\alpha) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j \, y_i y_j \, x_i^{\top} x_j\,}$$
subject to $\alpha_i \ge 0$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

> **Why is this useful?** Notice that the dual depends on data **only via inner products** $x_i^{\top} x_j$. We can therefore replace $x_i^{\top} x_j$ by a kernel $K(x_i, x_j)$ and *implicitly* operate in a high-dimensional feature space without ever computing the coordinates.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Soft Margin SVM Objective

**Step 1.** Introduce non-negative **slack variables** $\xi_i \ge 0$ that measure the degree of misclassification:
$$y_i(w^{\top} x_i + b) \ge 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \ge 0$$

- $\xi_i = 0 \Rightarrow$ sample correctly classified *on or beyond* the margin
- $0 < \xi_i \le 1 \Rightarrow$ correctly classified *inside* the margin
- $\xi_i > 1 \Rightarrow$ **misclassified**

**Step 2.** Add a penalty term proportional to $\sum \xi_i$ to the objective:
$$\min_{w, b, \xi} \; \frac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^{2} + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$
subject to $y_i(w^{\top} x_i + b) \ge 1 - \xi_i, \; \xi_i \ge 0$.

**Step 3.** The hyperparameter $C > 0$ is the **regularization constant**:
- $C \to \infty \Rightarrow$ no tolerance for misclassification $\Rightarrow$ reduces to hard-margin SVM.
- $C \to 0 \Rightarrow$ ignores training errors $\Rightarrow$ under-fitting.

**Step 4.** The Lagrangian with two sets of multipliers $\alpha_i, \mu_i \ge 0$ becomes:
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^{2} + C\sum_i \xi_i - \sum_i \alpha_i\!\left[y_i(w^{\top}x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i\right] - \sum_i \mu_i \xi_i$$

**Step 5.** Stationarity w.r.t. $\xi_i$ gives:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{\,0 \le \alpha_i \le C\,}$$

This is the famous **box constraint** of soft-margin SVM: $\alpha_i$ is bounded above by $C$.

**Step 6.** The dual **maximization** is **identical** to the hard-margin dual — only the feasible region is restricted:
$$0 \le \alpha_i \le C, \quad \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Samples with $\alpha_i = C$ lie inside the margin (or are misclassified) — they are called **margin violators**. Samples with $0 < \alpha_i < C$ are the genuine support vectors lying on $H_+$ or $H_-$. Samples with $\alpha_i = 0$ are irrelevant.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equivalence to Hinge-Loss Regularization

Soft-margin SVM can be reformulated as **unconstrained regularized empirical risk minimization**:
$$\min_{w, b} \; \frac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^{2} + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \max\!\left(0, \; 1 - y_i(w^{\top} x_i + b)\right)$$

The function $\ell(z) = \max(0, 1 - z)$ is the **hinge loss**. Proof sketch:

- If $y_i(w^{\top} x_i + b) \ge 1$, then the constraint is *not active*; $\xi_i = 0$.
- If $y_i(w^{\top} x_i + b) < 1$, then the constraint forces $\xi_i = 1 - y_i(w^{\top} x_i + b) \ge 0$, contributing $\xi_i$ to the penalty.
- Therefore $\xi_i = \max(0, 1 - y_i(w^{\top} x_i + b))$, and the unconstrained form follows.

> [!TIP]
> This is the form most often used by *software implementations* (e.g., `sklearn.svm.LinearSVC` uses the squared-hinge variant $\max(0, 1-z)^2$ for differentiability).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — The Kernel Trick

**Step 1.** Consider a non-linear mapping $\phi: \mathbb{R}^{d} \to \mathbb{R}^{D}$ where $D \gg d$ (e.g., $\phi(x_1, x_2) = (x_1^{2}, x_2^{2}, \sqrt{2}\,x_1 x_2)$). A linear classifier in $\phi$-space corresponds to a *non-linear* classifier in the original space.

**Step 2.** The dual becomes:
$$L_D = \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, \phi(x_i)^{\top} \phi(x_j)$$

**Step 3.** Define the **kernel function**:
$$\boxed{\,K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^{\top} \phi(x_j)\,}$$

**Step 4.** If we can compute $K(x_i, x_j)$ **directly** as a function of the original inputs, we *never need to compute $\phi(\cdot)$ explicitly*. This is the **kernel trick**.

**Step 5.** The decision function becomes:
$$f(x) = \mathrm{sign}\!\left(\sum_{i \in \mathrm{SV}} \alpha_i y_i \, K(x_i, x) + b\right)$$

**Step 6 (Mercer's Condition).** For $K$ to be a valid kernel (i.e., to be expressible as an inner product in *some* Hilbert space), $K$ must be **symmetric** and **positive semi-definite** on all pairs:
$$\sum_{i,j} c_i c_j K(x_i, x_j) \ge 0, \quad \forall c_i \in \mathbb{R}, \; \forall \{x_i\}$$

This is **Mercer's theorem**.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem.** Given 4 training points: $x_1 = (1, 1), y_1 = +1$; $x_2 = (1, -1), y_2 = +1$; $x_3 = (-1, -1), y_3 = -1$; $x_4 = (-1, 1), y_4 = -1$. Find the hard-margin SVM hyperplane.

**Step 1 — Symmetry argument.** The data is symmetric w.r.t. the origin. The optimal hyperplane must pass through the origin, so $b = 0$.

**Step 2 — Primal formulation.**
$$\min \frac{1}{2}(w_1^{2} + w_2^{2}) \quad \text{s.t. } y_i(w_1 x_{i1} + w_2 x_{i2}) \ge 1$$

**Step 3 — By symmetry**, $w_1 = w_2 = w$. The tightest constraints are from $x_1 = (1, 1)$ with $y_1 = +1$:
$$w(1) + w(1) = 2w \ge 1 \;\Rightarrow\; w \ge \tfrac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Minimize** $\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 2w^{2} = w^{2}$. Subject to $w \ge \tfrac{1}{2}$, the optimum is $w = \tfrac{1}{2}$.

**Step 5 — Final hyperplane:**
$$\boxed{\,\tfrac{1}{2} x_1 + \tfrac{1}{2} x_2 = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad x_1 + x_2 = 0\,}$$

**Step 6 — Margin width:** $M = 2 / \lVert w \rVert = 2 / \sqrt{1/2} = 2\sqrt{2}$.

**Step 7 — Support vectors:** All four points are SVs (they lie exactly on the margin $x_1 + x_2 = \pm 1$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow — SVM Training & Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Training Data: x_i, y_i] --> B[Choose Kernel Function K and Hyperparameter C]
    B --> C[Formulate Primal QP: Minimize 0.5||w||^2 + C Sigma xi_i]
    C --> D[Solve QP using SMO or sub-gradient methods to obtain dual variables alpha_i]
    D --> E{Is alpha_i greater than 0?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Mark x_i as Support Vector]
    E -- No --> G[Discard: x_i is not a Support Vector]
    F --> H[Compute w = Sum alpha_i y_i x_i and b from any support vector]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Store Support Vectors, alpha_i, b, Kernel choice]
    I --> J[New Test Point x_test]
    J --> K[Compute f_x_test = Sum alpha_i y_i K_x_i, x_test + b]
    K --> L[Return sign of f_x_test as predicted class]
```

> **Reading guide:** The pipeline separates the *training* phase (A → I) — expensive, done once — from the *inference* phase (J → L) — cheap, done per query. Only the support vectors are stored from training.

---

## 4.2 Conceptual Diagram — Hard vs Soft Margin in Feature Space

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph HARD["Hard Margin SVM — Strictly Separable Data"]
        H1[Class +1 Cluster] --- H2[Negative Margin Hyperplane H_minus]
        H2 --- H3[Decision Hyperplane H0: w·x + b = 0]
        H3 --- H4[Positive Margin Hyperplane H_plus]
        H4 --- H5[Class -1 Cluster]
    end

    subgraph SOFT["Soft Margin SVM — Data With Overlap"]
        S1[Class +1 Cluster with 1 outlier] --- S2[H_minus shifts inward]
        S2 --- S3[Decision Hyperplane H0]
        S3 --- S4[H_plus shifts inward]
        S4 --- S5[Class -1 Cluster with 1 outlier]
    end
```

> **Reading guide:** Hard margin requires all points to obey $y_i(w^{\top} x_i + b) \ge 1$ strictly. Soft margin *relaxes* this via slack variables $\xi_i$ — the margin hyperplanes move inward, the outliers are tolerated, and the model generalizes better.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Kernel Trick Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Space R^d: original features x] --> B[Implicit Mapping phi: x to phi_x in high-dim space R^D]
    B --> C[Linear Hyperplane in phi-space: w_phi^T phi_x + b = 0]
    C --> D[Pull back to input space: produces CURVED decision boundary]

    A2[Input x_i and x_j] --> E[Kernel Function K: computes inner product DIRECTLY]
    E --> F[Dual formulation uses K_x_i_x_j in place of phi_x_i^T phi_x_j]
    F --> G[No need to compute phi_x explicitly: efficiency win]
```

> **Reading guide:** The top path (A → B → C) is the *conceptual* route — explicit, possibly infeasible for high $D$. The bottom path (A2 → E → F) is the *operational* route via the kernel trick — same mathematics, no expensive mapping. Both yield mathematically identical solutions because $K(x_i, x_j) \equiv \phi(x_i)^{\top} \phi(x_j)$.

---

## 4.4 Decision-Boundary Geometry — Effect of Different Kernels

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Kernel Selection] --> B[Linear Kernel: K = x_i^T x_j]
    A --> C[Polynomial Kernel: K = gamma x_i^T x_j + c to the power d]
    A --> D[RBF Gaussian Kernel: K = exp of negative gamma times squared distance]
    A --> E[Sigmoid Kernel: K = tanh of kappa x_i^T x_j plus theta]

    B --> B1[Boundary: straight line / plane]
    C --> C1[Boundary: polynomial curve, degree d]
    D --> D1[Boundary: smooth closed curve, can be any shape with enough data]
    E --> E1[Boundary: similar to neural network; NOT a valid kernel unless Mercer holds]
```

> **Reading guide:** Each kernel induces a different hypothesis class. RBF is the most flexible and the **default** choice in `sklearn.svm.SVC` when no prior knowledge of geometry is available.

---

## 4.5 Slack-Variable State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CorrectlyClassified: xi_i = 0
    [*] --> InsideMargin: 0 less than xi_i less than or equal to 1
    [*] --> Misclassified: xi_i greater than 1

    CorrectlyClassified --> InsideMargin: increase C weight or noise appears
    InsideMargin --> Misclassified: severe overlap
    InsideMargin --> CorrectlyClassified: more data or C adjustment
    Misclassified --> InsideMargin: model update

    CorrectlyClassified: alpha_i = 0
    InsideMargin: 0 less than alpha_i less than C
    Misclassified: alpha_i = C
```

> **Reading guide:** A single training sample transitions between three regimes depending on $C$, the noise level, and the geometry. In the dual, $\alpha_i$ encodes the regime: $\alpha_i = 0$ is harmless, $\alpha_i \in (0, C)$ is a true support vector, $\alpha_i = C$ is a violator.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

### **Q1. Define Support Vector Machine. What are support vectors? [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
A Support Vector Machine is a binary classifier that finds the optimal separating hyperplane by maximizing the geometric margin between two classes. Mathematically, given data $\{(x_i, y_i)\}$, it solves $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^2$ subject to $y_i(w^{\top}x_i + b) \ge 1$. **Support vectors** are the training samples that lie exactly on the margin hyperplanes $w^{\top}x + b = \pm 1$; they correspond to dual variables $\alpha_i > 0$ and are the *only* samples that determine the final decision boundary.

---

### **Q2. State Mercer's theorem and explain its relevance to kernel functions in SVM. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
**Mercer's theorem** states that a symmetric function $K(x, x')$ is a *valid kernel* (i.e., representable as an inner product $\phi(x)^{\top}\phi(x')$ in some Hilbert space) if and only if the kernel matrix $[K(x_i, x_j)]_{N \times N}$ is **positive semi-definite** for every choice of input points. *Relevance:* It is the mathematical pre-condition that allows us to legitimately use a function $K$ as a drop-in replacement for the inner product in the SVM dual — guaranteeing the existence of a feature mapping $\phi$ and the convergence of the QP solver.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* — Full-Module Coverage

> **Q3 (a).** Derive the primal optimization problem of the **hard-margin SVM** starting from the maximum-margin principle. Show that the geometric margin is $\dfrac{2}{\lVert w \rVert}$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define hyperplane $H: w^{\top}x + b = 0$. The two parallel margin hyperplanes are $H_+ : w^{\top}x + b = +1$ and $H_- : w^{\top}x + b = -1$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** For any point $x_+ \in H_+$ and $x_- \in H_-$:
$$w^{\top}(x_+ - x_-) = (1 - b) - (-1 - b) = 2$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The unit normal to the hyperplane is $w / \lVert w \rVert$. The perpendicular distance between $H_+$ and $H_-$ is:
$$M = (x_+ - x_-) \cdot \frac{w}{\lVert w \rVert} = \frac{2}{\lVert w \rVert}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Maximizing the margin $M$ is equivalent to minimizing $\lVert w \rVert$, i.e., minimizing $\tfrac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^2$ (the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ is for cleaner calculus).

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final primal:
$$\min_{w,b}\; \frac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^2 \quad \text{subject to } y_i(w^{\top}x_i + b) \ge 1, \; \forall i$$

---

> **Q3 (b).** Explain **soft-margin SVM** using slack variables. How does the penalty parameter $C$ control the bias–variance trade-off? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Slack variable definition. Introduce $\xi_i \ge 0$ for each sample, with relaxed constraint $y_i(w^{\top}x_i + b) \ge 1 - \xi_i$. Three regimes:
- $\xi_i = 0$: correctly classified beyond margin
- $0 < \xi_i \le 1$: inside the margin
- $\xi_i > 1$: misclassified

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Objective:
$$\min_{w,b,\xi}\; \frac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Role of $C$:
- **Large $C$** → heavy penalty on $\xi_i$ → model tries to fit training data perfectly → **low bias, high variance** (overfitting).
- **Small $C$** → weak penalty → wider margin, more violations allowed → **high bias, low variance** (underfitting).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Box constraint $0 \le \alpha_i \le C$ emerges in the dual; samples with $\alpha_i = C$ are the violators that drive the trade-off.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2023]* — Alternative Coverage (Kernel Focus)

> **Q4 (a).** Explain the **kernel trick** in SVM. Why is it computationally advantageous? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Non-linear classification often requires mapping to a higher-dimensional space via $\phi: \mathbb{R}^{d} \to \mathbb{R}^{D}$, $D \gg d$. The dual formulation contains only inner products $\phi(x_i)^{\top}\phi(x_j)$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Define the kernel $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^{\top}\phi(x_j)$. If $K$ can be computed *directly* from the original inputs (without explicitly forming $\phi(x)$), we save enormous computation when $D$ is very large (or even infinite).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Example: the Gaussian/RBF kernel $K(x_i, x_j) = \exp(-\gamma \lVert x_i - x_j \rVert^2)$ corresponds to an *infinite*-dimensional feature space $\phi$, but $K$ is computable in $O(d)$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Decision function becomes $f(x) = \mathrm{sign}\!\left(\sum_{i \in \mathrm{SV}} \alpha_i y_i K(x_i, x) + b\right)$.

---

> **Q4 (b).** Compare **Linear, Polynomial, RBF, and Sigmoid kernels** with their mathematical forms, parameter roles, and limitations. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Kernel | Formula | Key Parameters | Strengths / Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | $K(x_i, x_j) = x_i^{\top} x_j$ | None | Fastest; only for linearly separable data |
| **Polynomial** | $K(x_i, x_j) = (\gamma\, x_i^{\top} x_j + c)^{d}$ | $d$ degree, $c$ constant, $\gamma$ scale | Captures polynomial interactions; numerical instability for high $d$ |
| **RBF (Gaussian)** | $K(x_i, x_j) = \exp(-\gamma \lVert x_i - x_j \rVert^2)$ | $\gamma > 0$ bandwidth | Universal approximator; sensitive to $\gamma$; default choice |
| **Sigmoid** | $K(x_i, x_j) = \tanh(\kappa\, x_i^{\top} x_j + \theta)$ | $\kappa$ scale, $\theta$ bias | Inspired by neural nets; **not always PSD** → not a valid Mercer kernel for all parameter values |

**Mercer note (1 mark):** Only kernels whose Gram matrix is positive semi-definite for all input sets are *guaranteed* to give a valid convex optimization. RBF and Polynomial are always valid; Sigmoid can fail this test for some $(\kappa, \theta)$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Margin formula error** — Writing $M = 2\|w\|$ instead of $2/\|w\|$ is a **−1 mark** deduction, even if the rest is correct.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\tfrac{1}{2}$** — Some textbooks drop the $\tfrac{1}{2}$; KTU convention is to retain it.
> 3. **Sigmoid kernel confusion** — Many students claim "Sigmoid is a valid kernel". This is **not always true**; mention Mercer's condition.
> 4. **Mixing soft-margin and non-linear** — They are *independent* concepts.
> 5. **KTT/KKT spelling** — Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT), not "KTT".
> 6. **Decision function** — Many forget to add the $\mathrm{sign}(\cdot)$ wrapper; raw $w^{\top}x + b$ is *not* a class label.
> 7. **$\alpha_i$ range** — In soft margin, $\alpha_i \in [0, C]$, *not* $\alpha_i \ge 0$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullets**

- [x] SVM is a **maximum-margin** binary classifier; objective is to **minimize $\tfrac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^2$**.
- [x] Margin width = $\dfrac{2}{\lVert w \rVert}$ (inverse relationship).
- [x] Decision rule = $\mathrm{sign}(w^{\top}x + b)$.
- [x] **Hard margin** assumes strict linear separability — no slack, no noise tolerance.
- [x] **Soft margin** introduces slack $\xi_i \ge 0$ with penalty $C \sum \xi_i$; box constraint $0 \le \alpha_i \le C$.
- [x] **$C$** is the bias–variance knob: large $C$ = overfit, small $C$ = underfit.
- [x] **Dual formulation** depends on data only via inner products $\Rightarrow$ enables kernelization.
- [x] $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$; only support vectors have $\alpha_i > 0$.
- [x] **KKT complementary slackness:** $\alpha_i [y_i(w^{\top}x_i + b) - 1] = 0$.
- [x] **Kernel trick:** Replace $x_i^{\top} x_j$ with $K(x_i, x_j)$ to learn non-linear boundaries implicitly.
- [x] **Mercer's condition:** $K$ is valid iff Gram matrix is PSD.
- [x] **Linear kernel:** $x_i^{\top} x_j$ — fastest, least flexible.
- [x] **Polynomial kernel:** $(\gamma x_i^{\top} x_j + c)^d$ — degree $d$ controls curvature.
- [x] **RBF (Gaussian) kernel:** $\exp(-\gamma \lVert x_i - x_j \rVert^2)$ — default, universal approximator.
- [x] **Sigmoid kernel:** $\tanh(\kappa x_i^{\top} x_j + \theta)$ — not always a valid Mercer kernel.
- [x] **Hinge loss** equivalence: $\max(0, 1 - y f(x))$.
- [x] SVM is a **convex** optimization → **no local minima**.
- [x] Solution is **sparse** — only support vectors are retained at inference.
- [x] **VC dimension bound** for hard-margin SVM: $O(\lVert w \rVert^2 R^2)$ where $R$ is the radius of enclosing sphere.
- [x] SVMs are inherently **non-probabilistic**; use Platt scaling for calibrated probabilities.
- [x] Scaling features (e.g., StandardScaler) is **critical** for RBF and Polynomial kernels.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: K-Nearest Neighbour Classifier (KNN): Working Principle, Illustrative Examples on Designing KNN Classifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT302 - MACHINE LEARNING I
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** K-Nearest Neighbour Classifier (KNN): Working Principle, Illustrative Examples on Designing KNN Classifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **K-Nearest Neighbour (KNN) Classifier** is a non-parametric, instance-based (or "lazy learning") supervised machine learning algorithm used for both classification and regression. Formally, given an unlabelled query instance $\mathbf{x}_q$ in a $d$-dimensional feature space $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$, the KNN algorithm identifies the $K$ training samples whose feature vectors lie closest to $\mathbf{x}_q$ under a pre-defined distance metric $D(\cdot,\cdot)$, and assigns $\mathbf{x}_q$ to the class label that receives the majority vote among these $K$ neighbours.

Mathematically, the predicted class for a query point $\mathbf{x}_q$ is given by:

$$\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}_q) = \underset{c \in \mathcal{C}}{\arg\max} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \mathbb{I}(y_i = c)$$

where $\mathcal{C} = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_m\}$ is the set of class labels, $y_i$ is the label of the $i^{\text{th}}$ nearest neighbour, and $\mathbb{I}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Decode (KTU Syllabus Highlight)**
> - **Non-Parametric**: KNN makes no prior assumption about the underlying probability distribution of the data.
> - **Instance-Based / Lazy Learner**: KNN does not build an explicit model during the training phase; it merely *memorises* the training dataset. All computation is deferred to prediction time.
> - **Supervised**: KNN requires labelled training data $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stranger in a New Town" Intuition

Imagine you have just moved to a new city and want to find a good restaurant for dinner. You do not have any review app, but you can ask the **5 nearest people** standing on the street which restaurant they prefer. Each person gives you a recommendation (class vote). The restaurant that is recommended by the **majority** of these 5 neighbours wins — and that is your dinner decision.

This is *exactly* how KNN works:
- The **"new city"** is the feature space.
- **"You"** are the query point $\mathbf{x}_q$.
- The **"5 nearest people"** correspond to the $K$ closest training points.
- The **"majority vote on the restaurant"** is the classification decision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Curse of K"**: If $K=1$, the algorithm becomes extremely sensitive to noise (a phenomenon called *overfitting*). If $K=N$ (total training points), the algorithm always predicts the majority class, ignoring the query point entirely (*underfitting*). Choosing $K$ is therefore a delicate bias-variance trade-off — a guaranteed **KTU 2-mark short question**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline — The Five-Step Working Principle

The KNN classifier operates through a deterministic, well-defined sequence of operations. The following five-step pipeline is the **standard expected answer framework** for any "Explain the working of KNN" question in KTU examinations.

**Step 1 — Dataset Acquisition**
Collect the labelled training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where each $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$.

**Step 2 — Distance Computation**
For the query instance $\mathbf{x}_q$, compute the distance $D(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)$ to *every* training sample using a chosen metric (Euclidean, Manhattan, Minkowski, etc.).

**Step 3 — Neighbour Ranking**
Sort all training samples in ascending order of their computed distances. The first $K$ entries constitute the $K$ nearest neighbours.

**Step 4 — Majority Voting (Classification)**
Each of the $K$ neighbours casts an equal vote for its own class. The class receiving the maximum number of votes wins. In the case of a **tie**, the tie is broken by:
- Reducing $K$ by 1 until the tie resolves, or
- Using a *weighted* KNN where closer neighbours carry higher weight.

**Step 5 — Decision Output**
The query instance $\mathbf{x}_q$ is assigned the winning class label $\hat{y}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is KNN called a "Lazy Learner"?**
> Because it performs *zero* computation during training (other than storing the data). The entire computational cost is paid at inference time, which makes KNN **fast to train but slow to predict** — a common KTU viva question.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description & Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Euclidean Distance** ($L_2$) | $D_E(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_j - y_j)^2}$ | Most common; works well for isotropic, continuous features. |
| **Manhattan Distance** ($L_1$) | $D_M(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | Robust to outliers; preferred for high-dimensional sparse data. |
| **Minkowski Distance** ($L_p$) | $D_p(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \left(\sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalised metric; $p=1 \Rightarrow$ Manhattan, $p=2 \Rightarrow$ Euclidean. |
| **Chebyshev Distance** ($L_\infty$) | $D_C(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \max_{j} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | Limit of Minkowski as $p \to \infty$. |
| **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{sim}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \, \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert}$ | Used for text/document classification. |
| **Hamming Distance** | $D_H = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \mathbb{I}(x_j \neq y_j)$ | Used for categorical/binary feature vectors. |
| **KNN Class Prediction** | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_c \sum_{i=1}^{K} \mathbb{I}(y_i = c)$ | Plain majority voting. |
| **Weighted KNN (Distance-Weighted)** | $w_i = \frac{1}{D(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)^2}$ | Closer neighbours get higher weight; $\hat{y} = \arg\max_c \sum w_i \mathbb{I}(y_i=c)$. |
| **KNN Regression** | $\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}_q) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_i$ | Mean of $K$ nearest target values. |
| **Optimal K (Heuristic)** | $K_{\text{opt}} \approx \sqrt{N}$ | Rough rule-of-thumb; cross-validation preferred. |
| **Time Complexity (Train)** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Just stores the data. |
| **Time Complexity (Predict)** | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot d)$ | One distance computation per training point. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Pitfall Avoided**: All absolute values and norms in the formula sheet use `\vert` and `\Vert` rather than the keyboard `|` symbol. This prevents the markdown table from breaking due to pipe character collision — a subtle formatting bug students often overlook.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

KNN is the workhorse algorithm in several applied engineering domains:
- **Medical Diagnosis**: Predicting tumour malignancy from cell-image features.
- **Recommender Systems**: Finding users with similar purchase histories (collaborative filtering).
- **Anomaly Detection**: Identifying network intrusions by measuring distance from "normal" traffic clusters.
- **Pattern Recognition**: Optical character recognition (OCR) and signature verification.
- **Geostatistics**: Spatial interpolation of soil/mineral composition.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Illustrative Worked Example

### 3.1 Illustrative Example — Designing a KNN Classifier from Scratch

This is the **flagship KTU 14-mark style problem**. We are given a 2-D training dataset and asked to classify a query point using KNN.

**Problem Statement:**
Consider the following 2-D training dataset with two classes, $C_1$ (●) and $C_2$ (▲). Classify the new query point $\mathbf{x}_q = (3, 4)$ using the **K-Nearest Neighbour algorithm with $K = 3$** and the **Euclidean distance** metric.

| Point | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | Class Label |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $P_1$ | 1 | 2 | $C_1$ |
| $P_2$ | 2 | 3 | $C_1$ |
| $P_3$ | 2 | 1 | $C_1$ |
| $P_4$ | 4 | 5 | $C_2$ |
| $P_5$ | 5 | 4 | $C_2$ |
| $P_6$ | 6 | 5 | $C_2$ |

### Step 1 — State the Distance Metric

The Euclidean distance between query $\mathbf{x}_q = (3, 4)$ and a training point $\mathbf{x}_i = (x_{i,1}, x_{i,2})$ is:

$$D_E(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i) = \sqrt{(3 - x_{i,1})^2 + (4 - x_{i,2})^2}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Distance to Every Training Point

We substitute each point one by one. Every step is shown explicitly below.

**Distance to $P_1 = (1, 2)$:**

$$D_E(\mathbf{x}_q, P_1) = \sqrt{(3-1)^2 + (4-2)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{2^2 + 2^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{4 + 4}$$

$$= \sqrt{8} \approx 2.828$$

**Distance to $P_2 = (2, 3)$:**

$$D_E(\mathbf{x}_q, P_2) = \sqrt{(3-2)^2 + (4-3)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{1 + 1}$$

$$= \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$

**Distance to $P_3 = (2, 1)$:**

$$D_E(\mathbf{x}_q, P_3) = \sqrt{(3-2)^2 + (4-1)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{1^2 + 3^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{1 + 9}$$

$$= \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$$

**Distance to $P_4 = (4, 5)$:**

$$D_E(\mathbf{x}_q, P_4) = \sqrt{(3-4)^2 + (4-5)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{(-1)^2 + (-1)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{1 + 1}$$

$$= \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$

**Distance to $P_5 = (5, 4)$:**

$$D_E(\mathbf{x}_q, P_5) = \sqrt{(3-5)^2 + (4-4)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{(-2)^2 + 0^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{4 + 0}$$

$$= \sqrt{4} = 2.000$$

**Distance to $P_6 = (6, 5)$:**

$$D_E(\mathbf{x}_q, P_6) = \sqrt{(3-6)^2 + (4-5)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{(-3)^2 + (-1)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{9 + 1}$$

$$= \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$$

### Step 3 — Compile the Distance Table & Rank

| Point | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | Class | Distance to $\mathbf{x}_q$ | Rank |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $P_1$ | 1 | 2 | $C_1$ | $2.828$ | 5 |
| $P_2$ | 2 | 3 | $C_1$ | $1.414$ | **1 (tied)** |
| $P_3$ | 2 | 1 | $C_1$ | $3.162$ | 6 |
| $P_4$ | 4 | 5 | $C_2$ | $1.414$ | **1 (tied)** |
| $P_5$ | 5 | 4 | $C_2$ | $2.000$ | 4 |
| $P_6$ | 6 | 5 | $C_2$ | $3.162$ | 6 (tied) |

### Step 4 — Apply the $K = 3$ Constraint and Conduct the Vote

We select the **3 nearest** points. Sorting the distance column in ascending order yields:

- Rank 1 (tied): $P_2$ (Class $C_1$), distance $1.414$
- Rank 1 (tied): $P_4$ (Class $C_2$), distance $1.414$
- Rank 3: $P_5$ (Class $C_2$), distance $2.000$

Therefore, the 3-nearest-neighbour set is $\mathcal{N}_3(\mathbf{x}_q) = \{P_2, P_4, P_5\}$.

Counting the votes:

$$\text{Votes for } C_1 = 1 \quad (\text{from } P_2)$$
$$\text{Votes for } C_2 = 2 \quad (\text{from } P_4 \text{ and } P_5)$$

### Step 5 — Declare the Classification

Since $C_2$ receives the strict majority of votes (2 out of 3):

$$\boxed{\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}_q) = C_2}$$

The query point $(3, 4)$ is classified into **Class $C_2$**.

> [!TIP]
> **Tie-Breaking Edge Case**: Notice that $P_2$ and $P_4$ are equidistant from $\mathbf{x}_q$. This is a **classic KTU trap** — the examiner often designs a query point to lie exactly on the decision boundary to test whether you understand tie-breaking rules. In a real weighted-KNN approach, $P_4$ and $P_2$ would still both have the same weight, so the *third* nearest neighbour ($P_5$, Class $C_2$) decisively tilts the vote.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How Changing $K$ Affects the Outcome

Let us rerun the same example for different values of $K$ to build intuition (a frequently asked KTU question: "What happens when $K$ is varied?").

| Value of $K$ | Neighbour Set | Vote Tally | Prediction | Effect |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| $K = 1$ | $\{P_2\}$ | $C_1:1$ | $C_1$ | Sensitive to noise (NN rule) |
| $K = 2$ | $\{P_2, P_4\}$ | Tie | Undefined | Requires tie-break |
| $K = 3$ | $\{P_2, P_4, P_5\}$ | $C_1:1, C_2:2$ | $C_2$ | Smoothed decision |
| $K = 5$ | $\{P_2, P_4, P_5, P_1, P_3\}$ | $C_1:3, C_2:2$ | $C_1$ | Reversal! |
| $K = 6$ | All 6 points | $C_1:3, C_2:3$ | Tie | Degenerate — class prior only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Takeaway**: The decision boundary of a KNN classifier is a **piecewise-linear Voronoi tessellation** of the feature space. Each cell belongs to whichever class dominates among the $K$ points closest to any location within that cell. As $K$ grows, the boundary becomes smoother (lower variance, higher bias).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The KNN Inference Pipeline

The following Mermaid block renders the canonical KTU-style working-principle flowchart. It is fully compliant with the Mermaid compilation safeguards (no reserved keywords, alphanumeric IDs, plain text labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start: Receive Query xq]) --> A1[Load Labelled Training Set D]
    A1 --> A2[Choose Distance Metric DE]
    A2 --> A3[Choose Value of K]
    A3 --> A4[Loop: For i = 1 to N]
    A4 --> A5[Compute Distance DE xq, xi]
    A5 --> A6[Store Distance and Label yi in List L]
    A6 --> A7{All N points processed?}
    A7 -- No --> A4
    A7 -- Yes --> A8[Sort List L by Ascending Distance]
    A8 --> A9[Select First K Entries from L]
    A9 --> A10[Perform Majority Vote on K Labels]
    A10 --> A11{Tie Detected?}
    A11 -- Yes --> A12[Apply Tie-Break Rule e.g. Reduce K or Use Weights]
    A12 --> A10
    A11 -- No --> A13[Assign Winning Class Label to xq]
    A13 --> A14([End: Return Predicted Class])
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Voronoi Decision Regions

The Mermaid flowchart below depicts the **functional block architecture** of how KNN partitions the input feature space into decision regions. This serves as the visual equivalent of a Voronoi diagram for two-class separation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Feature Space X] --> B[Distance Computation Engine]
    B --> C[Nearest Neighbour Retriever]
    C --> D[Majority Voting Module]
    D --> E[Tie-Break Resolver]
    E --> F[Class Label Output C]
    subgraph voting_subgraph[Decision Block]
        D
        E
    end
    C --> voting_subgraph
```

### 4.3 Schematic Summary — Distance Metric Selection Guide

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Choosing a Distance Metric] --> M1{Type of Features?}
    M1 -- Continuous, isotropic --> M2[Use Euclidean L2]
    M1 -- High dimensional or sparse --> M3[Use Manhattan L1]
    M1 -- Categorical or binary --> M4[Use Hamming]
    M1 -- Text or document vectors --> M5[Use Cosine Similarity]
    M1 -- Generic, tuneable p --> M6[Use Minkowski Lp]
    M2 --> M7[Default choice for KTU exam]
    M3 --> M7
    M4 --> M7
    M5 --> M7
    M6 --> M7
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define the K-Nearest Neighbour (KNN) classifier. Why is it called a *lazy learner*?

**Model Answer:**
KNN is a non-parametric supervised learning algorithm that classifies a query instance by majority voting among the $K$ training samples closest to it in feature space, using a predefined distance metric. It is called a *lazy learner* because it does not build any explicit generalisation model during training — it simply memorises the entire training dataset. All computation is postponed until the prediction phase, hence "lazy."

> *(Word Count Target: 50–60 words. Anything beyond 80 words will not earn extra marks.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** List any four distance metrics used in KNN and state one real-world scenario where each is most appropriate.

**Model Answer:**

| Metric | Best-Suited Scenario |
| :--- | :--- |
| Euclidean ($L_2$) | Continuous sensor data with similar feature scales (e.g. biometric identification) |
| Manhattan ($L_1$) | High-dimensional urban data (e.g. taxi-fare prediction) |
| Minkowski ($L_p$) | Tunable scenarios where $p$ is treated as a hyperparameter |
| Cosine Similarity | Text classification / document retrieval |

*(Note: 4 metrics × 1 line = 4 marks. A table earns 2 marks; written sentences also acceptable.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Internal Choice: A or B)

#### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of the K-Nearest Neighbour (KNN) classification algorithm with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the distance metric and the choice of $K$ in determining the classifier's performance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The KNN algorithm operates through five sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Training Phase (Storage):** The algorithm takes the labelled training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ and stores it as-is. No model parameters are learned. Time complexity: $\mathcal{O}(1)$.

**Stage 2 — Distance Computation:** At inference, the Euclidean distance (or chosen metric) is computed from the query point $\mathbf{x}_q$ to every training instance. The general Minkowski form is:

$$D_p(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i) = \left(\sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_{q,j} - x_{i,j} \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$$

**Stage 3 — Neighbour Selection:** The $N$ distances are sorted in ascending order; the first $K$ samples are selected as the nearest neighbours.

**Stage 4 — Voting:** Each neighbour votes for its class. The query point is assigned the class with the maximum total vote.

**Stage 5 — Output:** The predicted class label is returned.

**Role of Distance Metric:** The distance metric defines the geometry of "closeness" in the feature space. Different metrics produce different decision boundaries. Euclidean is the default, but Manhattan is preferred for high-dimensional sparse data, and Hamming for categorical features.

**Role of $K$:** $K$ controls the bias-variance trade-off. Small $K$ → low bias, high variance (noisy, overfits). Large $K$ → high bias, low variance (smoother, underfits). Optimal $K$ is selected by cross-validation, often $K \approx \sqrt{N}$ as a starting heuristic. If $K$ is even and the top two classes are tied, an odd $K$ (or weighted voting) is recommended.

*(Block diagram: refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid flowchart. Converting it to a hand-drawn sketch in the exam earns 1 extra mark.)*

---

**(b)** Given the following 2-D training data with two classes $A$ (●) and $B$ (▲), classify the query point $\mathbf{x}_q = (4, 4)$ using KNN with $K = 4$ and Manhattan distance. Show all distance calculations. **(7 marks)**

| Point | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | Class |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $P_1$ | 1 | 3 | $A$ |
| $P_2$ | 2 | 4 | $A$ |
| $P_3$ | 3 | 2 | $A$ |
| $P_4$ | 5 | 5 | $B$ |
| $P_5$ | 6 | 3 | $B$ |
| $P_6$ | 4 | 6 | $B$ |

**Model Solution:**

The Manhattan distance is:

$$D_M(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i) = \vert 4 - x_{i,1} \vert + \vert 4 - x_{i,2} \vert$$

We compute each distance explicitly:

$$D_M(\mathbf{x}_q, P_1) = \vert 4-1 \vert + \vert 4-3 \vert = 3 + 1 = 4$$

$$D_M(\mathbf{x}_q, P_2) = \vert 4-2 \vert + \vert 4-4 \vert = 2 + 0 = 2$$

$$D_M(\mathbf{x}_q, P_3) = \vert 4-3 \vert + \vert 4-2 \vert = 1 + 2 = 3$$

$$D_M(\mathbf{x}_q, P_4) = \vert 4-5 \vert + \vert 4-5 \vert = 1 + 1 = 2$$

$$D_M(\mathbf{x}_q, P_5) = \vert 4-6 \vert + \vert 4-3 \vert = 2 + 1 = 3$$

$$D_M(\mathbf{x}_q, P_6) = \vert 4-4 \vert + \vert 4-6 \vert = 0 + 2 = 2$$

Compiled distance table:

| Point | Class | Distance | Rank |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $P_1$ | $A$ | 4 | 6 |
| $P_2$ | $A$ | 2 | 1 (tied) |
| $P_3$ | $A$ | 3 | 4 (tied) |
| $P_4$ | $B$ | 2 | 1 (tied) |
| $P_5$ | $B$ | 3 | 4 (tied) |
| $P_6$ | $B$ | 2 | 1 (tied) |

The 3 points $P_2, P_4, P_6$ are tied at distance 2. To select 4 neighbours, we include all three plus the next closest, which are $P_3$ and $P_5$ (both at distance 3). The 4-nearest-neighbour set is therefore $\mathcal{N}_4 = \{P_2, P_4, P_6, P_3\}$ (breaking the secondary tie by selecting $P_3$ by index).

Vote tally: $A: 2$ (from $P_2, P_3$), $B: 2$ (from $P_4, P_6$) — **TIE**.

Since $K=4$ produces an inherent ambiguity, the standard KTU-recommended approach is to *resolve by reducing $K$ to 3*. The 3-nearest set is $\{P_2, P_4, P_6\}$ with vote tally $A:1, B:2$.

$$\boxed{\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}_q) = B}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Tie Handling**
> Many students either (a) declare the classification as "Tie — Undecidable" and stop, forfeiting 2 marks, or (b) silently pick a class without justification. The correct professional response is to explicitly apply a tie-breaking rule (e.g. reduce $K$, use distance-weighted voting, or fall back to class prior) and document the reasoning. Examiners award full credit only to students who *recognise* the tie and *resolve* it explicitly. **Loss of up to 3 marks if skipped.**

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast the following distance metrics used in KNN classifiers: Euclidean, Manhattan, Minkowski, and Cosine similarity. For each, state the formula, one advantage, and one limitation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Metric | Formula | Advantage | Limitation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Euclidean** ($L_2$) | $\sqrt{\sum (x_j - y_j)^2}$ | Intuitive, rotation-invariant, default choice | Sensitive to feature scale and outliers; suffers in high dimensions |
| **Manhattan** ($L_1$) | $\sum \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | More robust to outliers; works well with sparse / high-D data | Not rotation-invariant; less smooth gradient |
| **Minkowski** ($L_p$) | $\left(\sum \vert x_j - y_j \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalises $L_1$ and $L_2$; $p$ is a tunable hyperparameter | Choosing $p$ requires extra validation effort |
| **Cosine Similarity** | $\frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert}$ | Scale-invariant; ideal for text/document comparison where vector *direction* matters more than magnitude | Loses magnitude information; ill-defined for zero vectors |

---

**(b)** Design a KNN classifier for the dataset below and predict the class of the query point $\mathbf{x}_q = (5, 3)$. Use $K = 5$ and Euclidean distance. List each step of your calculation. **(7 marks)**

| Point | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | Class |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $P_1$ | 2 | 4 | $+$ |
| $P_2$ | 4 | 4 | $+$ |
| $P_3$ | 4 | 1 | $-$ |
| $P_4$ | 6 | 1 | $-$ |
| $P_5$ | 6 | 5 | $+$ |
| $P_6$ | 8 | 5 | $-$ |
| $P_7$ | 8 | 3 | $+$ |

**Model Solution:**

Distance formula:

$$D_E(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i) = \sqrt{(5-x_{i,1})^2 + (3-x_{i,2})^2}$$

Calculation for each point:

$$D_E(\mathbf{x}_q, P_1) = \sqrt{(5-2)^2 + (3-4)^2} = \sqrt{9+1} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$$

$$D_E(\mathbf{x}_q, P_2) = \sqrt{(5-4)^2 + (3-4)^2} = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$

$$D_E(\mathbf{x}_q, P_3) = \sqrt{(5-4)^2 + (3-1)^2} = \sqrt{1+4} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$$

$$D_E(\mathbf{x}_q, P_4) = \sqrt{(5-6)^2 + (3-1)^2} = \sqrt{1+4} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$$

$$D_E(\mathbf{x}_q, P_5) = \sqrt{(5-6)^2 + (3-5)^2} = \sqrt{1+4} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$$

$$D_E(\mathbf{x}_q, P_6) = \sqrt{(5-8)^2 + (3-5)^2} = \sqrt{9+4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606$$

$$D_E(\mathbf{x}_q, P_7) = \sqrt{(5-8)^2 + (3-3)^2} = \sqrt{9+0} = 3.000$$

Ranked distance table:

| Point | Class | Distance | Rank |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $P_2$ | $+$ | 1.414 | 1 |
| $P_3$ | $-$ | 2.236 | 2 (tied) |
| $P_4$ | $-$ | 2.236 | 2 (tied) |
| $P_5$ | $+$ | 2.236 | 2 (tied) |
| $P_7$ | $+$ | 3.000 | 5 |
| $P_1$ | $+$ | 3.162 | 6 |
| $P_6$ | $-$ | 3.606 | 7 |

Selecting the 5 nearest (top-5 by rank): $\{P_2, P_3, P_4, P_5, P_7\}$

Vote tally: $+$ class: 3 (from $P_2, P_5, P_7$), $-$ class: 2 (from $P_3, P_4$)

$$\boxed{\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}_q) = +}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Rounding Errors**
> In exam answer sheets, students are expected to retain **square-root form** (e.g. $\sqrt{5}$) until the final comparison, rather than prematurely rounding to 2.236. Premature rounding to two decimal places can invert the ranking of points like $P_3, P_4, P_5$ (all of which are $\sqrt{5}$), and examiners will deduct **1 full mark** if the wrong neighbours are chosen as a consequence. Always compare under the radical when distances are equal.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **KNN is non-parametric and instance-based** — no model is trained; the dataset *is* the model.
- **Lazy learning**: All computation happens at prediction time, giving $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot d)$ per query.
- **Distance metrics**: Euclidean is default; Manhattan for sparse/high-D; Hamming for categorical; Cosine for text.
- **Choice of $K$**: Small $K$ → noisy boundary (overfit); Large $K$ → smooth boundary (underfit). Use **odd** $K$ to avoid binary ties in two-class problems.
- **Feature scaling is mandatory** before applying KNN — unscaled features let large-magnitude features dominate the distance. Apply **StandardScaler** or **MinMaxScaler** as a pre-processing step.
- **Tie-breaking**: Reduce $K$ by 1, use distance-weighted voting, or fall back to class prior probability.
- **Voronoi cells**: The decision boundary of 1-NN is the union of Voronoi cell boundaries.
- **Curse of Dimensionality**: KNN degrades sharply in very high dimensions because all points become roughly equidistant. Dimensionality reduction (PCA) is often beneficial.
- **KNN for regression**: Replace majority vote with the **mean** (or weighted mean) of the $K$ nearest target values.
- **Cross-validation** is the gold standard for selecting $K$ — typically via $k$-fold CV on the training set.
- **KTU answer tip**: Always show the full distance table sorted in ascending order before selecting the $K$ neighbours — this single habit routinely earns the "methodology" 2 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Naive Bayes Classifier - Working Principle, Types of Naive Bayes Classifiers, Illustrative Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT302 - MACHINE LEARNING I
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Naive Bayes Classifier - Working Principle, Types of Naive Bayes Classifiers, Illustrative Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Naive Bayes Classifier (NBC)** is a family of probabilistic machine learning algorithms based on applying **Bayes' Theorem** with a *naive* (strong) assumption of **conditional independence** between every pair of features given the value of the class label. It is a **generative**, **supervised**, statistical classifier that predicts the class of a given instance by computing the posterior probability of each class and selecting the class with the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** estimate.

Mathematically, for a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ and a class variable $y \in \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$, the Naive Bayes classifier applies the rule:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x})$$

Applying Bayes' Theorem, this becomes:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

The denominator $P(\mathbf{x})$ is dropped because it remains constant across all classes and does not affect the argmax comparison.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Honest Doctor"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you visit a doctor with a combination of symptoms — fever, cough, body ache, and sore throat. The doctor doesn't compute the *joint* probability of all four symptoms occurring together (which is computationally impossible in real life). Instead, the doctor **naively assumes** that each symptom is *independently* generated by the disease. The doctor then multiplies the prior probability of the disease (say, Flu vs. Common Cold) by the individual likelihood of observing each symptom given that disease, and prescribes the diagnosis with the **highest product**.

**Key idea:** Naive Bayes treats every feature as an independent witness testifying for or against a class, and the classifier "counts votes" weighted by their individual reliability. This simplification, although unrealistic in many real-world scenarios, makes NBC remarkably fast, robust, and surprisingly accurate — especially in **high-dimensional text classification** problems.

## 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning | Role in NBC |
|---|---|---|
| **Prior Probability $P(C_k)$** | Probability of class $C_k$ *before* seeing the data | Acts as the "baseline bias" of the classifier |
| **Likelihood $P(x_i \mid C_k)$** | Probability of feature $x_i$ given the class | Captures the "evidence weight" of a feature |
| **Posterior $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x})$** | Updated probability of the class after seeing $\mathbf{x}$ | The final decision quantity |
| **Evidence $P(\mathbf{x})$** | Marginal probability of the feature vector | Normalizing constant (often ignored) |
| **MAP Estimate** | Class with the **Maximum A Posteriori** probability | The predicted class label $\hat{y}$ |
| **Conditional Independence** | $P(x_i, x_j \mid C_k) = P(x_i \mid C_k) \cdot P(x_j \mid C_k)$ | The "naive" simplifying assumption |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Foundation: Bayes' Theorem

The entire edifice of Naive Bayes rests on **Thomas Bayes' Theorem** (1763), which describes the probability of an event based on prior knowledge of conditions related to the event.

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

### Components Explained
- **$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x})$ — Posterior:** What we want — the probability of class $C_k$ *given* the observed features.
- **$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$ — Likelihood:** How probable the observed features are under the assumption of class $C_k$.
- **$P(C_k)$ — Prior:** Background probability of class $C_k$, usually estimated as the **relative frequency** of the class in the training set.
- **$P(\mathbf{x})$ — Evidence:** Total probability of observing the feature vector, computed as $\sum_{k} P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) P(C_k)$.

## 2.2 The "Naive" Assumption — Why It Works

In a realistic dataset, features are rarely independent (e.g., in text, the word "machine" strongly predicts the word "learning"). Computing the true joint distribution $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$ is intractable for non-trivial $n$ because the number of possible combinations grows **exponentially**. Naive Bayes sidesteps this by **assuming**:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

This **conditional independence assumption** decouples the features, allowing the likelihood to be computed as a simple product of per-feature probabilities. Although mathematically unsound in most cases, empirical studies show that NBC's classification accuracy is competitive with far more sophisticated models like **SVMs** and **neural networks**, especially when:
- The dataset is small or sparse.
- Features are high-dimensional (text, genomics).
- The independence assumption is *approximately* satisfied.

## 2.3 The MAP Decision Rule

The classification decision is taken by selecting the class that **maximizes the posterior**:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, multiplication of many small probabilities causes **arithmetic underflow** (numbers smaller than $10^{-308}$ in 64-bit IEEE 754). This is why implementations convert the product into a **log-sum**:
> $$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} \left[ \log P(C_k) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid C_k) \right]$$
> This is **numerically stable** and **monotonically equivalent** to the product form.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory formulas to memorize for the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE):**

| # | Formula | Description | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \, P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | Bayes' Theorem | Foundation of all NBC derivations |
| 2 | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$ | MAP Decision Rule | Final classification step |
| 3 | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} \left[ \log P(C_k) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid C_k) \right]$ | Log-form MAP | To prevent underflow in code/implementation |
| 4 | $\hat{P}(C_k) = \dfrac{N_{C_k}}{N}$ | Prior Estimation (MLE) | M-estimate of class frequency in training data |
| 5 | $\hat{P}(x_i = v \mid C_k) = \dfrac{\text{count}(x_i = v, C_k) + \alpha}{\text{count}(C_k) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$ | **Laplace (Additive) Smoothing** | When a feature value never appears with a class |
| 6 | $\hat{P}(x_i = v \mid C_k) = \dfrac{\text{count}(x_i = v, C_k) + \alpha}{\text{count}(C_k) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$ | Lidstone Smoothing ($\alpha < 1$) | Generalized smoothing parameter |
| 7 | $P(x_i = v \mid C_k) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{C_k}^2}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(v - \mu_{C_k})^2}{2\sigma_{C_k}^2}\right)$ | Gaussian Likelihood | For continuous features in **Gaussian NB** |
| 8 | $P(x_i = k \mid C_k) = \dfrac{N_{x_i=k, C_k} + \alpha}{N_{C_k} + \alpha \cdot K}$ | Multinomial Likelihood | For text/count data in **Multinomial NB** |
| 9 | $P(x_i \mid C_k) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x_i \cdot \theta_{C_k}}}$ | Bernoulli Likelihood (conceptual) | For binary features in **Bernoulli NB** |

> **Engineering Utility:** Naive Bayes is the workhorse behind **spam filters** (Gmail uses a variant), **sentiment analysis** pipelines, **medical diagnosis systems**, **real-time document categorization** in news aggregators, and **intrusion detection systems** in cybersecurity.
