## 5.1 📝 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each — Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Precision in the context of a binary classifier. State its formula.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (2 Marks)

**Precision** is a performance metric that measures the proportion of correctly predicted positive instances out of all instances predicted as positive.

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

It quantifies the *reliability* of positive predictions. A precision of 1.0 means every positive prediction is correct (no false positives), while 0 means all positive predictions are wrong.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Why is Precision alone insufficient to evaluate a classifier? Illustrate with one example.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (2 Marks)

Precision ignores True Negatives and False Negatives, so it can be misleading when the model misses many actual positives. **Example:** In cancer detection, a model that predicts only 1 patient as positive (correctly) has precision = 100%, but recall may be near 0% if it misses 99 actual cancer cases. Hence, precision must be combined with recall (or F1-score) for a complete picture.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the Confusion Matrix for a binary classification problem. Define Precision, Recall, and F1-Score with formulas. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A classifier produces the following confusion matrix on 200 test samples:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | 50 | 20 |
| **Actual Negative** | 10 | 120 |

Calculate Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 Marks

The **Confusion Matrix** is a tabular summary of a classifier's predictions vs. actual labels for a binary classification problem. It has four cells:

- **TP (True Positives):** Correctly predicted positives
- **TN (True Negatives):** Correctly predicted negatives
- **FP (False Positives):** Negatives incorrectly predicted as positives (Type I error)
- **FN (False Negatives):** Positives incorrectly predicted as negatives (Type II error)

**Formulas:**

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

**Precision** measures positive prediction accuracy. **Recall** measures coverage of actual positives. **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of the two, providing a balanced metric when class distribution is uneven.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Extract values from the confusion matrix.

$$
TP = 50, \quad FP = 10, \quad FN = 20, \quad TN = 120
$$

**Step 2:** Verify total.

$$
50 + 10 + 20 + 120 = 200 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3:** Compute Precision.

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} = 0.8333
$$

**Step 4:** Compute Recall.

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{50}{50 + 20} = \frac{50}{70} = 0.7143
$$

**Step 5:** Compute F1-Score.

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8333 \times 0.7143}{0.8333 + 0.7143} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.5952}{1.5476} = 2 \times 0.3846 = 0.7692
$$

**Final Answers:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Precision} = 0.833, \;\; \text{Recall} = 0.714, \;\; F_1 = 0.769}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Differentiate between Precision and Recall. Explain the Precision-Recall trade-off with respect to the decision threshold. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A logistic regression model is evaluated at two thresholds. At threshold $\tau = 0.5$, the confusion matrix yields $TP = 80$, $FP = 40$, $FN = 20$. At threshold $\tau = 0.7$, the values are $TP = 60$, $FP = 15$, $FN = 40$. Calculate precision and recall at both thresholds and comment on the trade-off. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 Marks

| Aspect | Precision | Recall |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Quality of positive predictions | Coverage of actual positives |
| **Formula** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ |
| **Focus** | Predicted Positive column | Actual Positive row |
| **Question Answered** | "When model says YES, how often is it right?" | "Of all actual YES, how many did model find?" |
| **Symmetry** | Asymmetric (ignores TN) | Asymmetric (ignores TN) |

**Precision-Recall Trade-off via Threshold:**

In probabilistic classifiers, the decision threshold $\tau$ governs the predicted class:

- **Lowering $\tau$** → More positives predicted → **Recall ↑**, **Precision ↓** (more FP)
- **Raising $\tau$** → Fewer positives predicted → **Precision ↑**, **Recall ↓** (more FN)

There is an inherent inverse relationship between precision and recall as the threshold varies.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Compute metrics at $\tau = 0.5$.

$$
\text{Precision}_{0.5} = \frac{80}{80 + 40} = \frac{80}{120} = 0.667
$$

$$
\text{Recall}_{0.5} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80
$$

**Step 2:** Compute metrics at $\tau = 0.7$.

$$
\text{Precision}_{0.7} = \frac{60}{60 + 15} = \frac{60}{75} = 0.80
$$

$$
\text{Recall}_{0.7} = \frac{60}{60 + 40} = \frac{60}{100} = 0.60
$$

**Step 3:** Tabulate the comparison.

| Threshold | Precision | Recall |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau = 0.5$ | 0.667 | 0.800 |
| $\tau = 0.7$ | 0.800 | 0.600 |

**Step 4:** Comment on the trade-off.

Increasing the threshold from 0.5 → 0.7 raised precision (0.667 → 0.80) but reduced recall (0.80 → 0.60). This confirms the **inverse trade-off**: a stricter threshold makes the classifier more conservative, producing fewer but more trustworthy positive predictions at the cost of missing some actual positives.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state precision convention** when $TP + FP = 0$ — always write "Precision = 0 by convention."
> 2. **Confusing Micro vs. Macro averaging** in multiclass problems — clearly state which one you're computing.
> 3. **Skipping units / percentage conversion** — express as both decimal AND percentage for clarity.
> 4. **Ignoring the asymmetric nature** — never claim precision equals accuracy or recall.
> 5. **Missing the trade-off explanation** — KTU values explicit "WHY" reasoning, not just numerical answers.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Precision (KTU Module 4)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Precision = Quality of positive predictions = $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$
- ✅ **Range:** $0 \leq \text{Precision} \leq 1$; **Best = 1**, **Worst = 0**
- ✅ **Asymmetric Metric:** Depends only on $TP$ and $FP$; **ignores $TN$ and $FN$**
- ✅ **Edge Case:** If $TP + FP = 0$, set Precision = **0 by convention**
- ✅ **Multiclass Variants:** Macro (per-class average), Micro (aggregate first), Weighted (by class support)
- ✅ **Trade-off:** Higher threshold → Higher Precision, Lower Recall (and vice versa)
- ✅ **Use When:** False Positives are **costlier** than False Negatives (spam, fraud, medical false alarms)
- ✅ **Companion Metric:** Always report with **Recall**; use **F1-Score** as a single balanced number
- ✅ **Curve:** Precision-Recall (PR) curve + **PR-AUC** preferred for imbalanced datasets
- ✅ **KTU Exam Tip:** Draw the **confusion matrix** first in numerical answers, then write formulas symbolically before substituting values.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recall
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT302 - MACHINE LEARNING I
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recall

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Recall (R)** is a fundamental **classification evaluation metric** defined as the ratio of the number of **True Positives (TP)** correctly predicted by a classifier to the total number of **actual positive instances** present in the dataset. It quantifies a model's ability to find **all the relevant cases** within a dataset.

Mathematically expressed in KTU board notation:

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

Recall is **synonymously** referred to in academic literature and KTU question papers as:
- **Sensitivity** (used heavily in Medical/Statistical contexts)
- **True Positive Rate (TPR)**
- **Hit Rate**
- **Completeness** (in Information Retrieval)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** When answering questions, always state that Recall answers the question — *"Of all the patients that **actually have** the disease, how many did the model **correctly flag** as diseased?"*

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Airport Security Scanner**
>
> Imagine **10 actual threats** (real weapons) pass through 100 bags at an airport. Your security scanner is your ML model. A perfect scanner catches **all 10 weapons** — that means **Recall = 1.0 (100%)**.
>
> Now suppose the scanner catches only **7 of the 10 weapons** and misses 3. The Recall is **7/10 = 0.7 (70%)**. The 3 missed weapons are the **False Negatives (FN)** — the most dangerous errors.

**Simple Rule of Thumb for Students:**

| Scenario | What Recall Measures |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Cancer Detection** | Of all cancer patients, how many were caught? |
| **Spam Filtering** | Of all spam emails, how many were caught? |
| **Fraud Detection** | Of all fraud cases, how many were flagged? |
| **Defect Detection (Manufacturing)** | Of all defective products, how many were identified? |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never confuse Recall with Precision.** Precision asks *"Of those flagged, how many were correct?"*, whereas Recall asks *"Of all actual positives, how many were caught?"*

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Confusion Matrix Foundation

Recall cannot be understood in isolation. It is derived from the **Confusion Matrix**, a $2 \times 2$ contingency table for binary classification.

| | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

> [!NOTE]
> - **TP** → Model says "Positive", Truth is "Positive" ✅
> - **FP** → Model says "Positive", Truth is "Negative" ❌ (Type I Error)
> - **FN** → Model says "Negative", Truth is "Positive" ❌ (Type II Error)
> - **TN** → Model says "Negative", Truth is "Negative" ✅

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Recall

1. **Identify the Denominator:** The denominator $TP + FN$ is the **column sum of the Actual Positive row**. This represents **every instance in reality that is positive**, regardless of what the model predicted.
2. **Identify the Numerator:** The numerator $TP$ is the count of cases where the model **successfully predicted the actual positive**.
3. **Compute the Ratio:** A value between **0 and 1** (or **0% to 100%**) is obtained.
4. **Interpret:** Higher Recall ⇒ The model misses fewer positives.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Meaning | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Recall (TPR / Sensitivity)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Out of actual positives, how many found? | **Medical Diagnosis, Fraud, Fault Detection** |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Out of predicted positives, how many correct? | **Spam Filters, Search Engines** |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Out of actual negatives, how many found? | Avoiding false alarms |
| **F1-Score** | $2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | Imbalanced datasets |
| **Accuracy** | $\dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}$ | Overall correctness | Balanced datasets only |
| **Fall-Out (FPR)** | $\dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $\vert$ should not appear in formula | ROC Curve construction |
| **Miss Rate (FNR)** | $\dfrac{FN}{TP + FN}$ | $1 - \text{Recall}$ | Inverse Recall |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions of Recall:**
> - **Best Case:** $FN = 0 \Rightarrow \text{Recall} = 1$ (all positives detected)
> - **Worst Case:** $TP = 0 \Rightarrow \text{Recall} = 0$ (no positive detected)
> - **Monotonic Property:** Recall is **non-decreasing** as the classification threshold decreases.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical AI (KTU-favoured example):** In cancer screening, the **cost of a False Negative is enormous** (a sick patient goes untreated). Thus, oncologists demand **High Recall**, even at the cost of some False Positives.
- **Manufacturing Quality Control:** Identifying all defective components in a production line requires **High Recall** because shipping a defective part is costlier than re-inspecting a good one.
- **Cybersecurity / Intrusion Detection:** Missing a malware instance (FN) is catastrophic; hence intrusion detection systems are tuned for **Maximum Recall**.
- **Information Retrieval (Search Engines):** A user searching for "Machine Learning notes" expects the system to retrieve **all relevant documents** — again pointing to Recall.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Recall from First Principles

Consider a binary classification problem where the target variable $Y \in \{0, 1\}$ and the predicted class $\hat{Y} \in \{0, 1\}$.

**Step 1:** Define the four fundamental outcomes as indicator expectations over a dataset of $N$ samples.

$$TP = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{I}(\hat{Y}_i = 1 \mid Y_i = 1)$$

**Step 2:** Define False Negatives — actual positives that were misclassified as negative.

$$FN = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{I}(\hat{Y}_i = 0 \mid Y_i = 1)$$

**Step 3:** The total number of actual positives in the dataset is:

$$P_{actual} = TP + FN$$

**Step 4:** By definition, Recall (R) is the proportion of correctly identified positives:

$$R = \frac{\text{Correctly Identified Positives}}{\text{Total Actual Positives}}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting from Steps 1, 2, and 3:

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**Step 6:** Taking the **probabilistic limit** as $N \rightarrow \infty$:

$$R = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1) = \frac{P(\hat{Y}=1, Y=1)}{P(Y=1)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This probabilistic form clarifies that Recall is mathematically the **conditional probability of a correct positive prediction given an actual positive instance**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard)

**Problem Statement:** A spam filter model is tested on **500 emails**. The resulting confusion matrix is:

| | Predicted Spam | Predicted Not-Spam |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Actual Spam** | 80 (TP) | 20 (FN) |
| **Actual Not-Spam** | 30 (FP) | 370 (TN) |

**Compute the Recall.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Extract values from the confusion matrix.
- $TP = 80$
- $FN = 20$

**Step 2:** Apply the Recall formula.

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values.

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{80}{80 + 20}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the denominator.

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{80}{100}$$

**Step 5:** Final result.

$$\text{Recall} = 0.8 \quad \text{or} \quad 80\%$$

**Interpretation:** Out of every **100 actual spam emails**, the model correctly identified **80**, while **20 spam emails slipped through** into the user's inbox. This means **20% of spam remains undetected**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis

| Property | Precision | **Recall** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Penalises | False Positives (FP) | **False Negatives (FN)** |
| Focuses on | Prediction Quality | **Coverage of Positives** |
| Denominator | $TP + FP$ | $TP + FN$ |
| Increases when | Threshold is raised | **Threshold is lowered** |
| Critical Domain | Search Engines, Recommendations | **Medical, Security, Fault Detection** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Precision-Recall Trade-off is the most frequently asked concept in KTU Module 4 questions.** Remember — increasing Recall typically decreases Precision and vice-versa, controlled by the decision threshold.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Recall Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Trained Classifier] --> B[Run Model on Test Set]
    B --> C[Construct Confusion Matrix]
    C --> D{TP exists?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Count True Positives TP]
    D -- No --> F[Recall = 0]
    E --> G[Count False Negatives FN]
    G --> H[Compute Total Positives = TP + FN]
    H --> I[Apply Recall Formula]
    I --> J[Recall = TP / TP + FN]
    J --> K[Threshold Check]
    K --> L{Recall meets domain requirement?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Model Approved]
    L -- No --> N[Tune Threshold or Re-train]
    N --> B
```

### 4.2 Confusion Matrix Visual (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph matrix[Confusion Matrix]
        direction TB
        N1["Actual Positive"]
        N2["Actual Negative"]
        N3["TP - True Positive"]
        N4["FP - False Positive"]
        N5["FN - False Neg"]
        N6["TN - True Neg"]
        N1 --- N3
        N1 --- N5
        N2 --- N4
        N2 --- N6
    end
    N3 --> R[Recall = TP / TP + FN]
    N5 --> R
    R --> Result[Recall Score 0 to 1]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Classification Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RawData[Raw Dataset] --> Split[Train Test Split]
    Split --> Train[Training Set]
    Split --> Test[Test Set]
    Train --> Model[Trained ML Model]
    Model --> Predict[Generate Predictions]
    Test --> Compare[Compare with Ground Truth]
    Predict --> Compare
    Compare --> CM[Build Confusion Matrix]
    CM --> RecallCalc[Recall Module TP over TP+FN]
    CM --> PrecisionCalc[Precision Module]
    CM --> F1Calc[F1 Score Module]
    RecallCalc --> Report[Final Evaluation Report]
    PrecisionCalc --> Report
    F1Calc --> Report
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In the Mermaid graph, observe that the Recall Module **only** depends on the **TP and FN** cells of the confusion matrix — it is **blind to FP and TN**. This is why Recall is a **positivity-focused** metric.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **Recall** in the context of a classification problem. Why is it also called the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** or **Sensitivity**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Recall is defined as the ratio of correctly predicted positive instances to the total number of actual positive instances in the dataset.

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

It is called the **True Positive Rate** because it expresses the proportion of actual positives that are correctly classified as positive. It is called **Sensitivity** because it measures the model's sensitivity to detecting the positive class. In medical diagnosis, a sensitive test rarely misses a person with the disease.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for naming TPR/Sensitivity with context)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Differentiate between **Precision** and **Recall** with one suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Precision | Recall |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Formula | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ |
| Focus | Quality of positive predictions | Coverage of actual positives |
| Penalises | False Positives | False Negatives |

**Example (1.5 marks):** In a **fraud detection system**, *Precision* tells the bank *"Of all transactions flagged, how many were actually fraudulent?"* while *Recall* tells *"Of all fraudulent transactions, how many did we catch?"* — for a bank, missing a fraud is dangerous, so high Recall is preferred.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each 14-mark question carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ✍️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]*

**(a)** Explain the **Confusion Matrix** for a binary classifier with a neat diagram. Derive the formula for **Recall** and discuss its significance in **imbalanced datasets**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Confusion Matrix Diagram (2 marks):**

| | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Actual Positive** | TP | FN |
| **Actual Negative** | FP | TN |

**2. Derivation of Recall (2.5 marks):**

From the matrix, the total number of actual positives is $TP + FN$. The correctly predicted positives are $TP$. Therefore:

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**3. Significance in Imbalanced Datasets (2.5 marks):**

In an imbalanced dataset (e.g., 99% negative, 1% positive), **Accuracy is misleading** — a dumb model predicting "all negative" achieves 99% accuracy. Recall becomes the **critical metric** because it focuses exclusively on how well the model detects the **minority (positive) class**. In cancer datasets where positives are only 0.5%, Recall tells the doctor the true detection rate.

---

**(b)** A medical diagnosis system produces the following confusion matrix on a test set of 200 patients: **TP = 80, FP = 20, TN = 90, FN = 10**. Calculate the **Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Accuracy**. Which metric is most important here and why? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $TP = 80$, $FP = 20$, $TN = 90$, $FN = 10$, $N = 200$.

**Step 1: Precision (1.5 marks)**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80$$

**Step 2: Recall (1.5 marks)**

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 10} = \frac{80}{90} \approx 0.889$$

**Step 3: F1-Score (2 marks)**

$$F1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.80 \cdot 0.889}{0.80 + 0.889}$$

$$F1 = \frac{1.4224}{1.689} \approx 0.842$$

**Step 4: Accuracy (1 mark)**

$$\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{80 + 90}{200} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.85$$

**Step 5: Justification (1 mark):**

In medical diagnosis, **Recall is the most important metric** because a False Negative (missing a sick patient) can cost a life. A Recall of **88.9%** means the system catches nearly 9 out of 10 actual disease cases.

---

### ✍️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the **Precision-Recall Trade-off** with a suitable example. Explain how varying the classification **threshold** affects these metrics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Trade-off Definition (2 marks):**

Precision and Recall are inversely related. Increasing one typically decreases the other. This phenomenon is the **Precision-Recall Trade-off**.

**2. Example (3 marks):**

Consider a cancer classifier with probabilities. At a **high threshold (0.9)**, the model only predicts "Cancer" when extremely confident. This gives **High Precision** (few False Positives) but **Low Recall** (misses real cancer cases → high FN). At a **low threshold (0.3)**, the model flags more cases. **High Recall** (catches all cancer) but **Low Precision** (more healthy people wrongly flagged).

**3. Threshold Effect (2 marks):**

- **Threshold ↑** → Precision ↑, Recall ↓
- **Threshold ↓** → Recall ↑, Precision ↓

The optimal threshold is selected based on the application's cost-sensitivity.

---

**(b)** A fraud detection system produced the following results: **TP = 150, FP = 50, TN = 600, FN = 200**. Compute and interpret the **Recall (Sensitivity)** and **Specificity (TNR)**. What does this tell you about the model? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $TP = 150$, $FP = 50$, $TN = 600$, $FN = 200$.

**Step 1: Recall / Sensitivity (2.5 marks)**

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{150}{150 + 200} = \frac{150}{350} = \frac{3}{7} \approx 0.4286$$

**Step 2: Specificity / TNR (2.5 marks)**

$$S = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{600}{600 + 50} = \frac{600}{650} = \frac{12}{13} \approx 0.9231$$

**Step 3: Interpretation (2 marks):**

The model detects only **42.86%** of actual fraud cases — this is **dangerously low** for a banking system. A bank would lose 57% of fraud cases. However, its Specificity of **92.31%** means it rarely wrongly flags legitimate transactions. **Conclusion:** The model's threshold is too strict; the bank should lower the threshold (or retrain) to boost Recall, accepting some false alarms.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Precision and Recall formulas** — always check the denominator: Precision uses $FP$, Recall uses $FN$. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to simplify** the fraction to decimal/percentage in numerical problems. (–0.5 marks)
> 3. **Not interpreting** the computed value — KTU examiners award marks for the *"So what does this mean?"* line.
> 4. **Writing only the formula** without substituting numerical values when the question is computational.
> 5. **Missing the trade-off discussion** when a question mentions both Precision and Recall.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Recall = TP / (TP + FN)** — always, no exceptions.
- ✅ **Synonyms:** Sensitivity, TPR, Hit Rate, Completeness.
- ✅ **Range:** $[0, 1]$ — Higher is better.
- ✅ **Recall = 1** means **no False Negatives** — the model catches every positive.
- ✅ **Recall = 0** means the model detected **no positives at all** (TP = 0).
- ✅ Recall is **crucial when FN cost is high** (medical, security, safety).
- ✅ Recall is **independent of TN and FP** — these cells do not enter the formula.
- ✅ **Trade-off:** Lower threshold $\Rightarrow$ Higher Recall, Lower Precision.
- ✅ **F1-Score** combines Precision and Recall via the harmonic mean.
- ✅ **In imbalanced datasets**, Recall is far more informative than Accuracy.
- ✅ **Miss Rate** = $1 - \text{Recall}$ = $\dfrac{FN}{TP + FN}$ = FNR.
- ✅ Recall $\neq$ Precision — Precision uses $FP$ in the denominator, Recall uses $FN$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: F1-Score
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT302 - MACHINE LEARNING I
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** F1-Score

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of binary (and extended to multi-class) classification under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *PCAMT302 – Machine Learning I*, the **F1-Score** (also called **F-measure** or **F1-measure**) is formally defined as the **harmonic mean of Precision and Recall**. It is a single scalar metric designed to balance the trade-off between the *positive predictive value* (Precision) and the *sensitivity* (Recall) of a classifier.

Mathematically, it is the special case of the general $F_\beta$ score where the weighting parameter $\beta = 1$, implying that **Precision and Recall are weighted equally**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):**
> The F1-Score is the *de facto* standard single-number evaluation metric for **imbalanced classification problems**, where plain **Accuracy** becomes misleading. It lives in the closed interval $\mathbf{0 \le F_1 \le 1}$, with $\mathbf{F_1 = 1}$ representing perfect classification and $\mathbf{F_1 = 0}$ representing complete failure.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Two Judges" Metaphor

Imagine you are a doctor who has screened **1,000 patients** for a rare disease. You must answer two questions:

1. **Precision (Judge A)**: *"Of the patients I told were sick, how many actually were sick?"* — This judge penalizes **false alarms**.
2. **Recall (Judge B)**: *"Of all the patients who were actually sick, how many did I correctly catch?"* — This judge penalizes **missed detections**.

A perfect model needs to satisfy **both** judges. If you declare *everyone* sick, Recall becomes 1.0 (you caught every sick patient) but Precision collapses to almost 0. If you declare *only one* patient sick and are right, Precision becomes 1.0 but Recall collapses. The **F1-Score** is the *single combined grade* that punishes you harshly if either judge gives a low score — it forces **balance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Harmonic Mean instead of Arithmetic Mean?**
> The arithmetic mean of (Precision=1.0, Recall=0.1) would give 0.55, which sounds decent. But the harmonic mean gives only $\approx 0.18$. This reflects reality: a model that catches only 10% of positives is **terrible**, regardless of precision. The harmonic mean *correctly* punishes extreme imbalance between the two metrics.

## 1.3 Placement in the KTU Evaluation Pipeline

In a typical KTU laboratory/project evaluation framework, the F1-Score is computed *after* the **Confusion Matrix** is filled in. It uses three out of the four cells of the matrix: **True Positives (TP), False Positives (FP), and False Negatives (FN)**. The **True Negatives (TN)** do not appear in the F1 formula — making F1 *insensitive to class imbalance in the negative class* (an advantage, not a bug).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Values Reminder:**
> - $F_1 = 1 \implies$ Perfect Precision **AND** Perfect Recall.
> - $F_1 = 0 \implies$ Either Precision $= 0$ or Recall $= 0$ (or both).
> - F1 is undefined only when both Precision $= 0$ **and** Recall $= 0$ (i.e., $TP = 0$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Building Blocks — Precision and Recall

Before F1, the KTU syllabus requires mastery of its parents. Both metrics are derived from the four cells of the **Confusion Matrix**:

| Cell | Full Form | Meaning |
|:-----|:----------|:--------|
| **TP** | True Positives | Model predicted *Positive*; actual class is *Positive*. |
| **FP** | False Positives | Model predicted *Positive*; actual class is *Negative* (Type I Error). |
| **FN** | False Negatives | Model predicted *Negative*; actual class is *Positive* (Type II Error). |
| **TN** | True Negatives | Model predicted *Negative*; actual class is *Negative*. |

The two parent metrics are defined as:

- **Precision** $\;(P)$ — *"When the model says YES, how often is it correct?"*
- **Recall** $\;(R)$ — *"Of all real YES cases, how many did the model catch?"*

## 2.2 The F1 Formula and Its Siblings

The **F1-Score** is mathematically defined as:

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2}{\dfrac{1}{P} + \dfrac{1}{R}} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
$$

The last form is the **micro-averaged** form, directly computed from the confusion matrix without separately computing P and R. It is the most reliable form for KTU board exams.

The general **$F_\beta$ family** weights Recall $\beta$ times more than Precision:

$$
F_\beta \;=\; (1+\beta^2)\,\frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 P + R}
$$

Special cases:
- $\beta = 1 \implies F_1$ (equal weight).
- $\beta = 2 \implies F_2$ (Recall weighted heavier — used in medical/disease detection).
- $\beta = 0.5 \implies F_{0.5}$ (Precision weighted heavier — used in spam filtering).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Medical Diagnosis (Cancer, COVID):** Prefer $F_2$ — missing a sick patient (low Recall) is catastrophic.
> - **Spam Filters, Recommendation Systems:** Prefer $F_{0.5}$ — false positives (wrong recommendations) annoy users.
> - **General KTU Lab/Project Work:** $F_1$ is the **default and most-cited** metric in the ML-I syllabus.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

| Formula | Expression | Variables / Range | Notes |
|:--------|:-----------|:------------------|:------|
| Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $0 \le P \le 1$ | Goes to 0 if $FP \gg TP$. |
| Recall (Sensitivity, TPR) | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $0 \le R \le 1$ | Goes to 0 if $FN \gg TP$. |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = \dfrac{2TP}{2TP + FP + FN}$ | $0 \le F_1 \le 1$ | **KTU favorite single metric.** |
| F1 (alternate) | $F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P + R}$ | requires $P+R > 0$ | Symmetric in P, R. |
| $F_\beta$ general | $F_\beta = (1+\beta^2)\dfrac{PR}{\beta^2 P + R}$ | $\beta > 0$ | $\beta=1$ recovers $F_1$. |
| Macro F1 (multi-class) | $F_1^{macro} = \dfrac{1}{K}\sum_{i=1}^{K} F_{1,i}$ | $K$ = no. of classes | Average of per-class F1s. |
| Weighted F1 (multi-class) | $F_1^{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{K} w_i \cdot F_{1,i}$ | $w_i = \text{support}_i / N$ | Weighted by class frequency. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-Class Extension (Micro vs Macro F1):**
> KTU 2024 explicitly tests the difference between *Micro-averaged* and *Macro-averaged* F1:
> - **Micro F1** = aggregate TP, FP, FN across all classes, then apply the binary F1 formula. **Equivalent to Accuracy** in single-label multi-class.
> - **Macro F1** = compute F1 for each class independently, then average. Treats all classes equally regardless of size.

## 2.4 Why F1 Works — The Harmonic Mean Logic

The harmonic mean of two positive numbers $a$ and $b$ is bounded above by their geometric mean, which is bounded above by their arithmetic mean:

$$
H(a,b) \;\le\; G(a,b) \;\le\; A(a,b)
$$

Crucially, **$H$ is dominated by its smaller argument**: it is small when *either* $a$ or $b$ is small. This is precisely the desired behaviour for a metric that should fail if **either** Precision **or** Recall fails.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:**
> Some students confuse **F1 with the arithmetic mean** $\frac{P+R}{2}$. If $P=1.0$ and $R=0.1$, the arithmetic mean is 0.55 (a passing grade), but $F_1 \approx 0.18$ (an F-grade). Examiners reward students who notice this and use the harmonic mean.

## 2.5 Decision Rule: When to Use F1 vs Accuracy

Use **F1-Score** when:
- The dataset is **imbalanced** (e.g., 95% negative, 5% positive).
- **False Positives** and **False Negatives** carry *different* but both *non-trivial* costs.
- A *single* defensible number is required for comparison between models.

Use **Accuracy** only when:
- Classes are roughly balanced.
- FP and FN costs are symmetric.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of F1 from the Harmonic Mean of P and R

**Starting Point:** The harmonic mean $H$ of two positive numbers $x$ and $y$ is defined as the reciprocal of the arithmetic mean of their reciprocals:

$$
H(x,y) \;=\; \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{x} + \dfrac{1}{y}\right)} \;=\; \frac{2xy}{x+y}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute P and R:** Set $x = P$ and $y = R$ (both Precision and Recall are non-negative real numbers, and we exclude the trivial case $P=R=0$):

$$
F_1 \;=\; H(P, R) \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the definitions of P and R in terms of the confusion matrix:**

$$
P \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \quad \text{and} \quad R \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator $2PR$:**

$$
2PR \;=\; 2 \cdot \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \cdot \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{(TP + FP)(TP + FN)}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator $P + R$:**

$$
P + R \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FP} + \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \;=\; \frac{TP(TP + FN) + TP(TP + FP)}{(TP + FP)(TP + FN)} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot TP^2 + TP \cdot FP + TP \cdot FN}{(TP + FP)(TP + FN)}
$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio $F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$:**

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{2 \cdot TP^2 + TP \cdot FP + TP \cdot FN}
$$

**Step 6 — Cancel the common factor of $TP$ from numerator and denominator** (valid since $TP > 0$ in any meaningful F1 calculation):

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU board form** of F1, used in every numerical problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Notice that the term $2 \cdot TP$ appears in the numerator *and* as the first term of the denominator. This means $F_1$ is mathematically equivalent to a "weighted" accuracy that double-counts the positives — a design choice that automatically protects against the dominance of the majority (negative) class.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Binary Classification (KTU Standard)

A spam filter is tested on **200 emails**. The resulting confusion matrix is:

| | Predicted Spam | Predicted Not-Spam |
|:--|:--:|:--:|
| **Actual Spam** | TP = 30 | FN = 10 |
| **Actual Not-Spam** | FP = 5 | TN = 155 |

**Compute Precision:**

$$
P \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \;=\; \frac{30}{30 + 5} \;=\; \frac{30}{35} \;=\; 0.857
$$

**Compute Recall:**

$$
R \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \;=\; \frac{30}{30 + 10} \;=\; \frac{30}{40} \;=\; 0.750
$$

**Compute F1 directly from the matrix formula:**

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot 30}{2 \cdot 30 + 5 + 10} \;=\; \frac{60}{75} \;=\; 0.800
$$

**Cross-check using the harmonic-mean formula:**

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot 0.857 \cdot 0.750}{0.857 + 0.750} \;=\; \frac{1.2857}{1.6071} \;\approx\; 0.800 \;\checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The model is *decent* but not great. It catches 75% of spam (Recall = 0.75) and is 85.7% reliable when it flags an email (Precision = 0.857). The F1 of 0.80 captures both, and would drop sharply if either metric degrades.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Multi-Class Macro vs Weighted F1

A 3-class image classifier (Cat, Dog, Bird) yields the following counts after testing on **120 images**:

| Class | TP | FP | FN |
|:--|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| Cat  | 35 | 5  | 5  |
| Dog  | 30 | 10 | 5  |
| Bird | 20 | 5  | 5  |

**Step 1 — Per-class F1 for Cat:**

$$
F_1^{\text{Cat}} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot 35}{2 \cdot 35 + 5 + 5} \;=\; \frac{70}{80} \;=\; 0.875
$$

**Step 2 — Per-class F1 for Dog:**

$$
F_1^{\text{Dog}} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot 30}{2 \cdot 30 + 10 + 5} \;=\; \frac{60}{75} \;=\; 0.800
$$

**Step 3 — Per-class F1 for Bird:**

$$
F_1^{\text{Bird}} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot 20}{2 \cdot 20 + 5 + 5} \;=\; \frac{40}{50} \;=\; 0.800
$$

**Step 4 — Macro F1 (simple average, equal weight per class):**

$$
F_1^{\text{macro}} \;=\; \frac{0.875 + 0.800 + 0.800}{3} \;=\; \frac{2.475}{3} \;=\; 0.825
$$

**Step 5 — Weighted F1 (weight by support, i.e., TP per class):**

Total support $N = 35 + 30 + 20 = 85$.

$$
F_1^{\text{weighted}} \;=\; \frac{35}{85}(0.875) + \frac{30}{85}(0.800) + \frac{20}{85}(0.800)
$$

$$
= \;0.4118 \cdot 0.875 + 0.3529 \cdot 0.800 + 0.2353 \cdot 0.800
$$

$$
= \;0.3603 + 0.2824 + 0.1882 \;=\; 0.8309
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Macro F1 ($=0.825$) treats the small "Bird" class equally with the large "Cat" class. Weighted F1 ($=0.831$) slightly favours "Cat" because it has more samples. For KTU board answers, **state which averaging you are using** — this is a common follow-up sub-question.

## 3.4 Quick-Reference Numerical Identity

For any classifier with at least one positive prediction, the F1-Score satisfies:

$$
F_1 \;\le\; \min(P, R) \;\le\; \max(P, R) \;\le\; 1
$$

This is the mathematical expression of the "harmonic mean is bounded above by the minimum" property. It is often quoted in KTU viva voce.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Confusion Matrix to F1 Pipeline

This block diagram shows how the four cells of the confusion matrix feed into Precision, Recall, and ultimately F1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Confusion Matrix] --> B[Four Cells]
    B --> B1[TP]
    B --> B2[FP]
    B --> B3[FN]
    B --> B4[TN]
    B1 --> C1[Precision P = TP / TP + FP]
    B2 --> C1
    B1 --> C2[Recall R = TP / TP + FN]
    B3 --> C2
    C1 --> D{F1 Calculation Path}
    C2 --> D
    B1 --> D
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    D --> E1[Path 1: Harmonic Mean of P and R]
    D --> E2[Path 2: Direct Matrix Form 2TP / 2TP + FP + FN]
    E1 --> F[Final F1 Score in 0 to 1]
    E2 --> F
    B4 -.Not used.-> G[TN ignored by F1]
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#9e9e9e,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#f9a825,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram top-to-bottom:** The Confusion Matrix supplies four counts. TN is greyed out because F1 does not depend on it. The two parallel paths converge into the same final F1 value — both are valid and give identical numerical answers (verified in Example 3.2).

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Decision Tree: Which F Variant to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose Evaluation Metric] --> B{Is the Dataset Imbalanced?}
    B -- No --> C[Use Accuracy]
    B -- Yes --> D{Are FP and FN Costs Symmetric?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Balanced Accuracy or F1]
    D -- No --> F{Which Error is More Costly?}
    F -- FN more costly --> G[Use F2 Score]
    F -- FP more costly --> H[Use F0.5 Score]
    F -- Equal costs --> I[Use F1 Score]
    E --> J{Do you need a single number?}
    J -- Yes --> I
    J -- No --> K[Report P, R, F1 separately]
    style A fill:#1565c0,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** A common 7-mark sub-part asks *"When would you prefer F2 over F1?"* Use this diagram as the basis for your answer. The expected keywords are: *medical diagnosis*, *cancer detection*, *rare disease screening* — anywhere a missed positive (FN) is worse than a false alarm (FP).

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — F1 Behavior Under Extreme Conditions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Perfect Model: P=1, R=1] --> A2[F1 = 1.0]
    A3[High Precision, Low Recall: P=1.0, R=0.1] --> A4[F1 = 0.18]
    A5[Low Precision, High Recall: P=0.1, R=1.0] --> A5b[F1 = 0.18]
    A5b --> A6
    A4 --> A6[Asymmetric scores punished equally]
    A6 --> A7[F1 always lower than min of P and R]
    style A2 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style A7 fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram illustrates the *symmetry* of F1 in P and R: swapping their values leaves F1 unchanged, but in both asymmetric cases F1 is driven very close to the smaller value. This is the visual proof of why F1 is the "balanced judge" referenced in the Section 1 analogy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **The most common mistake** is computing F1 as the arithmetic mean of P and R. This is **wrong**. Always use the harmonic mean form $F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$ or the matrix form $F_1 = \frac{2TP}{2TP + FP + FN}$.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** F1 is *dimensionless* (a ratio). Do not write units like "%" or "score points" — write it as a decimal between 0 and 1.
> 3. **Multi-class averaging:** Always specify whether you are computing *Macro*, *Micro*, or *Weighted* F1. A board answer that simply says "F1 = 0.83" without stating the averaging type will lose 2 of 7 marks.
> 4. **Undefined F1:** If $TP = 0$, F1 is **0 by convention** (not undefined). State this explicitly in viva.
> 5. **Confusion with F1-Score vs Accuracy:** If the question gives a confusion matrix and asks for "model performance", default to F1 if any class has < 30% representation.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the F1-Score. Why is the harmonic mean used instead of the arithmetic mean to combine Precision and Recall?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, defined as:

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

The **harmonic mean** is used because it is *dominated by the smaller value* of the two arguments. If Precision $= 1.0$ and Recall $= 0.1$, the arithmetic mean would yield $0.55$ (a misleadingly high score), whereas the harmonic mean yields $F_1 \approx 0.18$ (correctly indicating a poor model). This property ensures F1 reflects the **weakest link** in the Precision-Recall trade-off, which is the desired behaviour when both metrics are important. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Macro-F1 and Micro-F1 in multi-class classification.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Macro-F1 | Micro-F1 |
|:-------|:---------|:---------|
| Computation | Average of per-class F1 scores. | Aggregate TP, FP, FN across all classes, then apply binary F1 formula. |
| Class weighting | Treats all classes **equally**. | Weights classes **by frequency** (larger classes dominate). |
| Equivalence | Independent of class distribution. | **Mathematically equivalent to Accuracy** in single-label multi-class. |
| Use case | When minority classes are important (e.g., rare disease). | When overall performance matters and classes are balanced. |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two fully worked alternative questions are provided below.

---

### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A binary classifier was tested on **500 samples**. The confusion matrix is:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|:--|:--:|:--:|
| **Actual Positive** | TP = 80 | FN = 20 |
| **Actual Negative** | FP = 40 | TN = 360 |

Compute the **Precision**, **Recall**, **Accuracy**, and **F1-Score**. Comment on whether Accuracy or F1 is the more reliable metric here. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **$F_\beta$ score** family. For each value of $\beta \in \{0.5, 1, 2\}$, describe a real-world application where that specific $\beta$ is preferred, and justify the choice mathematically. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for B-A (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify counts:** $TP = 80$, $FP = 40$, $FN = 20$, $TN = 360$.

**Step 2 — Precision:**

$$
P \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \;=\; \frac{80}{80 + 40} \;=\; \frac{80}{120} \;=\; 0.667
$$

**Step 3 — Recall:**

$$
R \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \;=\; \frac{80}{80 + 20} \;=\; \frac{80}{100} \;=\; 0.800
$$

**Step 4 — Accuracy:**

$$
\text{Acc} \;=\; \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + FN + TN} \;=\; \frac{80 + 360}{500} \;=\; \frac{440}{500} \;=\; 0.880
$$

**Step 5 — F1-Score (matrix form):**

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot 80}{2 \cdot 80 + 40 + 20} \;=\; \frac{160}{220} \;=\; 0.727
$$

**Step 6 — Cross-check (harmonic mean form):**

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot 0.667 \cdot 0.800}{0.667 + 0.800} \;=\; \frac{1.0667}{1.467} \;\approx\; 0.727 \;\checkmark
$$

**Comment (1 mark inside the 7):** Although Accuracy is 88% (a high value), it is **misleading** because the dataset is imbalanced (500 samples with 100 positives vs 400 negatives). The TN count of 360 inflates the accuracy. The **F1 = 0.727** provides a more honest assessment by ignoring TN and reflecting both Precision and Recall of the *minority positive class*. Therefore, **F1 is the more reliable metric** in this scenario. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for B-A (b):**

The **$F_\beta$ score** generalises F1 by introducing a parameter $\beta$ that controls the relative weight of Recall over Precision:

$$
F_\beta \;=\; (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 P + R}
$$

The parameter $\beta$ has the following interpretation: *"Recall is considered $\beta$ times as important as Precision."*

**Case 1: $\beta = 0.5$ (Precision-weighted, $F_{0.5}$):**

$$
F_{0.5} \;=\; 1.25 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{0.25 P + R}
$$

**Real-world application: Email Spam Filtering.** False positives (legitimate emails marked as spam) are highly costly — users miss important emails. False negatives (spam in inbox) are merely an annoyance. Hence, **Precision must dominate**, and $F_{0.5}$ is preferred. **(2 marks)**

**Case 2: $\beta = 1$ (Balanced, $F_1$):**

$$
F_1 \;=\; 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

**Real-world application: General-purpose Information Retrieval** (e.g., a search engine). Both *returning irrelevant results* (low Precision) and *missing relevant results* (low Recall) are equally bad. $F_1$ treats them symmetrically. **(2 marks)**

**Case 3: $\beta = 2$ (Recall-weighted, $F_2$):**

$$
F_2 \;=\; 5 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{4P + R}
$$

**Real-world application: Medical Diagnosis of Cancer / Rare Disease.** False negatives (missing a sick patient) can be fatal; the patient loses the window for treatment. False positives (unnecessary follow-up tests) are less harmful. Hence, **Recall must dominate**, and $F_2$ is preferred. **(2 marks)**

**Mathematical Justification Summary (1 mark):** As $\beta$ increases, the denominator term $\beta^2 P$ grows, reducing the influence of Precision in the ratio. Hence high $\beta$ rewards models that achieve high Recall, even at moderate Precision cost. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** Derive the F1-Score formula **starting from the harmonic mean definition** and show that it reduces to $\dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-class classifier (Classes A, B, C) gives the following per-class counts on 200 test samples:

| Class | TP | FP | FN |
|:--|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| A | 60 | 10 | 10 |
| B | 40 | 15 | 10 |
| C | 30 | 5  | 20 |

Compute the **per-class F1 scores**, then compute both the **Macro-F1** and the **Weighted-F1** averages. State which average is more appropriate if Class C represents a critical minority class. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for B-B (a):**

**Step 1 — Start with the harmonic mean definition** for two positive reals $x$ and $y$:

$$
H(x,y) \;=\; \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{x}+\dfrac{1}{y}\right)}
$$

**Step 2 — Set $x = P$ and $y = R$** to obtain the F1 form:

$$
F_1 \;=\; H(P,R) \;=\; \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{P}+\dfrac{1}{R}\right)} \;=\; \frac{2PR}{P+R}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the confusion-matrix definitions** $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ and $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$:

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} + \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}}
$$

**Step 4 — Common denominator for the denominator fraction** $(TP+FP)(TP+FN)$:

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{2 TP^2}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}}{\dfrac{TP(TP+FN) + TP(TP+FP)}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the common denominator** $(TP+FP)(TP+FN)$:

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 TP^2}{TP(TP+FN) + TP(TP+FP)} \;=\; \frac{2 TP^2}{TP[(TP+FN)+(TP+FP)]}
$$

**Step 6 — Factor out TP from the denominator:**

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 TP^2}{TP \cdot [2TP + FP + FN]} \;=\; \frac{2 TP}{2 TP + FP + FN}
$$

This is the desired form. The derivation is complete. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for B-B (b):**

**Step 1 — F1 for Class A:**

$$
F_1^A \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot 60}{2 \cdot 60 + 10 + 10} \;=\; \frac{120}{140} \;=\; 0.857
$$

**Step 2 — F1 for Class B:**

$$
F_1^B \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot 40}{2 \cdot 40 + 15 + 10} \;=\; \frac{80}{105} \;\approx\; 0.762
$$

**Step 3 — F1 for Class C:**

$$
F_1^C \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot 30}{2 \cdot 30 + 5 + 20} \;=\; \frac{60}{85} \;\approx\; 0.706
$$

**Step 4 — Macro-F1 (equal weight per class):**

$$
F_1^{\text{macro}} \;=\; \frac{0.857 + 0.762 + 0.706}{3} \;=\; \frac{2.325}{3} \;\approx\; 0.775
$$

**Step 5 — Weighted-F1 (weight = $\text{support}_i / N$):**

Total support $N = 60 + 40 + 30 = 130$.

$$
F_1^{\text{weighted}} \;=\; \frac{60}{130}(0.857) + \frac{40}{130}(0.762) + \frac{30}{130}(0.706)
$$

$$
= \;0.4615 \cdot 0.857 + 0.3077 \cdot 0.762 + 0.2308 \cdot 0.706
$$

$$
= \;0.3955 + 0.2345 + 0.1629 \;=\; 0.7929
$$

**Step 6 — Choice of metric:** If Class C is a *critical minority class* (e.g., a rare but serious medical condition), the modeler wants to ensure Class C is treated as importantly as the larger classes. **Macro-F1 is the more appropriate metric** because it gives equal weight to all three F1 scores, preventing Class C from being overshadowed by the larger Class A. Weighted-F1 would dilute Class C's signal because it has the smallest support (30/130 ≈ 23%). **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **F1-Score — One-Page Revision Sheet**

- **Definition:** F1 is the **harmonic mean of Precision and Recall**, ranging in $[0, 1]$.
- **Canonical formula:** $\;\;F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}\;\;$ (most reliable, no division-by-zero risk).
- **Alternative formula:** $\;\;F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}\;\;$ (valid only when $P + R > 0$).
- **Why harmonic, not arithmetic?** Harmonic mean is *bounded above* by the minimum of the two arguments — it punishes weakness in either P or R.
- **TN is irrelevant:** F1 does not use the True Negative count. This is *by design* — F1 is robust to class imbalance in the negative class.
- **Boundary cases:** $F_1 = 1$ iff $P = R = 1$. $F_1 = 0$ iff $TP = 0$.
- **General family:** $F_\beta = (1+\beta^2) \dfrac{PR}{\beta^2 P + R}$. Increase $\beta$ → weight Recall more.
- **Multi-class:**
  - *Macro F1* — average of per-class F1; equal class importance.
  - *Micro F1* — pool all TP/FP/FN; equivalent to Accuracy in single-label multi-class.
  - *Weighted F1* — weighted by class support (frequency).
- **When to use F1 vs Accuracy:** Use F1 when the dataset is **imbalanced** or when **FP/FN costs differ**.
- **When to use F2 vs F1 vs F0.5:** F2 for *medical* (Recall critical), F1 for *balanced*, F0.5 for *spam/recommendations* (Precision critical).
- **Board-exam numerical workflow:** Read confusion matrix → compute $P$ and $R$ → plug into F1 formula → cross-check using the other form → state conclusion in plain English.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Never write F1 as the arithmetic mean $\frac{P+R}{2}$. This is the single most common KTU board-exam error.
- **Viva one-liner:** *"F1 is high only when both Precision and Recall are high; a model cannot game it by inflating just one of them."*

---

*End of Module 4 — F1-Score Notes | PCAMT302 Machine Learning I | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Receiver Operating Characteristics Curve (ROC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT302 - MACHINE LEARNING I
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Receiver Operating Characteristics Curve (ROC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve** is a two-dimensional graphical evaluation tool used in **supervised classification** to assess the diagnostic ability of a binary classifier as its **discrimination threshold** is varied. It plots the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** on the y-axis against the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** on the x-axis, thereby characterising the trade-off between *sensitivity* and *1 − specificity* across all possible decision thresholds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim)**: A ROC curve is the locus of points `(FPR(τ), TPR(τ))` traced as the classification threshold `τ` is swept from `+∞` (all negatives) to `−∞` (all positives). The **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** summarises the entire curve into a single scalar invariant.

### 1.2 Historical & Conceptual Origin

The ROC framework originated during **World War II** in the context of **RADAR signal detection**, where "Receiver Operators" (radio engineers) had to distinguish allied aircraft signals (true positives) from background noise (false positives). The methodology was formalised into statistics by psychologists and is now a cornerstone of **medical diagnostics, machine learning, and information retrieval**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Metal-Detector on a Beach

Imagine you are a **beach security guard** using a metal detector to find dangerous items (knives, weapons) among thousands of sunbathers. The detector has a **sensitivity dial** (threshold `τ`):

- **Turn sensitivity UP (low `τ`)**: The detector beeps for *everything* — you catch every knife (high TPR) but flag innocent people with watches and belt buckles (high FPR). Many false alarms.
- **Turn sensitivity DOWN (high `τ`)**: The detector only beeps for huge metal masses — few false alarms (low FPR) but you miss small knives (low TPR). Many missed threats.
- **ROC Curve**: As you rotate the dial through **every possible setting**, you trace a curve. The **shape** of that curve tells you *how good your metal detector fundamentally is*, independent of the dial setting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway**: A perfect detector hugs the **top-left corner** (catches every threat, no false alarms). A random detector follows the **diagonal line** from `(0,0)` to `(1,1)`. The further *above* the diagonal, the better the classifier.

### 1.4 The Confusion Matrix — Foundation of ROC

Every point on the ROC curve is derived from a 2×2 **Confusion Matrix** computed at a specific threshold `τ`:

| Actual ↓ / Predicted → | **Predicted Positive (1)** | **Predicted Negative (0)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive (1)** | **TP** (True Positive) | **FN** (False Negative) — *Type II Error* |
| **Actual Negative (0)** | **FP** (False Positive) — *Type I Error* | **TN** (True Negative) |

Where:
- **TP** = correctly classified positives
- **FP** = incorrectly flagged negatives (false alarm)
- **TN** = correctly classified negatives
- **FN** = missed positives (missed detection)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU**: Always explicitly state the confusion matrix before computing any TPR/FPR in your answer. Examiners award partial marks for this foundational step.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How the ROC Curve is Constructed

1. **Train a probabilistic classifier** (e.g., Logistic Regression, Naive Bayes, Neural Network with sigmoid output) that outputs a *score* `s(x) ∈ [0, 1]` rather than a hard label.
2. **Sort all N test instances in descending order of their predicted score** `s(x)`.
3. **Initialise** at threshold `τ = +∞`: every sample is classified as negative → point `(0, 0)`.
4. **Sweep `τ` downward** through every unique score. At each step, the instance whose score equals `τ` flips from negative to positive.
5. **Recompute TPR and FPR** after each flip. Plot the new point `(FPR, TPR)`.
6. **Final point** at `τ = −∞`: every sample is positive → point `(1, 1)`.
7. **Connect** consecutive points (usually with straight line segments) to form the ROC curve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a staircase shape?** Because each threshold change alters the classification of *only one* (or a few tied) sample(s). Each new point moves either rightward (a new FP), upward (a new TP), or both (a tied score).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Sorting step** is crucial — it ensures the threshold sweep is monotonic, producing an *efficient* ROC curve without redundant points.
- **TPR vs FPR trade-off**: Moving the threshold to be *more lenient* (lower `τ`) generally increases both, but the *relative* rate of increase depends on classifier quality.
- **Independence from class prior**: Unlike accuracy, ROC metrics are **invariant to class imbalance**, which is why ROC is preferred for imbalanced datasets in KTU-style problems.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-essential**. Memorise the relationships, not just the symbols.

| # | Metric | Formula | Range | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **True Positive Rate (TPR)** | $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | `[0, 1]` | Sensitivity / Recall — fraction of actual positives correctly caught |
| 2 | **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | `[0, 1]` | Fall-out — fraction of actual negatives incorrectly flagged |
| 3 | **True Negative Rate (TNR)** | $\text{TNR} = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | `[0, 1]` | Specificity — `1 − FPR` |
| 4 | **False Negative Rate (FNR)** | $\text{FNR} = \dfrac{FN}{TP+FN}$ | `[0, 1]` | Miss rate — `1 − TPR` |
| 5 | **Precision (PPV)** | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | `[0, 1]` | Positive Predictive Value — trust in a positive alert |
| 6 | **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+FP+TN+FN}$ | `[0, 1]` | Overall correctness — *misleading on imbalanced data* |
| 7 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | `[0, 1]` | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall |
| 8 | **AUC (Trapezoidal)** | $\text{AUC} = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \dfrac{(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i)(TPR_{i+1} + TPR_i)}{2}$ | `[0, 1]` | Area Under ROC Curve — probability that a random positive ranks above a random negative |
| 9 | **Gini Coefficient** | $G = 2 \cdot \text{AUC} - 1$ | `[-1, 1]` | Used in credit scoring; `G = 0` is random, `G = 1` is perfect |
| 10 | **Youden's J Statistic** | $J = \text{TPR} - \text{FPR}$ | `[0, 1]` | Optimal threshold maximises `J` |

### 2.4 The Probabilistic Interpretation of AUC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a KTU favourite for 7-mark sub-parts.**

$$\text{AUC} = P\big(s(x_{\text{pos}}) > s(x_{\text{neg}})\big)$$

That is, the AUC equals the probability that the classifier assigns a **higher score to a randomly chosen positive instance** than to a randomly chosen negative instance. This is the **Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney U statistic** (Wilcoxon, 1945; Hanley & McNeil, 1982).

- **AUC = 1.0** → perfect separation
- **AUC = 0.5** → no separation (random guessing)
- **AUC < 0.5** → worse than random; flip predictions
- **AUC = 0** → perfect anti-prediction (100% wrong)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of ROC |
|---|---|
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Comparing biopsy vs blood-test for cancer detection |
| **Fraud Detection** | Credit-card transaction classifiers |
| **Anomaly Detection** | Network intrusion detection systems (NIDS) |
| **Information Retrieval** | Ranking search-engine results |
| **Bioinformatics** | Microarray gene-expression classification |
| **Weather Forecasting** | Predicting extreme events (cyclones, floods) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Constructing an ROC Curve from Scratch

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A binary classifier produces the following predicted scores for 6 test instances along with their true labels. Construct the ROC curve and compute the AUC using the trapezoidal rule.

| Instance | True Label `y` | Predicted Score `s(x)` |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 1 (Positive) | 0.92 |
| P2 | 0 (Negative) | 0.85 |
| P3 | 1 (Positive) | 0.78 |
| P4 | 0 (Negative) | 0.61 |
| P5 | 1 (Positive) | 0.45 |
| P6 | 0 (Negative) | 0.20 |

**Totals**: `P = TP + FN = 3` positives; `N = FP + TN = 3` negatives.

---

#### Step 1 — Sort by Score (Descending)

| Rank | Instance | Label `y` | Score `s` |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1 | 1 | 0.92 |
| 2 | P2 | 0 | 0.85 |
| 3 | P3 | 1 | 0.78 |
| 4 | P4 | 0 | 0.61 |
| 5 | P5 | 1 | 0.45 |
| 6 | P6 | 0 | 0.20 |

**Logic**: Sorting ensures that as we lower the threshold, the *latest* sample to flip is always the one with the highest remaining score. This is the foundation of the efficient ROC construction algorithm.

---

#### Step 2 — Initialise Counters

$$\text{TP}_{\text{count}} = 0, \quad \text{FP}_{\text{count}} = 0$$

**ROC origin** at threshold `τ > 0.92`: all instances classified as negative.

$$\text{Point}_0 = (0, 0)$$

---

#### Step 3 — Sweep Threshold, One Sample at a Time

**Move 1** — Lower `τ` past 0.92 (instance P1, label = 1):
A positive is correctly caught → `TP` increments by 1.

$$\text{TPR}_1 = \frac{TP}{P} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

$$\text{FPR}_1 = \frac{FP}{N} = \frac{0}{3} = 0.000$$

$$\text{Point}_1 = (0.000,\ 0.333)$$

**Move 2** — Lower `τ` past 0.85 (instance P2, label = 0):
A negative is incorrectly flagged → `FP` increments by 1.

$$\text{TPR}_2 = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333, \quad \text{FPR}_2 = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

$$\text{Point}_2 = (0.333,\ 0.333)$$

**Move 3** — Lower `τ` past 0.78 (instance P3, label = 1):
A positive is caught → `TP` increments.

$$\text{TPR}_3 = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667, \quad \text{FPR}_3 = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

$$\text{Point}_3 = (0.333,\ 0.667)$$

**Move 4** — Lower `τ` past 0.61 (instance P4, label = 0):
A negative is incorrectly flagged → `FP` increments.

$$\text{TPR}_4 = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667, \quad \text{FPR}_4 = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$$

$$\text{Point}_4 = (0.667,\ 0.667)$$

**Move 5** — Lower `τ` past 0.45 (instance P5, label = 1):
A positive is caught → `TP` increments.

$$\text{TPR}_5 = \frac{3}{3} = 1.000, \quad \text{FPR}_5 = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$$

$$\text{Point}_5 = (0.667,\ 1.000)$$

**Move 6** — Lower `τ` past 0.20 (instance P6, label = 0):
A negative is incorrectly flagged → `FP` increments.

$$\text{TPR}_6 = \frac{3}{3} = 1.000, \quad \text{FPR}_6 = \frac{3}{3} = 1.000$$

$$\text{Point}_6 = (1.000,\ 1.000)$$

---

#### Step 4 — Tabulated ROC Points

| Point | FPR (x) | TPR (y) | Type of Move |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.000 | 0.000 | Origin |
| 1 | 0.000 | 0.333 | ↑ Vertical (TP added) |
| 2 | 0.333 | 0.333 | → Horizontal (FP added) |
| 3 | 0.333 | 0.667 | ↑ Vertical (TP added) |
| 4 | 0.667 | 0.667 | → Horizontal (FP added) |
| 5 | 0.667 | 1.000 | ↑ Vertical (TP added) |
| 6 | 1.000 | 1.000 | → Horizontal (FP added) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Staircase pattern**: Alternating vertical (TP) and horizontal (FP) moves. This is the canonical shape of a discrete ROC curve.

---

#### Step 5 — Compute AUC via Trapezoidal Rule

For two consecutive points $(x_i, y_i)$ and $(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1})$, the trapezoidal area is:

$$A_i = \frac{(x_{i+1} - x_i)(y_{i+1} + y_i)}{2}$$

**Sum over all consecutive pairs** $(i = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \frac{(0.000 - 0.000)(0.333 + 0.000)}{2} = 0.0000 \\[4pt]
A_1 &= \frac{(0.333 - 0.000)(0.333 + 0.333)}{2} = 0.0555 \\[4pt]
A_2 &= \frac{(0.333 - 0.333)(0.667 + 0.333)}{2} = 0.0000 \\[4pt]
A_3 &= \frac{(0.667 - 0.333)(0.667 + 0.667)}{2} = 0.2222 \\[4pt]
A_4 &= \frac{(0.667 - 0.667)(1.000 + 0.667)}{2} = 0.0000 \\[4pt]
A_5 &= \frac{(1.000 - 0.667)(1.000 + 1.000)}{2} = 0.3333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total AUC**:

$$\text{AUC} = \sum_{i=0}^{5} A_i = 0.0000 + 0.0555 + 0.0000 + 0.2222 + 0.0000 + 0.3333 = 0.6111$$

**Interpretation**: The classifier has a **~61.1% probability** of ranking a randomly chosen positive higher than a randomly chosen negative. This is *moderately better* than random (0.5) but far from perfect (1.0).

---

#### Step 6 — Compute Youden's J for Optimal Threshold

For each ROC point, compute $J = \text{TPR} - \text{FPR}$:

| Point | TPR | FPR | J = TPR − FPR |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.333 | 0.000 | **0.333** ← max so far |
| 2 | 0.333 | 0.333 | 0.000 |
| 3 | 0.667 | 0.333 | **0.333** ← tied max |
| 4 | 0.667 | 0.667 | 0.000 |
| 5 | 1.000 | 0.667 | 0.333 |

The **optimal threshold** corresponds to the points where `J` is maximised. Here, points 1, 3, and 5 all yield `J = 0.333`. The associated thresholds are `τ ∈ (0.85, 0.78]` (between samples P2 and P3) or `τ ∈ (0.61, 0.45]` (between P4 and P5).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical KTU tip**: When multiple points tie for maximum `J`, choose the one with **higher TPR** (catches more positives) — i.e., the *rightmost* of the tied points, which is point 5 at threshold `τ = 0.45`.

### 3.2 Alternative AUC Computation — Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney Method

When the dataset is large and computing all trapezoids is tedious, use the **rank-based formula**:

$$\text{AUC} = \frac{1}{P \cdot N} \sum_{i : y_i = 1} \Big( R_i - \frac{r_i(r_i + 1)}{2} \Big)$$

where `R_i` is the rank of positive sample `i` and `r_i` is the count of positives tied at that rank. This is computationally $O(N \log N)$ and is what library implementations use under the hood.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ROC Curve Construction — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram describes the *end-to-end pipeline* for generating and evaluating a ROC curve in a machine learning workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset] --> B[Train-Test Split]
    B --> C[Train Classifier M]
    C --> D[Predict Probabilities s_x]
    D --> E[Sort Descending by Score]
    E --> F[Init TP=0, FP=0]
    F --> G{For each unique score tau}
    G --> H[Classify all s_x >= tau as Positive]
    H --> I[Build Confusion Matrix]
    I --> J[Compute TPR = TP per P and FPR = FP per N]
    J --> K[Plot FPR, TPR on ROC axes]
    K --> L{More thresholds?}
    L -- Yes --> G
    L -- No --> M[Connect all points]
    M --> N[Compute AUC via Trapezoidal Rule]
    N --> O[Compare Classifiers]
    O --> P[Final Model Selection]
```

**Block Interpretation:**

- `A → B`: Stratified split preserving class proportions (KTU: mention stratification for imbalanced data).
- `B → C`: Fit a probabilistic model (Logistic Regression, SVM with Platt scaling, Random Forest with `predict_proba`).
- `C → D`: Obtain continuous scores, *not* hard labels.
- `D → E`: Sort step is the heart of efficient ROC construction.
- `E → G`: Sweep the threshold monotonically — only one sample's classification changes per step.
- `I → J`: Recompute TPR and FPR after each flip.
- `K → M`: Connect points to form the staircase-like curve.
- `N`: AUC summarises the curve into a single comparable scalar.
- `O → P`: The classifier with higher AUC (and practical operating point) is selected.

### 4.2 Multi-Classifier ROC Comparison — Decision Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph C1[Classifier A - Logistic Regression]
        A1[ROC Curve A] --> A2[AUC = 0.85]
    end
    subgraph C2[Classifier B - Random Forest]
        B1[ROC Curve B] --> B2[AUC = 0.91]
    end
    subgraph C3[Classifier C - SVM RBF]
        C1node[ROC Curve C] --> C2node[AUC = 0.88]
    end
    subgraph Diagonal[Random Baseline]
        D1[Diagonal Line y = x] --> D2[AUC = 0.50]
    end
    A2 --> E{Compare AUC values}
    B2 --> E
    C2node --> E
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Select Model B - Highest AUC]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- Three classifiers (A, B, C) and a random baseline are evaluated.
- Each model contributes a single AUC scalar.
- The comparison node (`E`) picks the model with the **largest AUC** (here, B with 0.91).
- **KTU Note**: If two AUCs are very close (within 0.02), consider **statistical significance** via DeLong's test or bootstrapped confidence intervals.

### 4.3 Threshold Selection — Operating Point Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[ROC Curve Computed] --> Q{Application Type?}
    Q -- Medical Diagnosis --> R[Maximise TPR - minimise FN]
    R --> S[Pick point with highest TPR acceptable FPR]
    Q -- Spam Filter --> T[Maximise Precision - minimise FP]
    T --> U[Pick point with high TPR but very low FPR]
    Q -- General Purpose --> V[Maximise Youden J = TPR - FPR]
    V --> W[Optimal threshold identified]
    S --> X[Deploy Model]
    U --> X
    W --> X
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb**:
> - **Cancer screening** (cost of FN is death) → pick threshold favouring high TPR.
> - **Spam filter** (cost of FP is user annoyance) → pick threshold favouring low FPR.
> - **Default case** → maximise Youden's J.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve. What does the Area Under the Curve (AUC) signify about a classifier's performance?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** is a graphical plot that illustrates the diagnostic ability of a binary classifier as its discrimination threshold is varied. It is drawn in a 2-D plane with the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** on the x-axis and the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** on the y-axis. Each point on the curve corresponds to a (FPR, TPR) pair obtained at a specific threshold value.

The **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** is a scalar metric ranging from 0 to 1 that quantifies the overall performance of the classifier across *all* possible thresholds. It can be interpreted probabilistically as the likelihood that the classifier will rank a randomly chosen positive instance higher than a randomly chosen negative instance. An AUC of **1.0** indicates a perfect classifier, **0.5** indicates a random classifier, and values below **0.5** indicate performance worse than random.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Key**: 1 mark for ROC definition, 1 mark for axes, 1 mark for AUC interpretation. Drawing a small diagram earns bonus partial credit.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between the ROC curve and the Precision-Recall (PR) curve. In what scenario is the PR curve preferred over the ROC curve?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ROC Curve | PR Curve |
|---|---|---|
| **Axes** | FPR (x) vs TPR (y) | Recall (x) vs Precision (y) |
| **Sensitivity to class imbalance** | Low — uses TNR denominator | High — uses only positive predictions |
| **Best for** | Roughly balanced datasets | **Heavily imbalanced** datasets |
| **Baseline** | Diagonal `y = x` (random) | Horizontal line at `y = P/(P+N)` |

The **PR curve is preferred over the ROC curve when the dataset is severely imbalanced** (e.g., fraud detection where positives are 0.1% of data). In such cases, the large number of true negatives dominates the FPR denominator `(FP + TN)`, making FPR artificially small and ROC overly optimistic. PR curves focus exclusively on the positive class's predictive performance and provide a more honest assessment.

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> **(a)** A binary classifier produces the following predicted scores for 8 test samples along with their true class labels. Plot the ROC curve point-by-point and tabulate `(FPR, TPR)` for every threshold.
> **(7 marks)**

| Sample | True Label | Predicted Score |
|---|---|---|
| S1 | 1 | 0.95 |
| S2 | 0 | 0.90 |
| S3 | 1 | 0.80 |
| S4 | 0 | 0.65 |
| S5 | 1 | 0.55 |
| S6 | 0 | 0.40 |
| S7 | 1 | 0.30 |
| S8 | 0 | 0.10 |

> **(b)** Using the ROC points obtained in part (a), calculate the AUC via the trapezoidal rule. Identify the optimal operating threshold using Youden's J statistic.
> **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Sort by score descending** (1 mark):

| Rank | Sample | Label | Score |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | S1 | 1 | 0.95 |
| 2 | S2 | 0 | 0.90 |
| 3 | S3 | 1 | 0.80 |
| 4 | S4 | 0 | 0.65 |
| 5 | S5 | 1 | 0.55 |
| 6 | S6 | 0 | 0.40 |
| 7 | S7 | 1 | 0.30 |
| 8 | S8 | 0 | 0.10 |

**Totals**: `P = 4` positives (S1, S3, S5, S7); `N = 4` negatives (S2, S4, S6, S8).

**Step 2 — Sweep thresholds and compute TPR, FPR** (5 marks):

For each sample moving downward, increment `TP` if label = 1, or `FP` if label = 0.

| Move | Sample | Label | TP | FP | TPR = TP/4 | FPR = FP/4 | (FPR, TPR) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | — | 0 | 0 | 0.000 | 0.000 | (0.000, 0.000) |
| 1 | S1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0.250 | 0.000 | (0.000, 0.250) |
| 2 | S2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0.250 | 0.250 | (0.250, 0.250) |
| 3 | S3 | 1 | 2 | 1 | 0.500 | 0.250 | (0.250, 0.500) |
| 4 | S4 | 0 | 2 | 2 | 0.500 | 0.500 | (0.500, 0.500) |
| 5 | S5 | 1 | 3 | 2 | 0.750 | 0.500 | (0.500, 0.750) |
| 6 | S6 | 0 | 3 | 3 | 0.750 | 0.750 | (0.750, 0.750) |
| 7 | S7 | 1 | 4 | 3 | 1.000 | 0.750 | (0.750, 1.000) |
| 8 | S8 | 0 | 4 | 4 | 1.000 | 1.000 | (1.000, 1.000) |

**Step 3 — Plot the staircase ROC curve** (1 mark): Connect all 9 points sequentially on a graph with FPR on x-axis and TPR on y-axis, both ranging from 0 to 1.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Apply the trapezoidal formula** (4 marks):

$$A_i = \frac{(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i) \cdot (TPR_{i+1} + TPR_i)}{2}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \frac{(0.000 - 0.000)(0.250 + 0.000)}{2} = 0.0000 \\[4pt]
A_1 &= \frac{(0.250 - 0.000)(0.250 + 0.250)}{2} = 0.0313 \\[4pt]
A_2 &= \frac{(0.250 - 0.250)(0.500 + 0.250)}{2} = 0.0000 \\[4pt]
A_3 &= \frac{(0.500 - 0.250)(0.500 + 0.500)}{2} = 0.1250 \\[4pt]
A_4 &= \frac{(0.500 - 0.500)(0.750 + 0.500)}{2} = 0.0000 \\[4pt]
A_5 &= \frac{(0.750 - 0.500)(0.750 + 0.750)}{2} = 0.1875 \\[4pt]
A_6 &= \frac{(0.750 - 0.750)(1.000 + 0.750)}{2} = 0.0000 \\[4pt]
A_7 &= \frac{(1.000 - 0.750)(1.000 + 1.000)}{2} = 0.2500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum to obtain AUC** (1 mark):

$$\text{AUC} = \sum_{i=0}^{7} A_i = 0.0000 + 0.0313 + 0.0000 + 0.1250 + 0.0000 + 0.1875 + 0.0000 + 0.2500$$

$$\boxed{\text{AUC} = 0.5938}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Youden's J at every point** (1.5 marks):

| Point | TPR | FPR | J = TPR − FPR |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.250 | 0.000 | **0.250** |
| 2 | 0.250 | 0.250 | 0.000 |
| 3 | 0.500 | 0.250 | **0.250** |
| 4 | 0.500 | 0.500 | 0.000 |
| 5 | 0.750 | 0.500 | **0.250** |
| 6 | 0.750 | 0.750 | 0.000 |
| 7 | 1.000 | 0.750 | **0.250** |
| 8 | 1.000 | 1.000 | 0.000 |

**Step 4 — Identify optimal threshold** (0.5 marks):

The maximum `J = 0.250` occurs at multiple points (1, 3, 5, 7). Selecting the **rightmost** of the ties (highest TPR = 1.0) → **Point 7** at threshold `τ ∈ (0.30, 0.10]`, i.e., just after sample S7 flips to positive.

**Final Optimal Threshold**: `τ ≈ 0.30`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** Explain the confusion matrix and derive the expressions for TPR, FPR, Precision, and F1-score in terms of TP, FP, TN, and FN. Discuss the relationship between TPR and FPR when the decision threshold of a classifier is varied.
> **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Three classifiers M1, M2, M3 are evaluated on a binary classification task. Their ROC curves yield AUC values of 0.72, 0.85, and 0.91 respectively. Two of them (M2 and M3) operate at the following points:
> - M2: TPR = 0.80, FPR = 0.20
> - M3: TPR = 0.85, FPR = 0.30
>
> **(i)** Which model would you choose for a **medical diagnosis** application (where missing a positive case is critical)? Justify with a metric-based argument.
> **(ii)** Which model would you choose for a **spam filter** (where false alarms annoy users)? Justify.
> **(iii)** What is the Gini coefficient for M3?
> **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Define the confusion matrix** (1.5 marks):

The confusion matrix is a 2×2 contingency table comparing predicted vs actual labels:

| | Predicted + | Predicted − |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual +** | TP | FN |
| **Actual −** | FP | TN |

**Step 2 — Derive the four metrics** (3 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TPR} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \text{Sensitivity} = \text{Recall} \\[4pt]
\text{FPR} &= \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = 1 - \text{Specificity} \\[4pt]
\text{Precision} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \text{Positive Predictive Value} \\[4pt]
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Discuss threshold variation** (2.5 marks):

As the decision threshold `τ` of a probabilistic classifier is decreased (i.e., the classifier becomes more lenient in declaring positives):
- More samples are classified as positive → both `TP` and `FP` typically increase.
- **TPR** increases monotonically (or stays flat) because additional positives can only be added, never removed, from the predicted-positive set.
- **FPR** also increases monotonically for the same reason applied to actual negatives.
- The ROC curve is therefore a *monotonically non-decreasing* function from `(0, 0)` to `(1, 1)`.
- At `τ → +∞`: TPR = 0, FPR = 0 (no positives predicted).
- At `τ → −∞`: TPR = 1, FPR = 1 (everything predicted positive).

This monotonicity is what guarantees the AUC integral is well-defined and bounded in `[0, 1]`.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

**Part (i) — Medical Diagnosis (cost of FN is critical)** (2.5 marks):

Compute **recall (TPR)** for both:
